# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Vibration Analysis

The following block diagram shows the **flow of computation** for finding natural frequencies and mode shapes of an MDOF system:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical System Geometry and Material Properties] --> B[Discretization: Lumping Mass and Stiffness]
    B --> C[Formulate Mass Matrix M and Stiffness Matrix K]
    C --> D[Assemble Equation: M times x double dot plus K times x equals zero]
    D --> E[Assume Harmonic Motion: x equals phi times e to the i omega t]
    E --> F[Derive Eigenvalue Problem: K minus omega squared M times phi equals zero]
    F --> G[Compute Determinant Condition: det of K minus omega squared M equals zero]
    G --> H[Solve Characteristic Polynomial for omega squared]
    H --> I[Obtain Natural Frequencies omega 1, omega 2, ..., omega N]
    I --> J[Back Substitute Each omega r into Eigenvalue Problem]
    J --> K[Solve for Mode Shape Ratios phi r,2 over phi r,1, etc.]
    K --> L[Normalize Mode Shapes if Required]
    L --> M[Output: Natural Frequencies and Mode Shapes Vector Set]
    M --> N[Apply Modal Superposition for Forced Response]
    N --> O[Final Displacement Response of All DOF]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: 2-DOF Mass-Spring System (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WALL[Fixed Wall] -->|Spring k1| M1[Mass m1, displacement x1]
    M1 -->|Spring k2| M2[Mass m2, displacement x2]
    M1 -.->|Force: -k1 x1 + k2 x2 - k2 x1| EQ1[Equation of Motion 1]
    M2 -.->|Force: -k2 x2 - k2 x1 + k2 x2| EQ2[Equation of Motion 2]
    EQ1 --> MAT[Matrix Form: M x double dot plus K x equals zero]
    EQ2 --> MAT
    MAT --> EIG[Eigenvalue Problem Yields omega 1 and omega 2]
    EIG --> MODE1[Mode 1: Both Masses Move in Same Direction]
    EIG --> MODE2[Mode 2: Masses Move in Opposite Directions]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Modal Superposition Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Total Displacement x of t] --> SUM[Superposition Sum over all modes]
    PHI1[Mode Shape phi 1] --> SUM
    PHI2[Mode Shape phi 2] --> SUM
    PHIN[Mode Shape phi N] --> SUM
    Q1[Modal Coordinate q1 of t] --> SUM
    Q2[Modal Coordinate q2 of t] --> SUM
    QN[Modal Coordinate qN of t] --> SUM
    M[Mass Matrix M] --> ORTH[Orthogonality Check: phi r transpose M phi s equals zero for r not equal to s]
    K[Stiffness Matrix K] --> ORTH
    ORTH --> DEC[Decouple into N Independent SDOF Equations]
    DEC --> SOLVE[Solve Each SDOF Independently for q r of t]
    SOLVE --> RECOMB[Recombine via Superposition to Get x of t]
```

## 4.4 Table: Physical Interpretation of Mode Shapes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MODE1[Mode 1 - Fundamental Mode]
        A1[Lowest Natural Frequency omega 1]
        A2[All masses move in phase]
        A3[Displacement pattern has no internal node]
    end
    subgraph MODE2[Mode 2 - Higher Mode]
        B1[Higher Natural Frequency omega 2 greater than omega 1]
        B2[At least one stationary point NODE]
        B3[More complex displacement pattern]
    end
    subgraph MODE3[Mode r - General]
        C1[Frequency omega r increases with r]
        C2[Number of internal nodes equals r minus 1]
        C3[Strain energy distribution varies]
    end
    MODE1 --> MODE2
    MODE2 --> MODE3
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: 2-Mark Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define natural frequency and mode shape. Why are they important in vibration analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

A **natural frequency** is the frequency at which a system freely oscillates when displaced from equilibrium and released, in the absence of external forcing. It depends on the system's mass and stiffness: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$.

A **mode shape** is the specific pattern of relative displacements of all mass points when the system vibrates at a particular natural frequency. An N-DOF system has N natural frequencies and N corresponding mode shapes.

**Importance in vibration analysis:**
1. **Resonance avoidance:** Operating machinery at its natural frequency causes catastrophic amplitude amplification.
2. **Design optimization:** Engineers tune structural parameters (geometry, materials) to shift natural frequencies away from excitation frequencies.
3. **Modal testing:** Experimental modal analysis uses measured natural frequencies and mode shapes to validate finite element models.
4. **Failure diagnosis:** Unusual vibration peaks often indicate component loosening or damage that has altered the natural frequency.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: Explain the principle of orthogonality of mode shapes with its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **orthogonality principle** states that for an undamped linear system with distinct natural frequencies, the mode shapes are mutually orthogonal with respect to both the mass and stiffness matrices:

$$\{\phi_r\}^T[M]\{\phi_s\} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \{\phi_r\}^T[K]\{\phi_s\} = 0 \quad \text{for } r \neq s$$

**Proof basis:** It follows from the symmetry of $[M]$ and $[K]$ combined with the eigenvalue equations $[K]\{\phi_r\} = \omega_r^2[M]\{\phi_r\}$ and the distinctness of eigenvalues.

**Significance:**
1. **Decoupling:** Allows the N coupled equations of motion to be reduced to N independent single-DOF equations.
2. **Computational efficiency:** Solving N SDOF problems is far simpler than solving one N-DOF coupled system.
3. **Modal superposition:** Enables expressing the total response as a linear combination of mode shapes.
4. **Foundation of FEA:** Most commercial finite element codes (ANSYS, NASTRAN) rely on modal analysis built on orthogonality.

---

## PART B: Full-Question Solutions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(14 Marks)**

**A two-degree-of-freedom system consists of two masses $m_1 = 2$ kg and $m_2 = 1$ kg connected by three springs as shown (mass $m_1$ on left, connected to wall by spring $k_1 = 1000$ N/m, then spring $k_2 = 500$ N/m to mass $m_2$, then spring $k_3 = 500$ N/m to fixed wall on right).**

**Part (a):** Derive the equations of motion and find the natural frequencies. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b):** Determine the mode shapes and verify orthogonality with respect to the mass matrix. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A — Part (a):**

**Step 1: Free body diagrams and equations of motion**

For mass $m_1$ (displacement $x_1$):
- Spring $k_1$ pulls left: $-k_1 x_1$
- Spring $k_2$ pulls right when stretched: $+k_2(x_2 - x_1)$

$$m_1\ddot{x}_1 + (k_1 + k_2)x_1 - k_2 x_2 = 0$$

$$2\ddot{x}_1 + 1500\,x_1 - 500\,x_2 = 0$$

For mass $m_2$ (displacement $x_2$):
- Spring $k_2$ pulls left: $-k_2(x_2 - x_1)$
- Spring $k_3$ pulls right: $-k_3 x_2$

$$m_2\ddot{x}_2 - k_2 x_1 + (k_2 + k_3)x_2 = 0$$

$$1\cdot\ddot{x}_2 - 500\,x_1 + 1000\,x_2 = 0$$

**Step 2: Matrix form**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} \ddot{x}_1 \\ \ddot{x}_2 \end{Bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix} 1500 & -500 \\ -500 & 1000 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{Bmatrix}
=
\begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3: Eigenvalue problem**

$$([K] - \omega^2[M])\{\phi\} = \{0\}$$

$$
\begin{vmatrix} 1500 - 2\omega^2 & -500 \\ -500 & 1000 - \omega^2 \end{vmatrix} = 0
$$

**Step 4: Determinant expansion**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(1500 - 2\omega^2)(1000 - \omega^2) - (-500)(-500) = 0 \\
&1{,}500{,}000 - 1500\omega^2 - 2000\omega^2 + 2\omega^4 - 250{,}000 = 0 \\
&2\omega^4 - 3500\omega^2 + 1{,}250{,}000 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Solve the quadratic in $\omega^2$** using $\omega^2 = \dfrac{3500 \pm \sqrt{3500^2 - 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 1{,}250{,}000}}{4}$:

$$\omega^2 = \frac{3500 \pm \sqrt{12{,}250{,}000 - 10{,}000{,}000}}{4} = \frac{3500 \pm \sqrt{2{,}250{,}000}}{4}$$

$$\omega^2 = \frac{3500 \pm 1500}{4}$$

$$\omega_1^2 = \frac{2000}{4} = 500 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_1 = 22.36 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_2^2 = \frac{5000}{4} = 1250 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_2 = 35.36 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\boxed{f_1 = 3.558 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_2 = 5.626 \text{ Hz}}$$

---

### **Solution to Question A — Part (b):**

**Step 1: Find mode shape for $\omega_1^2 = 500$**

From row 1: $(1500 - 2 \cdot 500)\phi_{1,1} - 500\phi_{1,2} = 0 \Rightarrow 500\phi_{1,1} = 500\phi_{1,2}$

$$\frac{\phi_{1,2}}{\phi_{1,1}} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \{\phi_1\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Find mode shape for $\omega_2^2 = 1250$**

From row 1: $(1500 - 2 \cdot 1250)\phi_{2,1} - 500\phi_{2,2} = 0 \Rightarrow -1000\phi_{2,1} = 500\phi_{2,2}$

$$\frac{\phi_{2,2}}{\phi_{2,1}} = -2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \{\phi_2\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{Bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Verify mass orthogonality**

$$
\{\phi_1\}^T[M]\{\phi_2\} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{Bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} 2 \\ -2 \end{Bmatrix}
= 2 - 2 = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4: Verify stiffness orthogonality**

$$
\{\phi_1\}^T[K]\{\phi_2\} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} 1500 & -500 \\ -500 & 1000 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{Bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{Bmatrix} 2500 \\ -2500 \end{Bmatrix}
= 2500 - 2500 = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5: Compute generalized masses**

$$M_1 = \{\phi_1\}^T[M]\{\phi_1\} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix} = 3 \text{ kg}$$

$$M_2 = \{\phi_2\}^T[M]\{\phi_2\} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{Bmatrix} 2 \\ -2 \end{Bmatrix} = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ kg}$$

$$\boxed{\{\phi_1\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{Bmatrix}, \quad \{\phi_2\} = \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{Bmatrix}, \quad \text{both orthogonal} \checkmark}$$

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(14 Marks)**

**A simplified vehicle suspension is modeled as a single-degree-of-freedom system with mass $m = 250$ kg and equivalent suspension stiffness $k = 40{,}000$ N/m. The car drives over a road bump at $v = 20$ m/s. The bump is approximated as a half-sine pulse of length $L = 0.4$ m.**

**Part (a):** Calculate the natural frequency, the time period, and the forcing frequency imparted by the road. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b):** If a small damper is added with damping ratio $\zeta = 0.3$, find the damped natural frequency. Comment on whether the vehicle will experience resonance at the given road speed. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B — Part (a):**

**Step 1: Natural frequency**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{40{,}000}{250}} = \sqrt{160} = 12.649 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Frequency in Hz**

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{12.649}{2\pi} = 2.013 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3: Period**

$$T = \frac{1}{f_n} = 0.497 \text{ s} \quad \text{or equivalently } T = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k} = 0.497 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Time duration of bump excitation**

The vehicle traverses the bump in time:

$$t_p = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{0.4}{20} = 0.02 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5: Dominant forcing frequency of the pulse**

A half-sine pulse of duration $t_p$ has its dominant frequency content at:

$$f_f = \frac{1}{2 t_p} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot 0.02} = 25 \text{ Hz}$$

**Comparison:** $f_f = 25$ Hz $\gg f_n = 2.013$ Hz.

**Step 6: Check for resonance**

Resonance occurs when forcing frequency equals natural frequency. Here $f_f / f_n = 25 / 2.013 \approx 12.4$, so the excitation is far above resonance.

**Step 7: Compute response amplitude using steady-state formula**

For base excitation, transmissibility at $r = \omega_f/\omega_n = 25/2.013 = 12.42$ is negligible.

$$\boxed{\omega_n = 12.65 \text{ rad/s}, \quad f_n = 2.01 \text{ Hz}, \quad T = 0.497 \text{ s}, \quad \text{no resonance risk}}$$

---

### **Solution to Question B — Part (b):**

**Step 1: Damped natural frequency formula**

For a damped SDOF system, the damped natural frequency is:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values** with $\omega_n = 12.649$ rad/s and $\zeta = 0.3$:

$$\omega_d = 12.649 \sqrt{1 - (0.3)^2} = 12.649 \sqrt{0.91} = 12.649 \times 0.9539$$

$$\omega_d = 12.066 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Convert to Hz**

$$f_d = \frac{\omega_d}{2\pi} = \frac{12.066}{2\pi} = 1.920 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4: Damping coefficient**

Critical damping: $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{40{,}000 \cdot 250} = 2 \times 3162.3 = 6324.6$ N·s/m

Actual damping: $c = \zeta c_c = 0.3 \times 6324.6 = 1897.4$ N·s/m

**Step 5: Resonance analysis**

Resonance in a damped system occurs at $\omega_{res} = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$:

$$\omega_{res} = 12.649\sqrt{1 - 0.18} = 12.649\sqrt{0.82} = 11.456 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_{res} = 1.823 \text{ Hz}$$

The forcing frequency $f_f = 25$ Hz is **far above** both $f_n = 2.01$ Hz and $f_{res} = 1.82$ Hz.

**Step 6: Conclusion**

$$\boxed{\omega_d = 12.07 \text{ rad/s}, \quad f_d = 1.92 \text{ Hz}, \quad \text{vehicle will NOT experience resonance at } v = 20 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Comment:** Since the forcing frequency from the road bump is much higher than the suspension natural frequency, the suspension will *isolate* the vehicle body from the road disturbance — the bump will be felt as a sharp transient but not amplified. The damping further reduces the transient amplitude.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rad/s to Hz** when the question asks for frequency in Hz. Always divide by $2\pi$.
> 2. **Mixing up natural frequency and damped natural frequency** — they are NOT the same. $\omega_d < \omega_n$ always.
> 3. **Skipping the determinant expansion** in MDOF problems — KTU expects you to show the characteristic polynomial expansion explicitly, not just write the final answer.
> 4. **Not normalizing mode shapes** — when required, divide by the maximum element or by $\sqrt{\{\phi\}^T[M]\{\phi\}}$.
> 5. **Confusing mass and stiffness orthogonality** — they are both true for distinct modes in an undamped system, but mass orthogonality is the *fundamental* one (stiffness orthogonality is derived from it).
> 6. **Ignoring the sign of mode shape ratios** — a negative ratio in higher modes indicates out-of-phase motion (presence of a node between masses).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **natural frequency** is intrinsic to a system: $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ for SDOF.
- An **N-DOF system has exactly N natural frequencies** and N mode shapes.
- The **eigenvalue problem** is the central equation: $([K] - \omega^2[M])\{\phi\} = \{0\}$.
- The **characteristic equation** $\det([K] - \omega^2[M]) = 0$ is a polynomial of degree N in $\omega^2$.
- **Mode shapes are not unique in magnitude** — only their *ratios* are determined; normalization gives absolute values.
- **Mode shapes are orthogonal** with respect to both mass and stiffness matrices for distinct natural frequencies.
- **Modal superposition** decouples the MDOF system into N independent SDOF systems — one per mode.
- **Lowest frequency = fundamental mode**; higher frequencies correspond to more complex patterns with more internal nodes.
- **Resonance condition** for undamped system: forcing frequency = natural frequency → unbounded amplitude.
- **Damped natural frequency** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ is always less than $\omega_n$ for $\zeta < 1$.
- **Real-world applications:** earthquake engineering, rotor dynamics, automotive suspension, aerospace flutter, machine tool chatter.
- **Symmetry of matrices:** $[M]$ and $[K]$ being symmetric and positive definite guarantees real, positive eigenvalues and orthogonal eigenvectors.
- **Number of nodes in mode $r$** (for a string/beam) is $r-1$; for discrete systems, "nodes" correspond to masses with zero displacement.
- **Generalized coordinates** $q_r$ carry the time-dependence; mode shapes $\phi_r$ carry the spatial dependence.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Strategy:** For this topic, focus on three things: (1) deriving and solving the eigenvalue problem for a 2-DOF system, (2) verifying orthogonality numerically, and (3) computing the free vibration response using modal superposition. These three operations cover ~85% of marks typically awarded in Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Systems and Wave Theory Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT632 - INDUSTRIAL NOISE AND VIBRATION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Continuous Systems and Wave Theory Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuous (Distributed Parameter) System:** A mechanical system in which the mass and elasticity (and sometimes damping) are continuously distributed throughout the body, rather than being concentrated at discrete points. The motion of such a system is described by **Partial Differential Equations (PDEs)** rather than the ordinary differential equations (ODEs) used for lumped parameter systems.

In the context of industrial noise and vibration control, continuous systems are critical because almost every real engineering structure—machine shafts, piping, panels, thin plates, and long slender members—behaves as a distributed system at higher frequencies. The governing mathematical framework for all continuous vibrating systems is the **Wave Equation**.

The general one-dimensional wave equation has the canonical form:

$$\frac{\partial^2 u(x,t)}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \frac{\partial^2 u(x,t)}{\partial x^2}$$

where:
- $u(x,t)$ = displacement (or rotation, depending on context)
- $c$ = **phase velocity / wave speed** (m/s)
- $x$ = spatial coordinate along the system
- $t$ = time

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Plucked Guitar String

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine plucking a guitar string. A single "discrete" model would treat the string as a single mass on a single spring — completely missing the music. A continuous system model, however, recognizes that the string is a distributed entity: every small segment of the string has its own tiny mass, its own tiny stiffness from neighboring segments, and can vibrate locally. The "wave" you see traveling down the string is exactly the solution to the wave equation — a cooperative motion of infinitely many infinitesimal masses connected by infinitesimal springs.

The **3 essential ingredients** of any continuous system are:
1. **Distributed Inertia** ($\rho$ — mass per unit volume)
2. **Distributed Elasticity** ($E$ — Young's Modulus / $G$ — Shear Modulus / $T$ — Tension)
3. **Geometric Constraint** (boundary conditions at the ends)

## 1.3 Classification of Continuous Systems in Industrial Practice

| System Type | Physical Example | Field Variable | Key Material Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **String / Cable** | Power transmission lines, belts | Transverse displacement $y(x,t)$ | Tension $T$ |
| **Longitudinal Rod / Bar** | Piston rod, drill string, hydraulic actuator | Axial displacement $u(x,t)$ | Young's Modulus $E$ |
| **Torsional Shaft** | Turbine shafts, drive shafts, propeller shafts | Angular twist $\theta(x,t)$ | Shear Modulus $G$ |
| **Euler–Bernoulli Beam** | Machine tool frames, floor joists | Transverse displacement $w(x,t)$ | $E$ + Area moment $I$ |
| **Membrane** | Loudspeaker cones, drum heads | Lateral displacement $w(x,y,t)$ | Tension $T$ per unit length |
| **Thin Plate** | Cabinet panels, duct walls, machine guards | Lateral displacement $w(x,y,t)$ | Flexural rigidity $D$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In this module, we focus on the **one-dimensional continuous systems**: strings, longitudinal rods, and torsional shafts. The wave theory established here forms the foundation for the multi-dimensional systems (beams, plates) studied in later modules.

## 1.4 Why Continuous Systems Matter in Noise & Vibration Control

- **High-frequency analysis:** When excitation frequency approaches the lowest natural frequency, the discrete model fails. Continuous models are essential above this range.
- **Wave propagation:** Understanding how vibration energy travels along shafts and pipes is fundamental to controlling structure-borne noise.
- **Modal density:** Continuous systems have theoretically infinite natural frequencies, with increasingly dense modal spacing.
- **Industrial relevance:** Gear whine, shaft whirling, pipe resonance, and panel radiation are all continuous-system phenomena.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Fundamental One-Dimensional Continuous Systems

The unified mathematical framework — **the wave equation** — applies to all three systems below. The only thing that changes is the **physical meaning of the wave speed $c$**.

### System A: Longitudinal Vibration of a Uniform Rod
- **Setup:** A prismatic rod of cross-sectional area $A$, density $\rho$, Young's modulus $E$, length $L$.
- **Field variable:** $u(x,t)$ = axial displacement at position $x$.
- **Wave equation:**

$$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = \frac{E}{\rho} \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad c_L = \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$$

- **Industrial example:** Acoustic wave inside a thin, fluid-filled pipe (quasi-longitudinal).

### System B: Transverse Vibration of a Stretched String
- **Setup:** A string of length $L$, mass per unit length $\mu$, under constant tension $T$.
- **Field variable:** $y(x,t)$ = transverse displacement.
- **Wave equation:**

$$\frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2} = \frac{T}{\mu} \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad c_T = \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$$

- **Industrial example:** Belt drives, band-saw blades, conveyor cords.

### System C: Torsional Vibration of a Circular Shaft
- **Setup:** A circular shaft of length $L$, density $\rho$, shear modulus $G$, polar moment of area $J$.
- **Field variable:** $\theta(x,t)$ = angular displacement (twist).
- **Wave equation:**

$$\frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial t^2} = \frac{G}{\rho} \frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial x^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad c_{tor} = \sqrt{\frac{G}{\rho}}$$

- **Industrial example:** Propeller shafts, turbine rotors, drilling strings.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for this topic. Memorize the wave speeds, boundary conditions, and natural-frequency formulas. **For KTU 2024, marks are routinely awarded for correctly writing the wave speed expression AND identifying the boundary conditions.**

### Core Equations and Wave Speeds

| System | Governing PDE | Wave Speed $c$ | Inertia Term | Stiffness Term |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Longitudinal Rod | $u_{tt} = c^2 u_{xx}$ | $c = \sqrt{E/\rho}$ | $\rho A$ (kg/m) | $EA$ (N) |
| Transverse String | $y_{tt} = c^2 y_{xx}$ | $c = \sqrt{T/\mu}$ | $\mu$ (kg/m) | $T$ (N) |
| Torsional Shaft | $\theta_{tt} = c^2 \theta_{xx}$ | $c = \sqrt{G/\rho}$ | $\rho J$ (kg·m) | $GJ$ (N·m²) |

> *Subscript notation:* $u_{tt} = \partial^2 u/\partial t^2$, $u_{xx} = \partial^2 u/\partial x^2$.

### Standard Boundary Conditions

| Boundary Type | Spatial Condition | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed (Clamped)** | $u(0,t) = 0$ | No displacement allowed |
| **Free** | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = 0$ | No internal force / torque |
| **Pinned / Simply Supported** | $u(L,t) = 0$ AND $\dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = 0$ | Displacement zero, no bending moment |
| **Sliding (Guided)** | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = 0$ | No rotation, free to translate |

### Natural Frequencies — One-Dimensional Continuous Systems

The general solution via separation of variables gives a **frequency equation** that depends on boundary conditions. Below: $\beta_n$ is the **wavenumber** of the $n^{th}$ mode, and $\omega_n = c \beta_n$.

| Boundary Condition (Length $L$) | Frequency Equation | Natural Frequencies $\omega_n$ |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed–Fixed (rod/string) | $\sin(\beta L) = 0$ | $\omega_n = \dfrac{n\pi c}{L},\; n = 1,2,3,\ldots$ |
| Free–Free | $\sin(\beta L) = 0$ | $\omega_n = \dfrac{n\pi c}{L},\; n = 1,2,3,\ldots$ |
| Fixed–Free (cantilever) | $\cos(\beta L) = 0$ | $\omega_n = \dfrac{(2n-1)\pi c}{2L},\; n = 1,2,3,\ldots$ |
| Fixed–Pinned | $\tan(\beta L) = \beta L$ | $\omega_n = \dfrac{\beta_n c}{L}$ (transcendental, solved numerically) |

### Mode Shape Functions (Fixed–Fixed and Free–Free)

$$X_n(x) = \sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)$$

The total solution is then:

$$u(x,t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[A_n \cos(\omega_n t) + B_n \sin(\omega_n t)\right] \sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)$$

## 2.3 Traveling Wave Interpretation (D'Alembert's Insight)

> [!NOTE]
> **D'Alembert's General Solution:** Any solution of the 1-D wave equation can be written as the sum of two oppositely traveling waves:

$$u(x,t) = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct)$$

- $f(x - ct)$ → wave traveling in **+x** direction (rightward)
- $g(x + ct)$ → wave traveling in **−x** direction (leftward)

When the wave reflects from a boundary, **forward and backward waves superpose** to create a **standing wave** (the modal solution above). This is the fundamental link between **wave theory** and **modal theory** in vibration analysis.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industrial Application | Continuous System Modeled | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Drilling-string vibration | Torsional/longitudinal shaft | Prevents torsional fatigue failures |
| Hydraulic pipelines | Longitudinal rod with fluid coupling | Avoids water-hammer / pipe rupture |
| Belt-drives in industry | Transverse string | Prevents belt flutter and whip |
| Turbine shaft design | Torsional shaft with disks | Critical-speed calculation |
| Quench-tank piping | Longitudinal rod | Avoids resonance at pump frequencies |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section presents the **complete, exhaustive derivations** required for the 14-mark KTU board questions. Each derivation is built from Newton's second law applied to an infinitesimal element. No steps are skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation I: Wave Equation for Longitudinal Vibration of a Uniform Rod

### Step 1 — System Description
Consider a uniform prismatic rod of:
- Cross-sectional area: $A$
- Density: $\rho$
- Young's modulus: $E$
- Length: $L$

The rod is aligned along the $x$-axis. Let $u(x,t)$ denote the **axial displacement** of the cross-section originally at position $x$.

### Step 2 — Stress–Strain Relationship
The longitudinal strain at position $x$ is:

$$\varepsilon(x,t) = \frac{\partial u(x,t)}{\partial x}$$

By Hooke's Law, the longitudinal stress is:

$$\sigma(x,t) = E \cdot \varepsilon(x,t) = E \frac{\partial u(x,t)}{\partial x}$$

### Step 3 — Internal Axial Force
The internal axial force at any cross-section is:

$$F(x,t) = \sigma(x,t) \cdot A = EA \frac{\partial u(x,t)}{\partial x}$$

### Step 4 — Isolate an Infinitesimal Element
Consider an element of length $dx$ located between $x$ and $x+dx$. The forces acting on its two faces are:

- Left face at $x$: $F(x,t)$ acts in $+x$ direction (tension).
- Right face at $x+dx$: $F(x+dx,t)$ acts in $-x$ direction.

### Step 5 — Expand the Right-Face Force Using a Taylor Series

$$F(x+dx,t) = F(x,t) + \frac{\partial F}{\partial x}\,dx + \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial^2 F}{\partial x^2}\,(dx)^2 + \cdots$$

Retaining only the first-order (linear) term:

$$F(x+dx,t) \approx F(x,t) + \frac{\partial F}{\partial x}\,dx$$

### Step 6 — Net Force on the Element (Newton's Second Law Setup)
Taking the rightward direction as positive, the **net axial force** on the element is:

$$F_{net} = F(x,t) - F(x+dx,t) = -\frac{\partial F}{\partial x}\,dx$$

Substituting $F = EA\,u_x$:

$$F_{net} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left[EA \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right]dx = -EA \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\,dx$$

(Absent body forces, assuming uniform $E$ and $A$.)

### Step 7 — Apply Newton's Second Law
The mass of the element is $\rho A\,dx$. Newton's second law $F = ma$ gives:

$$\rho A\,dx \cdot \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = -EA \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\,dx$$

> Note: a positive $\partial^2 u/\partial x^2$ (concave-up displacement) means the element is being compressed, hence the net force is in the negative $x$ direction. This justifies the negative sign that is *essential* in the wave equation. Some texts define $F$ with the opposite sign convention and obtain the same final PDE — **students must be consistent with their sign convention**.

### Step 8 — Simplify and Identify the Wave Speed
Cancel $A\,dx$ from both sides:

$$\rho \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = -E \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$

Many texts (and KTU exam solutions) write this with a **positive** coefficient on the right-hand side, using the alternative sign convention that defines net force on the element as $F(x+dx,t) - F(x,t)$. Adopting that convention:

$$\rho \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = E \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$

Dividing through by $\rho$:

$$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = \frac{E}{\rho} \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$

Define the **longitudinal wave speed** $c_L$ as:

$$c_L = \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

Hence the **governing wave equation** is:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = c_L^2 \,\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\;}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Wave Equation for Transverse Vibration of a Stretched String

### Step 1 — System Description
A flexible string of length $L$, mass per unit length $\mu$ (kg/m), stretched under constant tension $T$ (N). Small transverse vibrations are assumed: $\left|\partial y/\partial x\right| \ll 1$.

### Step 2 — Geometry of Tension
At any point, the string makes an angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal, where:

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{\partial y}{\partial x} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sin\alpha = \frac{\partial y/\partial x}{\sqrt{1+(\partial y/\partial x)^2}} \approx \frac{\partial y}{\partial x}$$

(small-angle approximation).

### Step 3 — Vertical Components of Tension
The vertical component of tension at the **left** end of element $dx$ is $T\sin\alpha(x)$. The vertical component at the **right** end is $T\sin\alpha(x+dx)$.

### Step 4 — Net Vertical Force

$$F_y = T\sin\alpha(x+dx) - T\sin\alpha(x) \approx T\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{\partial y}{\partial x}\right)dx = T\frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2}\,dx$$

### Step 5 — Apply Newton's Second Law
The mass of the element is $\mu\,dx$, and its vertical acceleration is $\partial^2 y/\partial t^2$:

$$\mu\,dx \cdot \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2} = T\frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2}\,dx$$

### Step 6 — Simplify and Identify the Wave Speed

$$\mu \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2} = T \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2}$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2} = \frac{T}{\mu} \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2}$$

Define the **transverse wave speed** in the string:

$$c_T = \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}} \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

The **wave equation** is:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial t^2} = c_T^2 \,\frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2}\;}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Wave Equation for Torsional Vibration of a Circular Shaft

### Step 1 — System Description
A uniform circular shaft of length $L$, density $\rho$, shear modulus $G$, polar moment of area $J$. The angular twist about the shaft axis at position $x$ is $\theta(x,t)$.

### Step 2 — Torsion Formula (St. Venant)
The internal torque in the shaft is:

$$T(x,t) = GJ \frac{\partial \theta(x,t)}{\partial x}$$

### Step 3 — Elemental Mass Moment of Inertia
For an element of length $dx$:

$$dI = \rho J\,dx$$

### Step 4 — Net Torque on the Element
Using the same expansion as in the rod case:

$$T_{net} = GJ\frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial x^2}\,dx$$

### Step 5 — Rotational Newton's Second Law
Rate of change of angular momentum = net torque:

$$\rho J\,dx \cdot \frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial t^2} = GJ\frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial x^2}\,dx$$

### Step 6 — Simplify and Identify the Wave Speed

$$\frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial t^2} = \frac{G}{\rho} \frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial x^2}$$

Define the **torsional wave speed**:

$$c_{tor} = \sqrt{\frac{G}{\rho}} \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

The **wave equation** is:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial t^2} = c_{tor}^2 \,\frac{\partial^2 \theta}{\partial x^2}\;}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Solution: Separation of Variables (Longitudinal Rod, Fixed–Fixed)

### Step 1 — Assume a Separable Form

$$u(x,t) = X(x)\,T(t)$$

### Step 2 — Substitute into the PDE

$$X(x)\,T''(t) = c^2 X''(x)\,T(t)$$

### Step 3 — Separate the Variables

$$\frac{T''(t)}{c^2 T(t)} = \frac{X''(x)}{X(x)} = -\beta^2$$

where $-\beta^2$ is the **separation constant** (negative so that the solution is sinusoidal in both $x$ and $t$, giving oscillatory motion).

### Step 4 — Solve the Two ODEs

**Spatial ODE:**

$$X''(x) + \beta^2 X(x) = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad X(x) = C_1\cos(\beta x) + C_2\sin(\beta x)$$

**Temporal ODE:**

$$T''(t) + \omega^2 T(t) = 0 \quad\text{where}\quad \omega = c\beta$$

$$T(t) = C_3\cos(\omega t) + C_4\sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 5 — Apply Fixed–Fixed Boundary Conditions
For a rod fixed at $x=0$ and $x=L$:

$$u(0,t) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; X(0) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_1 = 0$$

$$u(L,t) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; X(L) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C_2\sin(\beta L) = 0$$

For a non-trivial solution ($C_2 \neq 0$):

$$\sin(\beta L) = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \beta_n = \frac{n\pi}{L}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

### Step 6 — Natural Frequencies and Mode Shapes

$$\omega_n = c\beta_n = \frac{n\pi c}{L} = \frac{n\pi}{L}\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$$

$$X_n(x) = \sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)$$

### Step 7 — General Solution (Superposition)

$$u(x,t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)\left[A_n\cos(\omega_n t) + B_n\sin(\omega_n t)\right]$$

The coefficients $A_n$ and $B_n$ are determined from initial conditions using orthogonality of sine functions:

$$A_n = \frac{2}{L}\int_0^L u(x,0)\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)dx, \quad B_n = \frac{2}{\omega_n L}\int_0^L \dot{u}(x,0)\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)dx$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Comparison: Wave Speeds in Common Engineering Materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These numbers appear frequently in numerical problems. Memorize the **orders of magnitude**.

| Material | $E$ (GPa) | $G$ (GPa) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $c_L = \sqrt{E/\rho}$ (m/s) | $c_{tor} = \sqrt{G/\rho}$ (m/s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Steel | 200 | 80 | 7850 | 5050 | 3190 |
| Aluminium | 70 | 26 | 2700 | 5090 | 3100 |
| Copper | 110 | 42 | 8960 | 3500 | 2165 |
| Concrete | 30 | 12 | 2400 | 3540 | 2236 |
| Rubber (soft) | 0.01 | 0.003 | 1100 | 95 | 52 |

> **Key insight:** The longitudinal wave speed in steel (~5 km/s) is roughly 15 times the speed of sound in air (~343 m/s) — this is why vibrations travel along a steel shaft much faster than the resulting airborne noise propagates.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Continuous-System Analysis Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **logical workflow** for analysing any one-dimensional continuous vibrating system, as expected in KTU problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify the System Type] --> B{Discrete or Continuous?}
    B -- Discrete --> C[Lumped Parameter Model - ODE]
    B -- Continuous --> D[Select the PDE]
    D --> D1[Longitudinal Rod: u_tt = E/rho times u_xx]
    D --> D2[Transverse String: y_tt = T/mu times y_xx]
    D --> D3[Torsional Shaft: theta_tt = G/rho times theta_xx]
    D1 --> E[Compute Wave Speed c]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Identify Boundary Conditions]
    F --> F1[Fixed: u = 0]
    F --> F2[Free: du/dx = 0]
    F --> F3[Pinned: u = 0, d2u/dt2 = 0]
    F1 --> G[Apply Separation of Variables]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Derive Frequency Equation]
    H --> I[Solve for Wavenumber beta_n]
    I --> J[Compute Natural Frequencies omega_n = c times beta_n]
    J --> K[Obtain Mode Shapes X_n of x]
    K --> L[Superpose All Modes]
    L --> M[Apply Initial Conditions via Orthogonality]
    M --> N[Final Solution u of x and t]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Comparison: Discrete vs. Continuous System Modelling

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph DISCRETE[Lumped Parameter System]
        M1[Mass m] -- k1 --> M2[Mass m]
        M2 -- k2 --> M3[Mass m]
    end
    subgraph CONTINUOUS[Continuous System]
        S1[Infinitesimal Mass rho A dx] -. k dx .-> S2[Infinitesimal Mass rho A dx]
        S2 -. k dx .-> S3[Infinitesimal Mass rho A dx]
        S3 -. k dx .-> S4[Infinitesimal Mass rho A dx]
    end
    DISCRETE -. Finite DOF .-> ODE[Ordinary Differential Equation]
    CONTINUOUS -. Infinite DOF .-> PDE[Partial Differential Equation]
```

## 4.3 Boundary Condition Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start: Pick End x = 0 or x = L] --> Q{Is the end physically clamped or welded?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Fixed: u = 0]
    Q -- No --> S{Is the end free to move axially?}
    S -- Yes --> T[Free: du/dx = 0]
    S -- No --> U{Is the end pinned or simply supported?}
    U -- Yes --> V[Pinned: u = 0 and d2u/dt2 = 0]
    U -- No --> W[Sliding or Mixed: Apply Engineering Judgement]
    R --> BC[Apply as Boundary Condition for X of x]
    T --> BC
    V --> BC
    W --> BC
```

## 4.4 Physical Setup Diagram (Mermaid Representation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    W[Wall Fixed End x = 0] -->|Rigid Support| R[Rod or String of Length L]
    R -->|Cross Section A, Density rho, Modulus E or G| E[End x = L]
    E --> ENDTYPE{End Condition}
    ENDTYPE -- Fixed --> F1[Wall]
    ENDTYPE -- Free --> F2[No Constraint]
    ENDTYPE -- Spring k --> F3[Elastic Support k]
    ENDTYPE -- Dashpot c --> F4[Viscous Damper c]
    ENDTYPE -- Mass M --> F5[Lumped Mass M]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions test direct recall. Answer each in **3–4 lines** with the key formula highlighted.

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a continuous system. How does it differ from a discrete system in terms of mathematical modelling?**

**Model Answer:**
A continuous (distributed parameter) system has its mass, stiffness, and damping **continuously distributed** throughout its volume, with no separation into distinct discrete elements. Consequently, its motion is governed by a **partial differential equation (PDE)** — the wave equation. A discrete (lumped parameter) system, by contrast, has finite degrees of freedom described by a finite set of coordinates and is governed by an **ordinary differential equation (ODE)**. Continuous systems have an infinite number of natural frequencies, while discrete systems have a finite number.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the one-dimensional wave equation. Define the wave speed for a longitudinal rod and a torsional shaft.**

**Model Answer:**
The one-dimensional wave equation is:

$$\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$

- For a **longitudinal rod**: $c_L = \sqrt{E/\rho}$ (m/s), where $E$ is Young's modulus and $\rho$ is the density.
- For a **torsional shaft**: $c_{tor} = \sqrt{G/\rho}$ (m/s), where $G$ is the shear modulus.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE follows a **Module Internal Choice** pattern: a single 14-mark question with two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks, OR a two-part question (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks with choice between two full questions. We provide BOTH complete alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the one-dimensional wave equation governing the **longitudinal vibration of a uniform rod**. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A uniform steel rod of length $L = 2\,\text{m}$, cross-section $A = 200\,\text{mm}^2$, and density $\rho = 7850\,\text{kg/m}^3$ is fixed at both ends. Given $E = 2.0 \times 10^{11}\,\text{N/m}^2$, determine the **first three natural frequencies** and the **corresponding mode shapes**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation — refer to Section 3.1 (above).**
Key elements that MUST appear for full 7 marks:
1. Free-body diagram of element $dx$ — **1 mark**
2. Hooke's law and expression for axial force $F = EA\,u_x$ — **1 mark**
3. Taylor expansion of $F(x+dx,t)$ — **1 mark**
4. Net force $= -EA\,u_{xx}\,dx$ — **1 mark**
5. Newton's second law application — **1 mark**
6. Final PDE $\partial^2 u/\partial t^2 = (E/\rho)\partial^2 u/\partial x^2$ — **1 mark**
7. Identification of wave speed $c = \sqrt{E/\rho}$ and statement of assumptions (small strain, linear elastic, uniform cross-section, no damping) — **1 mark**

**Assumptions:**
- Hooke's law valid (linear elastic material)
- Small displacements and small strains
- Uniform cross-section and material properties
- No body forces (gravity neglected)
- Plane sections remain plane

**(b) Numerical Solution**

Step 1: Compute the wave speed.

$$c_L = \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.0 \times 10^{11}}{7850}} = \sqrt{2.5477 \times 10^{7}} \approx 5047.5\,\text{m/s}$$

Step 2: Apply the fixed–fixed natural-frequency formula.

$$\omega_n = \frac{n\pi c_L}{L} = \frac{n\pi \times 5047.5}{2} = n\pi \times 2523.75\,\text{rad/s}$$

Step 3: Compute the first three natural frequencies.

$$\omega_1 = 1 \times \pi \times 2523.75 = 7927.6\,\text{rad/s} \quad(\approx 1261.7\,\text{Hz})$$

$$\omega_2 = 2 \times \pi \times 2523.75 = 15855.2\,\text{rad/s} \quad(\approx 2523.4\,\text{Hz})$$

$$\omega_3 = 3 \times \pi \times 2523.75 = 23782.8\,\text{rad/s} \quad(\approx 3785.1\,\text{Hz})$$

Step 4: Mode shapes.

$$X_n(x) = \sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right) = \sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{2}\right), \quad 0 \le x \le 2\,\text{m}$$

- $X_1(x) = \sin(\pi x/2)$ — one half-wave
- $X_2(x) = \sin(\pi x)$ — one full wave
- $X_3(x) = \sin(3\pi x/2)$ — one and a half waves

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check:** $A$ was given but is **not needed** for natural-frequency calculation of a uniform rod — it only affects the force level, not the frequency. Students who waste time computing $EA$ or $\rho A$ in part (b) are losing 1 mark on time efficiency.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion for the **transverse vibration of a uniform stretched string**, clearly defining all symbols. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A string of length $L = 1.5\,\text{m}$, mass per unit length $\mu = 0.05\,\text{kg/m}$, is stretched with a tension $T = 200\,\text{N}$. The string is plucked at its mid-point by an amount $h = 0.01\,\text{m}$ and released from rest. Find the **general solution** and the **coefficient of the first three modes**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation — refer to Section 3.2 (above).**
Required ingredients for full 7 marks:
1. Free-body diagram and small-angle assumption — **1 mark**
2. Vertical components of tension — **1 mark**
3. Net vertical force expression — **1 mark**
4. Newton's second law: $\mu\,y_{tt} = T\,y_{xx}$ — **1 mark**
5. Final PDE $y_{tt} = (T/\mu)\,y_{xx}$ — **1 mark**
6. Wave speed $c_T = \sqrt{T/\mu}$ — **1 mark**
7. Assumptions listed — **1 mark**

**(b) Numerical Solution**

Step 1: Compute the wave speed.

$$c_T = \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}} = \sqrt{\frac{200}{0.05}} = \sqrt{4000} \approx 63.246\,\text{m/s}$$

Step 2: Natural frequencies (fixed–fixed string, both ends pinned).

$$\omega_n = \frac{n\pi c_T}{L} = \frac{n\pi \times 63.246}{1.5} = n \times 132.46\,\text{rad/s}$$

Step 3: Initial conditions. Plucked at mid-point $(x_0 = L/2)$ by an amount $h$, then released from rest.

The initial displacement profile is triangular (assuming an ideal pluck):

$$u(x,0) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{2h}{L}\,x, & 0 \le x \le L/2 \\[6pt] \dfrac{2h}{L}(L-x), & L/2 \le x \le L \end{cases}$$

and the initial velocity is zero everywhere: $\dot{u}(x,0) = 0$.

Step 4: Because the initial velocity is zero, $B_n = 0$ for all $n$.

Step 5: Compute $A_n$ using orthogonality.

$$A_n = \frac{2}{L}\int_0^L u(x,0)\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)dx$$

Splitting the integral at $x = L/2$:

$$A_n = \frac{2}{L}\left[\int_0^{L/2} \frac{2h}{L}x\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)dx + \int_{L/2}^{L} \frac{2h}{L}(L-x)\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)dx\right]$$

Step 6: Using standard integration by parts, this reduces to:

$$A_n = \frac{8h}{n^2\pi^2}\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi}{2}\right)$$

Step 7: First three coefficients (note: $\sin(n\pi/2) = 0$ for even $n$):

- $n = 1$: $A_1 = \dfrac{8(0.01)}{\pi^2}\sin(\pi/2) = \dfrac{0.08}{\pi^2} \approx 8.106 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
- $n = 2$: $A_2 = 0$ (node at pluck point)
- $n = 3$: $A_3 = \dfrac{8(0.01)}{9\pi^2}\sin(3\pi/2) = -\dfrac{0.08}{9\pi^2} \approx -9.007 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}$

Step 8: General solution.

$$y(x,t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \cos(\omega_n t)\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right)$$

$$y(x,t) = 8.106\times10^{-3}\cos(132.46\,t)\sin(\pi x/1.5) - 9.007\times10^{-4}\cos(397.4\,t)\sin(3\pi x/1.5) + \cdots$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the small-angle assumption** in the string derivation — the substitution $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha = \partial y/\partial x$ is a critical step. **−1 mark** if missed.
> 2. **Mixing up sign conventions** in the rod derivation. If the final PDE has a negative sign on the right-hand side, the student may have defined the net force inconsistently. **−1 mark** unless a clear sign convention is stated.
> 3. **Failing to apply BOTH boundary conditions** in part (b). The fixed-end condition $X(0)=0$ gives $C_1=0$ and the condition at $x=L$ gives the frequency equation. **−2 marks** if only one is applied.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention that the rod's cross-sectional area $A$ cancels out** — examiners deduct marks for including $A$ in the frequency formula.
> 5. **Units mismatch:** $\omega$ in rad/s, $f$ in Hz, $c$ in m/s, $L$ in m. **−0.5 mark** for unit errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Continuous Systems & Wave Theory**

### Core Concepts
- A **continuous system** has mass, stiffness, and damping **distributed** throughout its body — modelled by a **PDE**.
- All 1-D continuous vibrating systems are governed by the **wave equation** $u_{tt} = c^2 u_{xx}$.
- Three canonical systems: **longitudinal rod**, **transverse string**, **torsional shaft**.
- Wave speed expressions:
  - $c_L = \sqrt{E/\rho}$
  - $c_T = \sqrt{T/\mu}$
  - $c_{tor} = \sqrt{G/\rho}$

### Mathematical Tools
- **Separation of variables**: $u(x,t) = X(x)T(t)$ → two ODEs.
- **Boundary conditions** determine the **frequency equation**.
- For fixed–fixed / free–free: $\sin(\beta L) = 0 \Rightarrow \beta_n = n\pi/L$.
- For fixed–free: $\cos(\beta L) = 0 \Rightarrow \beta_n = (2n-1)\pi/(2L)$.
- For fixed–pinned: $\tan(\beta L) = \beta L$ (transcendental — solved numerically).

### Solution & Modes
- **Mode shapes** $X_n(x) = \sin(n\pi x/L)$ for fixed–fixed.
- **Natural frequencies** $\omega_n = c \beta_n = n\pi c / L$.
- General solution = **superposition** of all modes, weighted by $A_n$, $B_n$.
- $A_n$, $B_n$ computed via **orthogonality of sine functions**.

### Physical Insight
- **D'Alembert**: $u(x,t) = f(x-ct) + g(x+ct)$ — forward and backward waves.
- Boundary reflections superpose forward and backward waves to create **standing waves** (the modal solution).
- Continuous systems have **infinite** natural frequencies, with **dense** modal spacing at high frequency.

### Exam-Specific Must-Knows
- **Stating assumptions** is mandatory for full marks in derivation questions.
- **Sign convention** must be clearly stated.
- **Cross-section area cancels** for uniform rods — don't include it in frequency formulas.
- Know the **orders of magnitude**: steel $c_L \approx 5000$ m/s; aluminium similar; rubber $\sim 100$ m/s.
- Use SI units throughout; double-check **rad/s vs Hz** conversions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wave Equation and Relation to Acoustics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT632 - INDUSTRIAL NOISE AND VIBRATION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Wave Equation and Relation to Acoustics # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **acoustic wave equation** is a second-order partial differential equation that describes the propagation of small mechanical disturbances (sound) through a fluid medium. In one spatial dimension, for a homogeneous, lossless medium, it is expressed as: $$\frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}}$$ where $p(x,t)$ is the **acoustic (sound) pressure fluctuation** in Pascals (Pa), $x$ is the spatial coordinate in meters (m), $t$ is the time in seconds (s), and $c$ is the **speed of sound** in the medium (m/s), given by: $$c = \sqrt{\frac{B}{\rho_{0}}} = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma P_{0}}{\rho_{0}}}$$ Here, $B$ is the **adiabatic bulk modulus** (Pa), $\rho_0$ is the **ambient (mean) fluid density** (kg/m³), $\gamma$ is the **ratio of specific heats** ($C_p/C_v$, dimensionless), and $P_0$ is the **ambient static pressure** (Pa). For a standard atmosphere at **20 °C**, the speed of sound in air is approximately **c = 343 m/s**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The wave equation forms the mathematical foundation of industrial noise control. Every acoustic phenomenon — noise propagation, absorption, reflection, and resonance — is governed by a form of this equation. Mastery of its derivation is essential for Part B (14-mark) problems. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a long row of billiard balls placed on a smooth table, touching each other. If you push the first ball gently, it nudges the second, which nudges the third, and so on. The disturbance travels down the line, but each individual ball only moves a tiny bit from its rest position. The "push" (pressure pulse) travels far, but the matter itself barely moves. This is exactly what happens with sound: - The **disturbance** (pressure variation) propagates at speed $c$ through the medium. - The **particles** of the medium (air molecules) merely oscillate about their mean positions. - Sound is a **longitudinal mechanical wave** — the vibration direction is *parallel* to the propagation direction. A vibrating motor, a struck drum, or a human vocal cord creates tiny, rapid local compressions and rarefactions in air. These pressure fluctuations propagate as an **acoustic wave** carrying energy outward. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition Box — Acoustic Pressure ($p$):** > The difference between the instantaneous total pressure and the ambient (static) pressure: > $$p(x,t) = P(x,t) - P_{0}$$ > It is this fluctuating part that the human ear detects, not the absolute atmospheric pressure (~101,325 Pa). ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Value in Air (20 °C) | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Speed of sound | $c$ | **343** | m/s | | Density of air | $\rho_0$ | **1.204** | kg/m³ | | Acoustic impedance of air | $Z_0 = \rho_0 c$ | **≈ 413** | Pa·s/m (rayl) | | Ratio of specific heats | $\gamma$ | **1.4** | — | | Reference sound pressure | $p_{ref}$ | **20 × 10⁻⁶** | Pa | | Reference sound intensity | $I_{ref}$ | **10⁻¹²** | W/m² | | Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_0$ | **101,325** | Pa | > [!NOTE] > The product $\rho_0 c$ is called the **characteristic acoustic impedance** of the medium. It governs how much pressure is generated for a given particle velocity, and is the cornerstone of reflection/transmission analysis at interfaces. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Foundational Acoustic Equations The wave equation is **not** postulated; it is *derived* by combining three fundamental physical principles applied to a fluid element. Each of the three governing equations addresses a different aspect of fluid behaviour. ### 2.1.1 Equation of Continuity (Conservation of Mass) This law states that mass can neither be created nor destroyed in a fluid element. For a one-dimensional control volume of cross-sectional area $A$ and thickness $dx$, the net mass flow rate out must equal the rate of decrease of mass within the element. $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho_{0} \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0$$ where: - $\rho(x,t) = \rho_0 + \rho'$ is the instantaneous density (kg/m³). - $u(x,t)$ is the **acoustic particle velocity** (m/s), the velocity of the medium's small oscillations. - The product $\rho_0 u$ represents the **mass flux** (kg/(m²·s)). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters:** It couples density changes to motion. If particles move apart ($\partial u/\partial x > 0$), the local density must drop. ### 2.1.2 Equation of Motion (Euler's Linearised Form) Newton's second law applied to the same fluid element. The net pressure force across the element accelerates it. Linearising for small disturbances (neglecting the product of small quantities): $$\rho_{0} \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$ This is a **force balance**, not an energy balance. The LHS is inertial force per unit volume, RHS is the net pressure gradient force per unit volume. ### 2.1.3 Equation of State (Thermodynamic Coupling) For acoustic processes that occur much faster than thermal diffusion, the compression/expansion is **adiabatic** (no heat exchange with surroundings). For an ideal gas undergoing small adiabatic perturbations: $$p = c^{2} \rho \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = c^{2} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$$ The proportionality constant $c^2 = \gamma P_0 / \rho_0$ is the square of the speed of sound. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Trap:** Students often write $p = c \rho$. The correct relation is $p = c^2 \rho$ where $c$ has units of m/s. The factor $c$ alone has the wrong units for a pressure–density proportionality. ## 2.2 Why Combine All Three? None of the three equations alone can predict wave propagation: - **Continuity** relates $\rho$ and $u$. - **Motion** relates $p$ and $u$. - **State** relates $p$ and $\rho$. By eliminating $\rho$ and $u$ (the *acoustical* variables), we obtain a single equation in $p$ alone — the celebrated **acoustic wave equation**. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho_0 \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0$ | Mass conservation | Derivation step 1 | | 2 | $\rho_0 \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}$ | Newton's 2nd law for fluid | Derivation step 2 | | 3 | $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial t} = c^2 \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$ | Adiabatic equation of state | Derivation step 3 | | 4 | $\dfrac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = \dfrac{1}{c^2} \dfrac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}$ | **Acoustic wave equation** | Core KTU equation | | 5 | $c = \sqrt{B/\rho_0} = \sqrt{\gamma P_0 / \rho_0}$ | Speed of sound | Frequency-wavelength link | | 6 | $Z_0 = \rho_0 c$ | Characteristic impedance | Reflection coefficient | | 7 | $I = \dfrac{p_{rms}^{2}}{\rho_0 c}$ | Acoustic intensity | Power per unit area | | 8 | $\lambda = c/f$ | Wavelength | Frequency scaling | | 9 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{rms}/p_{ref})$ | Sound Pressure Level (dB) | Industrial noise metrics | | 10 | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_{ref})$ | Sound Intensity Level (dB) | Power-based dB scale | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The wave equation is the engineer's lens into: - **Industrial noise control** — designing silencers, enclosures, and barriers. - **Architectural acoustics** — predicting reverberation and room modes. - **Ultrasonic NDT (non-destructive testing)** — locating cracks via reflected waves. - **Environmental noise** — predicting propagation from highways, factories, and airports. - **Hearing conservation** — calculating permissible exposure durations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why $\partial^2/\partial t^2$ appears (not first-order):** A wave must *propagate* — meaning the rate of change of pressure at one location depends on what happened at adjacent locations a moment ago. The second time derivative encodes *momentum* of the disturbance. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the 1-D Acoustic Wave Equation This is the **single most important derivation** in industrial noise and vibration control. KTU examiners expect every algebraic step. ### Step 1 — Differentiate the Equation of Continuity with Respect to Time Starting with the continuity equation: $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho_{0} \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0$$ Differentiate both sides with respect to $t$ (with $\rho_0$ constant, since the mean density does not change with time): $$\frac{\partial^{2} \rho}{\partial t^{2}} + \rho_{0} \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right) = 0$$ Since time and space derivatives commute (Clairaut's theorem for smooth fields): $$\frac{\partial^{2} \rho}{\partial t^{2}} + \rho_{0} \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}\right) = 0 \quad \text{...(i)}$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute the Equation of Motion From the linearised Euler equation: $$\rho_{0} \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{1}{\rho_{0}} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$ Insert this into equation (i): $$\frac{\partial^{2} \rho}{\partial t^{2}} + \rho_{0} \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-\frac{1}{\rho_{0}} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) = 0$$ The $\rho_0$ cancels: $$\frac{\partial^{2} \rho}{\partial t^{2}} - \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial x^{2}} = 0 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ ### Step 3 — Use the Equation of State to Eliminate Density From the adiabatic equation of state: $$p = c^{2} \rho \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = c^{2} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$$ Differentiate again with respect to $t$: $$\frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}} = c^{2} \frac{\partial^{2} \rho}{\partial t^{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial^{2} \rho}{\partial t^{2}} = \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$ ### Step 4 — Combine (ii) and (iii) Substitute (iii) into (ii): $$\frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}} - \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial x^{2}} = 0$$ Rearranging gives the canonical form: $$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}}\;}$$ This is the **one-dimensional acoustic wave equation**. It is a linear, homogeneous, second-order PDE with constant coefficients. ## 3.2 D'Alembert's General Solution (Travelling-Wave Form) Applying the method of characteristics, the general solution to the wave equation is: $$p(x,t) = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct)$$ where $f$ and $g$ are **arbitrary twice-differentiable functions** determined by initial and boundary conditions. **Physical interpretation:** - $f(x - ct)$: a wave travelling in the **+x direction** (forward-travelling). - $g(x + ct)$: a wave travelling in the **−x direction** (backward-travelling, or reflected). To verify, apply the chain rule. Let $\xi = x - ct$ and $\eta = x + ct$: $$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = f'(\xi), \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial t} = -c\, f'(\xi)$$ $$\frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x^{2}} = f''(\xi), \quad \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial t^{2}} = c^{2} f''(\xi)$$ Thus $\partial^2 f/\partial x^2 = (1/c^2)\partial^2 f/\partial t^2$. Same for $g$. Both individually satisfy the wave equation, so their sum does too. ## 3.3 Harmonic (Sinusoidal) Solution For **time-harmonic** excitation — the most common case in industrial noise (motors, fans, compressors at fixed frequency) — we assume a solution of the form: $$p(x,t) = \Re\{\hat{P}(x)\, e^{j\omega t}\}$$ where $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency (rad/s) and $\hat{P}(x)$ is the complex pressure amplitude (a phasor). Substituting into the wave equation, the time derivative $\partial^2/\partial t^2$ becomes multiplication by $(j\omega)^2 = -\omega^2$: $$\frac{\partial^{2} \hat{P}}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{-\omega^{2}}{c^{2}} \hat{P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial^{2} \hat{P}}{\partial x^{2}} + k^{2} \hat{P} = 0$$ where $k = \omega/c$ is the **acoustic wavenumber** (rad/m). The solution is: $$\hat{P}(x) = A e^{-jkx} + B e^{+jkx}$$ The first term is the forward-travelling wave; the second is the backward-travelling wave. The wavenumber–frequency–wavelength relationship is: $$k = \frac{\omega}{c} = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{c}{f}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Link:** The wavenumber $k$ is what allows noise barriers, mufflers, and quarter-wave resonators to be physically sized. For example, a quarter-wave resonator of length $L$ resonates when $kL = \pi/2$, i.e. $L = \lambda/4$. ## 3.4 Extension to Three Dimensions For a wave propagating in three-dimensional space, the wave equation generalises to: $$\nabla^{2} p = \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}}$$ where the **Laplacian operator** in Cartesian coordinates is: $$\nabla^{2} p = \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial z^{2}}$$ The general solution in 3-D is a **spherical wave** from a point source: $$p(r,t) = \frac{A}{r}\, f(t - r/c) + \frac{B}{r}\, g(t + r/c)$$ The $1/r$ amplitude decay is the well-known **inverse-square law** of sound intensity ($I \propto 1/r^2$). ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: From Microscopic to Macroscopic | Mathematical Step | Physical Law | Variables Eliminated | Remaining Variable | |---|---|---|---| | Continuity | Conservation of mass | — | $\rho$, $u$ | | Motion (Euler) | Newton's 2nd law | $u$ | $p$, $\rho$ | | State (adiabatic) | First law of thermodynamics | $\rho$ | $p$ alone | | Combined | Wave propagation | $\rho$, $u$ | $p$ only | The wave equation is the **mathematical embodiment** of the fact that a localised pressure disturbance must travel as a wave at the local speed of sound. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Derivation Logic Flow The following Mermaid diagram maps the logical flow of how the three governing equations combine to yield the wave equation. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Equation of Continuity
Conservation of Mass] --> B[Differentiate with respect to t] B --> C[Substitute equation of motion
Euler linearised] C --> D[Density eliminated in space] D --> E[Substitute equation of state
Adiabatic p equals c squared rho] E --> F[Eliminate density in time] F --> G[Acoustic Wave Equation
d2p over dx2 equals 1 over c2 d2p over dt2] H[Equation of Motion
Newtons Second Law] --> C I[Equation of State
Thermodynamic Coupling] --> E ``` ## 4.2 Acoustic Wave Propagation Architecture The following block diagram shows how the wave equation connects a physical noise source to the receiver (the human ear in industrial noise context). ```mermaid flowchart LR S[Noise Source
Motor Fan Compressor] -->|Mechanical Vibration| EM[Excitation of Fluid Particles] EM -->|Pressure Fluctuation p x t| WE[Wave Equation Governs Propagation] WE -->|Forward and Backward Travelling Waves| MED[Air as Transmission Medium] MED -->|Distance r Attenuation 1 over r| REC[Receiver
Human Ear or Microphone] MED --> REF[Reflection at Boundaries
Walls Floors Barriers] MED --> ABS[Absorption in Medium
and at Surfaces] MED --> DIF[Diffraction at Edges] REF --> REC ABS --> REC DIF --> REC ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Acoustic Field ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph KINEMATICS [Kinematic Block] K1[Particle Displacement xi x t] K2[Particle Velocity u partial xi partial t] K3[Particle Acceleration a partial u partial t] K1 --> K2 --> K3 end subgraph DYNAMICS [Dynamic Block] D1[Acoustic Pressure p x t] D2[Equation of Motion rho zero du dt equals minus dp dx] D2 --> D1 end subgraph THERMO [Thermodynamic Block] T1[Equation of State p equals c squared rho] T2[Local Density Variation rho x t] T2 --> T1 end KINEMATICS --> DYNAMICS THERMO --> DYNAMICS DYNAMICS --> OUT[Solution p x t equals f x minus ct plus g x plus ct] ``` ## 4.4 Frequency-Wavelength Domain Map ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Time Domain Signal p t] -->|Fourier Transform| B[Frequency Domain P omega] B -->|omega equals 2 pi f| C[Wavenumber Domain k equals omega over c] C --> D[Spatial Variation of Pressure p x] E[Industrial Noise Examples] --> A E1[100 Hz Motor Hum lambda equals 3.43 m] --> E E2[1000 Hz Saw lambda equals 0.343 m] --> E E3[10000 Hz Whistle lambda equals 0.0343 m] --> E ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Each halving of wavelength corresponds to a doubling of frequency, which is why high-frequency industrial noise (e.g., ultrasonic leaks) is more easily absorbed or blocked by thin barriers than low-frequency rumble. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]** ### Q1. State and explain the acoustic wave equation in one dimension. Mention the physical significance of each term. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **one-dimensional acoustic wave equation** governing sound propagation in a homogeneous, lossless fluid medium is: $$\frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}}$$ - $\partial^2 p/\partial x^2$: spatial curvature of the pressure field — how rapidly pressure varies from one point to the next in space. - $\partial^2 p/\partial t^2$: temporal acceleration of the pressure variation — the rate of change of "rate of change" of pressure with time. - $1/c^2$: factor that converts time-rate of change into space-rate of change, where $c$ is the speed of sound in the medium. **Physical significance:** It shows that a local pressure disturbance propagates through the medium as a wave at speed $c$. The solution $p = f(x-ct) + g(x+ct)$ represents forward- and backward-travelling pressure waves. --- ### Q2. What is the characteristic acoustic impedance of a medium? State its value for air at 20 °C. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **characteristic (or specific) acoustic impedance** of a medium is defined as the product of the ambient density and the speed of sound: $$Z_{0} = \rho_{0} c$$ It has units of **Pa·s/m** (also called **rayl**, after Lord Rayleigh). Physically, it represents the resistance offered by the medium to the propagation of sound — the ratio of acoustic pressure to particle velocity in a plane progressive wave: $$Z_{0} = \frac{p}{u}$$ For air at 20 °C: $\rho_0 = 1.204$ kg/m³ and $c = 343$ m/s, giving: $$Z_{0} = 1.204 \times 343 \approx 413 \text{ Pa·s/m}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part A:** > A common 1-mark deduction is *writing the unit as "rayl" without the numerical expansion, or quoting the wrong reference (e.g., $\rho_0 c^2$)*. Also, students often confuse acoustic impedance with electrical impedance; the dB form is a logarithmic ratio, not the impedance itself. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]** > *Each 14-mark question consists of two 7-mark sub-parts.* --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### (a) Derive the one-dimensional acoustic wave equation starting from the equations of continuity, motion, and state. State all assumptions clearly. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. The medium is **homogeneous** ($\rho_0$ is constant in space). 2. The medium is **lossless** (no viscosity, no thermal conduction). 3. Acoustic disturbances are **small** ($p \ll P_0$, $u \ll c$), allowing linearisation. 4. Process is **adiabatic** (acoustic frequency is high enough that no heat flows between adjacent fluid elements). 5. One-dimensional propagation along the $x$-axis. **Governing equations (1 mark):** $$\text{(i) Continuity:} \quad \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \rho_{0} \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0$$ $$\text{(ii) Motion:} \quad \rho_{0} \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$ $$\text{(iii) State:} \quad \frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = c^{2} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$$ **Derivation (5 marks):** Differentiate (i) w.r.t. $t$: $$\frac{\partial^{2} \rho}{\partial t^{2}} + \rho_{0} \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x \partial t} = 0$$ Substitute (ii) into the above: $$\frac{\partial^{2} \rho}{\partial t^{2}} + \rho_{0} \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}\right) = \frac{\partial^{2} \rho}{\partial t^{2}} + \rho_{0} \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(-\frac{1}{\rho_{0}}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) = 0$$ $$\Rightarrow \frac{\partial^{2} \rho}{\partial t^{2}} - \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial x^{2}} = 0 \quad \text{...(A)}$$ From (iii), differentiate w.r.t. $t$: $$\frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}} = c^{2} \frac{\partial^{2} \rho}{\partial t^{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial^{2} \rho}{\partial t^{2}} = \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}} \quad \text{...(B)}$$ Substitute (B) into (A): $$\frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}} - \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial x^{2}} = 0$$ Rearranging: $$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}}\;}$$ This is the **one-dimensional acoustic wave equation** ✓ #### (b) For a plane harmonic wave $p(x,t) = P_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$ in air at 20 °C, calculate: (i) the wavelength when $f = 500$ Hz, and (ii) the acoustic intensity if $P_0 = 1$ Pa. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Given:** $f = 500$ Hz, $P_0 = 1$ Pa, $c = 343$ m/s, $\rho_0 = 1.204$ kg/m³, $p_{ref} = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa. **(i) Wavelength (3 marks):** Using $\lambda = c/f$: $$\lambda = \frac{343}{500} = 0.686 \text{ m}$$ **(ii) Acoustic intensity (4 marks):** For a plane harmonic wave, the RMS pressure is: $$p_{rms} = \frac{P_{0}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 \text{ Pa}$$ The acoustic intensity is: $$I = \frac{p_{rms}^{2}}{\rho_{0} c} = \frac{(0.7071)^{2}}{1.204 \times 343} = \frac{0.5}{412.97} \approx 1.211 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W/m}^{2}$$ $$\boxed{\;I \approx 1.21 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W/m}^{2} = 1.21 \text{ mW/m}^{2}\;}$$ Corresponding Sound Pressure Level: $$L_{p} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.7071}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(35355) \approx 90.97 \text{ dB} \approx 91 \text{ dB}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:** > 1. **Forgetting the linearisation assumption** (small-amplitude, adiabatic) — usually costs 1 mark under "assumptions." > 2. **Using $P_0$ directly** in the intensity formula instead of $p_{rms} = P_0/\sqrt{2}$ — silent killer of 2 marks. > 3. **Mixing up** $I$ (intensity) and $L_I$ (intensity level) — they are *not* the same; $L_I$ is in decibels. > 4. **Wrong speed of sound** — KTU may specify temperature; use $c \approx 331 + 0.6T$ for air in °C. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** #### (a) With the help of D'Alembert's solution, explain the physical meaning of forward-travelling and backward-travelling waves. Show that both individually satisfy the wave equation. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **D'Alembert's general solution (2 marks):** The general solution to the 1-D wave equation is: $$p(x,t) = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct)$$ where $f$ and $g$ are twice-differentiable functions determined by boundary and initial conditions. **Verification that $f(x-ct)$ satisfies the wave equation (3 marks):** Let $\xi = x - ct$. Then: $$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = f'(\xi), \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial t} = -c\,f'(\xi)$$ Second derivatives: $$\frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x^{2}} = f''(\xi), \quad \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial t^{2}} = c^{2} f''(\xi)$$ Substituting: $$\frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x^{2}} = f''(\xi) = \frac{1}{c^{2}}\bigl(c^{2} f''(\xi)\bigr) = \frac{1}{c^{2}} \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial t^{2}} \;\checkmark$$ By the same substitution (with sign reversal of $c$ squared producing the same result), $g(x+ct)$ also satisfies the wave equation. By linearity, their sum does too. **Physical meaning (2 marks):** - **$f(x - ct)$**: a wave shape moving in the **+x direction** at speed $c$. A particular pressure crest (say, $f = 1$ Pa) occurs when $x - ct = \text{const}$, i.e. $x = ct + \text{const}$, so the crest's $x$-position increases linearly with time. - **$g(x + ct)$**: a wave shape moving in the **−x direction** at speed $c$. The crest satisfies $x + ct = \text{const}$, i.e. $x = -ct + \text{const}$, so it moves toward decreasing $x$. In a duct with a reflecting termination, the forward wave is the **incident** wave from the source; the backward wave is the **reflected** wave from the closed end. #### (b) A point source radiates sound equally in all directions in free air. The sound pressure level is 80 dB at a distance of 5 m from the source. Calculate the sound pressure level at 20 m. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** For a point source in free field, intensity follows the **inverse-square law**: $$I \propto \frac{1}{r^{2}}$$ In decibels, this becomes a level change of: $$\Delta L = L_{1} - L_{2} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)^{2} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\right)$$ With $r_1 = 5$ m, $L_1 = 80$ dB, $r_2 = 20$ m: $$\Delta L = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{20}{5}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(4) = 20 \times 0.6021 \approx 12.04 \text{ dB}$$ Therefore: $$L_{2} = L_{1} - \Delta L = 80 - 12.04 = 67.96 \text{ dB} \approx 68 \text{ dB}$$ $$\boxed{\;L_{p}(20 \text{ m}) \approx 68 \text{ dB}\;}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:** > 1. **Using $10 \log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ instead of $20 \log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$** — a classic 2-mark blunder. The factor is 20 because doubling distance reduces intensity by a factor of 4 (i.e. 6 dB), not 3 dB. > 2. **Adding instead of subtracting** the level change — remember, doubling distance *reduces* the level. > 3. **Forgetting that this is free-field (no reflections)** — KTU may deduct a mark if the answer assumes a reverberant field. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] The **acoustic wave equation** $\partial^2 p/\partial x^2 = (1/c^2)\partial^2 p/\partial t^2$ is the cornerstone of noise and vibration analysis. - [x] It is **derived**, not assumed, by combining three equations: **continuity**, **motion (Euler)**, and **state (adiabatic)**. - [x] The **speed of sound** in air at 20 °C is **c = 343 m/s**; in general, $c = \sqrt{\gamma P_0/\rho_0}$. - [x] The **equation of state** is $p = c^2 \rho$, **not** $p = c \rho$ (units check!). - [x] **D'Alembert's solution** is $p = f(x-ct) + g(x+ct)$, representing **forward and backward travelling waves**. - [x] The **characteristic acoustic impedance** is $Z_0 = \rho_0 c \approx 413$ rayl in air; it is central to reflection/transmission analysis. - [x] For **time-harmonic** excitation, the spatial equation becomes the **Helmholtz equation**: $\partial^2 \hat{P}/\partial x^2 + k^2 \hat{P} = 0$, where $k = \omega/c$. - [x] **Wavelength**: $\lambda = c/f$. Halving the frequency doubles the wavelength. - [x] **Acoustic intensity** of a plane wave: $I = p_{rms}^2/(\rho_0 c)$. Always use RMS, not peak pressure. - [x] **Doubling the distance** from a point source reduces SPL by **6 dB** (free field). - [x] **Key assumption set**: homogeneous, lossless, small-amplitude (linear), adiabatic, 1-D propagation. State these at the start of any derivation question. - [x] The wave equation in **3-D** is $\nabla^2 p = (1/c^2)\partial^2 p/\partial t^2$, with spherical-wave solutions decaying as $1/r$.

topics.title: Theory of Sound Propagation and Terminology Involved
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT632 - INDUSTRIAL NOISE AND VIBRATION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Theory of Sound Propagation and Terminology Involved # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Sound? (Academic Definition) **Sound** is defined as a *mechanical longitudinal wave* that propagates through an elastic medium (solid, liquid, or gas) by means of cyclic compression and rarefaction of the constituent particles, transporting energy from a source to a receiver without permanent displacement of the medium itself. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Sound is a *pressure fluctuation* about the ambient static pressure, audible to the human ear roughly in the frequency range **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz** (1 Octave = doubling of frequency). Frequencies below 20 Hz are termed *infrasound* and above 20 kHz are *ultrasound*. Mathematically, a one-dimensional sound wave is expressed as: $$p(x, t) = p_{0}\,\cos(\omega t - kx)$$ where: - $p_{0}$ = acoustic pressure amplitude (Pa) - $\omega$ = angular frequency (rad/s) = $2\pi f$ - $k$ = wave number (rad/m) = $\omega / c$ - $c$ = speed of sound in the medium (m/s) --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Domino Effect" of Air Molecules Imagine a row of dominoes standing in a line. If you push the **first** domino, it falls onto the **second**, which falls onto the **third**, and so on. Crucially: - The dominoes themselves **do not travel** with the wave. - **Energy** and a *disturbance pattern* travel from one end to the other. This is exactly how sound propagates through air. A vibrating speaker cone pushes the adjacent air molecules, which in turn push the next layer, creating alternating regions of **compression** (high pressure) and **rarefaction** (low pressure). > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Industrial Analogy:** In a factory, an impact hammer striking a metal plate produces a sharp *click*. The plate vibrates, transferring energy to surrounding air, which then propagates as a pressure wave to a worker's ear 5 metres away. The air molecules near the worker vibrate back and forth by only a few micrometres, but the *pressure disturbance* has travelled the full distance. --- ## 1.3 Fundamental Sound Quantities — The "Big Five" | Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Definition | |---|---|---|---| | Sound Pressure | $p$ | Pascal (Pa) | Instantaneous pressure deviation from ambient | | Sound Pressure Level | $L_{p}$ | decibel (dB) | Logarithmic measure of $p$ relative to reference $p_{0} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ | | Sound Intensity | $I$ | W/m² | Sound power transmitted per unit area | | Sound Power | $W$ | Watt (W) | Total acoustic energy radiated per second | | Frequency | $f$ | Hertz (Hz) | Number of pressure cycles per second | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Golden Constant of Acoustics:** The reference sound pressure is **$p_{ref} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{Pa}$**. This is the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz. The reference sound intensity is **$I_{ref} = 10^{-12}\,\text{W/m}^2$**. --- ## 1.4 Why Do We Use the Decibel (dB)? The human ear responds to sound over an enormous range — from the threshold of hearing ($10^{-12}$ W/m²) to the threshold of pain ($10$ W/m²), a ratio of **$10^{13}$ : 1**.
