Using linear units on a graph would be impractical. **Alexander Graham Bell's solution:** Use a *logarithmic* scale that compresses this enormous range into a manageable 0–140 dB scale. $$L = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{X}{X_{ref}}\right)\;\;\text{dB}$$ For *intensity-like* quantities (power, intensity, energy): $$L = 10\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{X}{X_{ref}}\right)$$ For *pressure-like* quantities (pressure, voltage, displacement): $$L = 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{X}{X_{ref}}\right)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why 20 instead of 10 for pressure?** Because acoustic intensity is proportional to the *square* of pressure ($I \propto p^2$), and $10\,\log(p^2) = 20\,\log(p)$. --- ## 1.5 The Four Modes of Sound Propagation Sound does not just travel in straight lines. It interacts with its environment through four fundamental mechanisms: 1. **Reflection** — Sound bouncing off hard surfaces (walls, floors). 2. **Refraction** — Bending of sound due to a gradient in the speed of sound (e.g., wind or temperature gradients). 3. **Diffraction** — Bending of sound around obstacles or through openings (very pronounced at low frequencies). 4. **Absorption** — Conversion of acoustic energy into heat within a porous or resonant material. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Sound Wave A sound wave is fully described by **four primary parameters**: ### (a) Frequency ($f$) - Number of complete pressure oscillations per second. - $f = 1/T$ where $T$ is the period (seconds). - Audible band: **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz**. ### (b) Wavelength ($\lambda$) - Spatial distance over which one complete cycle occurs. - $\lambda = c / f$ - For $c = 343$ m/s (air at 20 °C): - At 100 Hz → $\lambda = 3.43$ m - At 1000 Hz → $\lambda = 0.343$ m - At 10,000 Hz → $\lambda = 0.0343$ m ### (c) Speed of Sound ($c$) - Depends on the **medium** and its **temperature**. - In an ideal gas: $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ where $\gamma$ is the ratio of specific heats, $R$ is the gas constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature. - In air at 20 °C: **$c = 343$ m/s**. - In water at 20 °C: **$c \approx 1480$ m/s**. - In steel: **$c \approx 5000$ m/s**. ### (d) Amplitude ($p_0$ or $u_0$) - The maximum deviation from the mean (for pressure or particle velocity). - Determines *loudness*. --- ## 2.2 Speed of Sound — Detailed Derivation For an ideal gas, the speed of sound is: $$c = \sqrt{\gamma\,R\,T}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For air, the speed of sound (m/s) can be approximated as: > $$c \approx 331 + 0.6\,T_{C}$$ > where $T_{C}$ is the temperature in °C. This linear approximation is accurate to within ±0.5 m/s for typical engineering calculations between -20 °C and +40 °C. --- ## 2.3 Acoustic Wave Equation (1-D Plane Wave) Starting from the conservation of mass, momentum, and the equation of state for an inviscid fluid, the **1-D acoustic wave equation** is: $$\frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{1}{c^{2}}\,\frac{\partial^{2} p}{\partial t^{2}}$$ The general solution is: $$p(x, t) = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct)$$ - $f(x - ct)$ → wave travelling in the **+x** direction. - $g(x + ct)$ → wave travelling in the **-x** direction. --- ## 2.4 Acoustic Impedance ($Z$) **Acoustic impedance** is the product of density and speed of sound in a medium: $$Z = \rho\,c \quad (\text{rayl or Pa·s/m})$$ For air at 20 °C: $\rho = 1.21$ kg/m³, so $Z \approx 413$ rayl. For water at 20 °C: $\rho = 998$ kg/m³, so $Z \approx 1.48 \times 10^{6}$ rayl. > [!NOTE] > **Why is impedance important?** The mismatch in impedance at a boundary determines how much sound is reflected vs. transmitted. A large mismatch (e.g., air-to-water) reflects almost all sound; a matched impedance transmits it fully. --- ## 2.5 Sound Field Geometries | Field Type | Wavefront Shape | Intensity Decay | Equation | |---|---|---|---| | Plane wave | Flat parallel surfaces | No decay (ideal) | $I = \text{const}$ | | Spherical wave | Expanding spheres from a point | $I \propto 1/r^{2}$ | $I = W / (4\pi r^{2})$ | | Cylindrical wave | Expanding cylinders from a line | $I \propto 1/r$ | $I = W / (2\pi r L)$ | Where: - $W$ = acoustic power of the source (W) - $r$ = distance from source (m) - $L$ = length of the line source (m) --- ## 2.6 Inverse Square Law (Free Field) For a point source radiating uniformly in all directions (spherical wave): $$L_{p}(r) = L_{p}(r_{0}) - 20\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r}{r_{0}}\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Result:** In a *free field*, every **doubling of distance** from a point source reduces the sound pressure level by **6 dB**. For a line source (cylindrical wave): $$L_{p}(r) = L_{p}(r_{0}) - 10\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r}{r_{0}}\right)$$ > Every doubling of distance from a line source reduces SPL by **3 dB**. --- ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $L_{p} = 20\log_{10}(p/p_{ref})$ | Sound Pressure Level (dB re 20 µPa) | dB | | 2 | $L_{I} = 10\log_{10}(I/I_{ref})$ | Sound Intensity Level (dB re 10⁻¹² W/m²) | dB | | 3 | $L_{W} = 10\log_{10}(W/W_{ref})$ | Sound Power Level (dB re 10⁻¹² W) | dB | | 4 | $L_{I} = L_{p}$ (plane wave) | Intensity-pressure relation (in dB) | dB | | 5 | $L_{p}(r) = L_{W} - 20\log_{10}(r) - 11$ | Point source SPL at distance $r$ | dB | | 6 | $L_{p}(r) = L_{W} - 10\log_{10}(r) - 8$ | Line source SPL at distance $r$ | dB | | 7 | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | Speed of sound in ideal gas | m/s | | 8 | $\lambda = c/f$ | Wavelength | m | | 9 | $k = 2\pi/\lambda = \omega/c$ | Wave number | rad/m | | 10 | $Z = \rho c$ | Characteristic acoustic impedance | rayl | | 11 | $I = p_{rms}^{2} / (\rho c)$ | Intensity from RMS pressure | W/m² | | 12 | $I = W / (4\pi r^{2})$ | Point source intensity | W/m² | | 13 | $L_{p1} - L_{p2} = 20\log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ | Inverse square law | dB | | 14 | $L_{tot} = 10\log_{10}\sum 10^{L_i/10}$ | dB addition of multiple sources | dB | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Real-World Use:** These formulas form the backbone of every industrial noise survey. An acoustical engineer walks around a noisy machine with a sound level meter, applies Equations 5 and 6 to back-calculate the **sound power level** ($L_W$) of the machine, which is an *intrinsic* property of the machine independent of the room. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Speed of Sound in an Ideal Gas We begin with Newton's second law applied to a small fluid element undergoing an adiabatic compression: ### Step 1 — Starting Assumption For a small acoustic disturbance, the process is **adiabatic** (no heat transfer between adjacent fluid layers because the timescale is much shorter than thermal diffusion time). The equation of state for an adiabatic process is: $$p\,V^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$$ Differentiating: $$dp = -\gamma\,\frac{p}{V}\,dV = \gamma\,\frac{p}{\rho}\,d\rho$$ So the **bulk modulus** is: $$B = \gamma\,p$$ ### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law to a Fluid Slab A slab of fluid of thickness $dx$, cross-section $A$, and density $\rho$ experiences a net force $A\,dp$ when its right face is displaced by $\xi$ and the left face by $\xi + d\xi$. By Newton's law: $$\rho\,A\,dx\,\frac{\partial^{2}\xi}{\partial t^{2}} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\,A\,dx$$ Dividing by $A\,dx$ and using the linearised equation of state $\partial p / \partial x = -B\,\partial^{2}\xi / \partial x^{2}$: $$\rho\,\frac{\partial^{2}\xi}{\partial t^{2}} = B\,\frac{\partial^{2}\xi}{\partial x^{2}}$$ ### Step 3 — Identify the Wave Speed Comparing with the standard 1-D wave equation $\partial^{2}\xi/\partial t^{2} = c^{2}\,\partial^{2}\xi/\partial x^{2}$: $$c^{2} = \frac{B}{\rho} = \frac{\gamma p}{\rho}$$ ### Step 4 — Use Ideal Gas Law For an ideal gas, $p = \rho R T$ (per unit mass, $R$ is the specific gas constant). Substituting: $$c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$$ This is the final result. ### Step 5 — Numerical Evaluation in Air at 20 °C (293 K) For air: $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K), $T = 293$ K. $$c = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 293}$$ $$c = \sqrt{117{,}763.4} \approx 343.16\;\text{m/s}$$ This matches the textbook value of **343 m/s**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Inverse Square Law for a Point Source ### Step 1 — Power Conservation A point source radiates total acoustic power $W$ (W). In a *free field* (no reflections), this power is distributed over the surface of an expanding sphere of radius $r$. Surface area of a sphere: $$S = 4\pi r^{2}$$ ### Step 2 — Intensity at Distance $r$ By definition, intensity is power per unit area: $$I = \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}$$ ### Step 3 — Express SPL at $r$ Using $I = p_{rms}^{2}/(\rho c)$ and the decibel definition: $$L_{p} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{ref}}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}\,I_{ref}}\right)$$ ### Step 4 — Compare SPL at Two Distances Let $L_{p1}$ be the SPL at $r_1$ and $L_{p2}$ at $r_2$: $$L_{p1} - L_{p2} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2^{2}}{r_1^{2}}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$ Or, equivalently: $$L_{p}(r) = L_{p}(r_{0}) - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r}{r_{0}}\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Doubled-Distance Rule (KTU Favourite):** Putting $r = 2 r_{0}$: > $$\Delta L_{p} = -20\log_{10}(2) \approx -6\;\text{dB}$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example: SPL in a Foundry **Problem:** A forging hammer in a foundry has a sound power level of $L_W = 110$ dB re 10⁻¹² W. Calculate the SPL at a distance of (a) 5 m, (b) 10 m, and (c) 20 m. Assume free-field conditions. **Given:** $L_W = 110$ dB, $r_1 = 5$ m, $r_2 = 10$ m, $r_3 = 20$ m. ### Solution **Part (a):** Use Equation 5 from the formula sheet. $$L_{p}(5) = L_{W} - 20\log_{10}(5) - 11$$ $$L_{p}(5) = 110 - 20(0.699) - 11 = 110 - 13.98 - 11 = 85.02\;\text{dB}$$ **Part (b):** At $r = 10$ m: $$L_{p}(10) = 110 - 20\log_{10}(10) - 11 = 110 - 20 - 11 = 79\;\text{dB}$$ **Part (c):** At $r = 20$ m: $$L_{p}(20) = 110 - 20\log_{10}(20) - 11 = 110 - 26.02 - 11 = 72.98\;\text{dB}$$ ### Verification using Inverse-Square Rule From 5 m to 10 m: distance doubled → SPL should drop by ~6 dB. $85.02 - 79 = 6.02$ dB ✓ From 10 m to 20 m: distance doubled → SPL should drop by ~6 dB. $79 - 72.98 = 6.02$ dB ✓ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The 6 dB-per-doubling rule is one of the most tested relationships in KTU noise control papers. Always verify your final answer against this sanity check. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example: Adding Two Incoherent Sound Sources **Problem:** Two machines operate simultaneously in a workshop. Machine A produces 85 dB and Machine B produces 82 dB at the operator's position. What is the combined SPL? **Step 1 — Convert dB to linear intensity ratios** $$10^{L_A/10} = 10^{85/10} = 10^{8.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{8}$$ $$10^{L_B/10} = 10^{82/10} = 10^{8.2} = 1.585 \times 10^{8}$$ **Step 2 — Sum the linear values** $$I_{tot} = 3.162 \times 10^{8} + 1.585 \times 10^{8} = 4.747 \times 10^{8}$$ **Step 3 — Convert back to dB** $$L_{tot} = 10\log_{10}(4.747 \times 10^{8}) = 10(8.676) = 86.77\;\text{dB}$$ ### Shortcut Method Difference $\Delta L = 85 - 82 = 3$ dB. From KTU tables, "add 3 dB" → add **1.76 dB** to the louder level. $L_{tot} = 85 + 1.76 = 86.76$ dB ✓ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule of Thumb:** > - Two identical sources → +3 dB. > - Difference $\geq$ 10 dB → quieter source is negligible, total ≈ louder source. > - Difference = 0 dB → +3 dB. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Sound Propagation The diagram below models the entire signal-flow chain of an industrial noise event — from the physical source, through the propagation medium, to the receiver (human ear or microphone). This is the "block diagram" view that KTU examiners often ask you to draw and label. ```mermaid flowchart LR srcA[Source Block
Mechanical Vibration] srcB[Source Block
Impact or Friction] srcC[Source Block
Fluid Flow Turbulence] sourceMix[Composite Source
Sound Power W] nearField[Near Field Zone
Reactive Region r less than 2 lambda] farField[Far Field Zone
Radiative Region r greater than 2 lambda] medium[Propagation Medium
Air with rho and c] refl[Reflection
From Rigid Walls] absb[Absorption
Porous Materials] refr[Refraction
Wind and Temperature Gradient] diff[Diffraction
Around Obstacles] envMix[Effective Field
Direct plus Reflected] rxMic[Receiver Microphone
SPL Lp] rxHum[Receiver Human Ear
Perceived Loudness] rxInst[Receiver Instrument
Damage Threshold] srcA --> sourceMix srcB --> sourceMix srcC --> sourceMix sourceMix --> nearField nearField --> farField farField --> medium medium --> refl medium --> absb medium --> refr medium --> diff refl --> envMix absb --> envMix refr --> envMix diff --> envMix envMix --> rxMic envMix --> rxHum envMix --> rxInst classDef sourceBlock fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#ff8c00,color:#000 classDef fieldBlock fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 classDef propBlock fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 classDef rxBlock fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 class srcA,srcB,srcC,sourceMix sourceBlock class nearField,farField,medium fieldBlock class refl,absb,refr,diff,envMix propBlock class rxMic,rxHum,rxInst rxBlock ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The orange blocks are *sources*, blue blocks are the *acoustic field*, green blocks are *propagation phenomena*, and pink blocks are *receivers*. Every industrial noise problem can be mapped onto this architecture. --- ## 4.2 Sound Field Geometry Comparison This diagram contrasts the three fundamental wave geometries, showing how wavefronts spread and intensity decays with distance. ```mermaid flowchart TB plane["Plane Wave
Intensity I constant
No decay
Example: Inside a long duct"] sphr["Spherical Wave
Intensity I proportional to 1 over r squared
6 dB drop per doubling of distance
Example: Point source in free field"] cyl["Cylindrical Wave
Intensity I proportional to 1 over r
3 dB drop per doubling of distance
Example: Long busy highway"] plane --> cmp["Compare decay rates"] sphr --> cmp cyl --> cmp cmp --> out["At large r: spherical is the most aggressive attenuator"] classDef waveStyle fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000 class plane,sphr,cyl,out waveStyle class cmp waveStyle ``` --- ## 4.3 Decibel Scale Reference The decibel scale is logarithmic, and small numerical changes represent large physical changes. The figure below anchors common sound sources against the dB scale. ```mermaid flowchart LR a0["0 dB
Threshold of Hearing"] a20["20 dB
Whisper at 1 m"] a40["40 dB
Quiet Library"] a60["60 dB
Normal Conversation"] a80["80 dB
Heavy Traffic"] a85["85 dB
8 hour OSHA Exposure Limit"] a100["100 dB
Pneumatic Drill"] a120["120 dB
Jet Engine at 100 m
Threshold of Pain"] a140["140 dB
Instant Ear Damage"] a0 --> a20 --> a40 --> a60 --> a80 --> a85 --> a100 --> a120 --> a140 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Safety Note:** A 10 dB increase represents a *perceived* doubling of loudness but a **10× increase** in acoustic intensity. This is why industrial regulations focus on 85 dB as the 8-hour action limit. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define the following terms: (a) Sound Pressure Level, (b) Sound Power Level, (c) Sound Intensity Level. State their reference values.** #### Model Answer - **(a) Sound Pressure Level ($L_p$):** The logarithmic measure of RMS sound pressure relative to the reference pressure $p_{ref} = 20\;\mu\text{Pa}$. $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p_{rms}/p_{ref})$ dB. - **(b) Sound Power Level ($L_W$):** The logarithmic measure of acoustic power radiated by a source relative to $W_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W. $L_W = 10\log_{10}(W/W_{ref})$ dB. - **(c) Sound Intensity Level ($L_I$):** The logarithmic measure of sound intensity relative to $I_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W/m². $L_I = 10\log_{10}(I/I_{ref})$ dB. > *(3 marks: 1 mark each for the three definitions, 0.5 mark extra for each reference value.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **State and explain the inverse square law for a point sound source. Mention the change in SPL when distance is doubled.** #### Model Answer The **inverse square law** states that in a free field, the sound intensity $I$ from a point source varies inversely as the square of the distance $r$: $$I = \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}$$ In decibels, the SPL at a distance $r$ is: $$L_{p}(r) = L_{p}(r_{0}) - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r}{r_{0}}\right)$$ **Doubling the distance** ($r = 2 r_0$): $$\Delta L_{p} = -20\log_{10}(2) \approx -6\;\text{dB}$$ Hence, SPL reduces by **6 dB** for every doubling of distance from a point source. *(3 marks: 1.5 for law, 1.5 for doubling result.)* --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the speed of sound in an ideal gas, starting from the basic equation of state and Newton's second law. Calculate the speed of sound in air at 27 °C. (7 Marks) **(b)** A point source in a free field has a sound power level of 120 dB re 10⁻¹² W. Determine the SPL at distances of 2 m, 10 m, and 50 m. Verify your results using the doubling-distance rule. (7 Marks) #### Model Solution **(a) Derivation** *(Already shown in Section 3.1 of these notes.)* - Step 1: Adiabatic condition $pV^\gamma = \text{const}$ → bulk modulus $B = \gamma p$. - Step 2: Newton's second law on fluid element → $\rho \ddot{\xi} = B \xi''$. - Step 3: Compare with wave equation → $c = \sqrt{B/\rho} = \sqrt{\gamma p/\rho}$. - Step 4: Use ideal gas law $p = \rho R T$ → $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$. **Numerical at 27 °C (300 K):** $$c = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 300} = \sqrt{120{,}540} \approx 347.2\;\text{m/s}$$ *(1 mark for each step + 1 mark for numerical.)* **(b) SPL Calculations** Use $L_p(r) = L_W - 20\log_{10}(r) - 11$: - At $r = 2$ m: $L_p = 120 - 20\log_{10}(2) - 11 = 120 - 6.02 - 11 = 102.98 \approx 103$ dB. - At $r = 10$ m: $L_p = 120 - 20\log_{10}(10) - 11 = 120 - 20 - 11 = 89$ dB. - At $r = 50$ m: $L_p = 120 - 20\log_{10}(50) - 11 = 120 - 33.98 - 11 = 75.02 \approx 75$ dB. **Verification (2 m → 10 m → 50 m):** - 2 m to 10 m: distance factor 5 → $\Delta L_p = -20\log_{10}(5) = -13.98 \approx -14$ dB. From 103 to 89 → drop of 14 dB ✓. - 10 m to 50 m: distance factor 5 → drop of 14 dB. From 89 to 75 → drop of 14 dB ✓. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write $L_p = L_W - 20\log_{10}(r) - 11$ but forget the **$-11$** constant. It comes from $10\log_{10}(4\pi) \approx 11$. **Always include it for a point source.** Forgetting it costs **2 marks**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the four basic mechanisms by which sound propagates and interacts with its surroundings: reflection, refraction, diffraction, and absorption. (7 Marks) **(b)** Three identical machines, each producing 80 dB SPL, operate simultaneously at a workstation. What is the combined SPL? If one machine is switched off, what is the new SPL? (7 Marks) #### Model Solution **(a) Four Propagation Mechanisms** *(1.75 marks each)* 1. **Reflection:** When a sound wave strikes a hard, rigid surface (impedance mismatch), a portion of its energy is bounced back. Examples: industrial walls, ceilings, hard floors. 2. **Refraction:** Sound bends when it passes through a medium where the speed of sound changes continuously (e.g., temperature gradient in air, wind shear). Snell's law analogue: $\sin\theta_1 / c_1 = \sin\theta_2 / c_2$. 3. **Diffraction:** Sound bends around obstacles or through openings. Most pronounced when obstacle size $<$ wavelength (low frequencies diffract more easily). This is why you hear bass through walls but not treble. 4. **Absorption:** Sound energy is converted to heat in porous materials (fibreglass, mineral wool) or resonant panels. Characterised by the **absorption coefficient** $\alpha$, ranging from 0 (perfect reflector) to 1 (perfect absorber). *(Neat sketches expected: 0.5 mark reserved for diagrams.)* **(b) Addition of Sound Sources** **Three machines, each 80 dB:** Convert to linear intensities: $I_0 \propto 10^{80/10} = 10^8$. Total: $3 \times 10^8$. $$L_{tot} = 10\log_{10}(3 \times 10^{8}) = 10(8.477) = 84.77 \approx 84.8\;\text{dB}$$ **After switching off one machine (two remaining at 80 dB):** $$L_{tot} = 10\log_{10}(2 \times 10^{8}) = 10(8.301) = 83.01 \approx 83\;\text{dB}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common student error is to *linearly add* the dB values (80 + 80 + 80 = 240 dB). The dB scale is **logarithmic**, not linear. You must convert to intensities, add, then convert back. This error alone can cost **3 marks**. Another trap: forgetting that *incoherent* sources (different machines) add on a *power* basis, not a pressure basis. Use $10\log$, not $20\log$. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** - **Sound** is a *longitudinal mechanical pressure wave* in the range 20 Hz – 20 kHz. - The **wave equation** $\partial^2 p/\partial x^2 = (1/c^2)\,\partial^2 p/\partial t^2$ governs all acoustic propagation. - **Speed of sound** in air at 20 °C = **343 m/s**; in water ≈ **1480 m/s**; in steel ≈ **5000 m/s**. - **Reference values:** $p_{ref} = 20\;\mu\text{Pa}$, $I_{ref} = 10^{-12}\;\text{W/m}^2$, $W_{ref} = 10^{-12}\;\text{W}$. - **SPL formula:** $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p/p_{ref})$ dB. **SWL:** $L_W = 10\log_{10}(W/W_{ref})$ dB. - **6 dB rule:** SPL drops by **6 dB** for every doubling of distance from a *point* source. - **3 dB rule:** SPL drops by **3 dB** for every doubling of distance from a *line* source. - **3 dB addition:** Two identical incoherent sources add to give **+3 dB** above the single-source level. - **Inverse square law:** $I \propto 1/r^2$ for a point source; **cylindrical:** $I \propto 1/r$. - **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho c$. Mismatch causes reflection; matching causes transmission. - **Wavelength:** $\lambda = c/f$. Low frequencies have long wavelengths → diffract easily; high frequencies are easily blocked. - **Propagation mechanisms:** *Reflection* (hard surfaces), *Refraction* (speed gradient), *Diffraction* (around obstacles), *Absorption* (energy → heat). - **Near field vs. far field:** Far field begins at $r > 2\lambda$ (or $r > 3D$ for a source of diameter $D$). - **Plane wave relation:** $L_p = L_I$ in dB (only for plane waves). - **Speed of sound in air approximation:** $c \approx 331 + 0.6\,T_C$ m/s. - **Always sanity-check** your dB answers using the doubling-distance rule. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 scheme typically allocates 60% of marks to Part A and 40% to Part B. Master the derivations of *speed of sound* and *inverse square law* — they are the two most-repeated derivations in this module.

topics.title: Plane Wave and Spherical Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT632 - INDUSTRIAL NOISE AND VIBRATION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Plane Wave and Spherical Waves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Acoustic Wave — Foundational Definition

An **acoustic wave** is a mechanical disturbance that propagates through an elastic medium (gas, liquid, or solid) by means of particle-to-particle interaction, transferring energy from one location to another without permanent displacement of the medium itself. In the context of **Industrial Noise and Vibration Control**, the *acoustic variable* of primary interest is the **sound pressure**, denoted $p(x,y,z,t)$, which is the small fluctuation of static atmospheric pressure produced by the source.

The propagation of sound is governed by the three governing physical principles of acoustics:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Foundational Pillars of Acoustic Wave Propagation**
> 1. **Equation of Continuity** — Conservation of mass
> 2. **Equation of Motion (Euler's Equation)** — Newton's second law applied to fluid particles
> 3. **Equation of State (Isentropic Process)** — Thermodynamic relation $p = c^2 \rho$

These three principles, when linearised and combined, yield the classical **three-dimensional wave equation**:

$$
\nabla^2 p = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}
$$

where $c$ is the **speed of sound** in the medium. For air at $20^\circ\text{C}$, $c \approx \mathbf{343 \text{ m/s}}$.

## 1.2 Plane Wave — The Formal Definition

A **plane wave** is an acoustic disturbance in which the wavefronts (surfaces of constant phase) are **infinite parallel planes** oriented perpendicular to the direction of propagation. The sound pressure in a plane wave is a function of *only one* spatial coordinate (say $x$) and time $t$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plane Wave — KTU Definition**
> "An acoustic wave whose wavefronts are plane surfaces and whose properties are uniform over any plane perpendicular to the direction of propagation. The amplitude remains constant with distance from the source."

### 1.2.1 Real-World Analogy (Plane Wave)

Imagine a **long, perfectly straight train of carriages** moving uniformly on a perfectly straight, infinitely long track. Every carriage moves identically — there is no spreading, no attenuation with distance, and no curvature of the "wavefront." This is the essence of a plane wave. In practice, a plane wave is an *idealisation* that is approached in:
- The interior of a long, smooth, straight duct
- At a sufficient distance from a very large vibrating surface (e.g., a wall)
- In the **far field** along the central axis of a directional source

## 1.3 Spherical Wave — The Formal Definition

A **spherical wave** is an acoustic disturbance radiated from a *point source* such that the wavefronts are **concentric spheres** expanding outward at the speed of sound. The sound pressure in a spherical wave is a function of the radial distance $r$ from the source and time $t$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Spherical Wave — KTU Definition**
> "An acoustic wave whose wavefronts are spherical surfaces centred on the source, with amplitude decreasing inversely with distance (for pressure) and inversely with the square of distance (for intensity)."

### 1.3.1 Real-World Analogy (Spherical Wave)

Drop a **pebble into a still pond**. The ripples expand outward as ever-widening circular rings. The same phenomenon happens in 3D in air when a small machine (motor, valve, siren) vibrates. The total acoustic power is "spread" over a sphere of ever-growing area $4\pi r^2$, so the intensity falls off as $1/r^2$ — the famous **inverse square law**.

## 1.4 Why Both Waves Matter in Industrial Noise Control

| Wave Type | Engineering Scenario |
|---|---|
| **Plane Wave** | HVAC duct design, silencer sizing, impedance tube measurements, room acoustics at grazing incidence |
| **Spherical Wave** | Outdoor noise propagation from factories, sound power measurements, environmental impact assessment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight (2024 Scheme):**
> Questions in Module 1 typically test the *physical distinction* between these two wave types, the *form of the solution*, the *intensity relationships*, and the *validity of the inverse square law*. Always state assumptions clearly — a plane wave is *non-physical* in free field, but a *valid approximation* in ducts and far fields.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Dimensional Wave Equation (Starting Point)

Combining the linearised continuity equation, Euler's equation of motion, and the adiabatic equation of state yields:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial z^2} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}
$$

This is the **homogeneous scalar wave equation** for sound pressure. The geometry of the problem (plane, spherical, cylindrical) dictates the coordinate system and, consequently, the form of the solution.

## 2.2 Plane Wave — Theory of Operation

### 2.2.1 The 1D Wave Equation
For a plane wave propagating along the $x$-axis, the wave equation reduces to:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}
$$

### 2.2.2 The General Solution (d'Alembert's Solution)

The general solution is a linear combination of two progressive waves travelling in opposite directions:

$$
p(x,t) = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct)
$$

**Why this form?** Substituting $p = f(x-ct)$ into the 1D wave equation gives:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial \xi^2} = \frac{1}{c^2} c^2 \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial \xi^2} \quad \text{where } \xi = x - ct
$$

Both terms are identical, confirming $f(x-ct)$ is a valid solution. The same logic applies to $g(x+ct)$.

### 2.2.3 The Harmonic (Time-Periodic) Solution

For a *monochromatic* (single-frequency) plane wave, the **phasor form** is:

$$
p(x,t) = \text{Re}\left\{ \hat{p}\, e^{j(\omega t - kx)} \right\}
$$

where:
- $\hat{p}$ = complex pressure amplitude (Pa)
- $\omega$ = angular frequency (rad/s)
- $k = \omega / c$ = acoustic wavenumber (rad/m)
- $j = \sqrt{-1}$

### 2.2.4 Particle Velocity and Acoustic Impedance

The particle velocity in the $x$-direction is obtained from Euler's linearised equation:

$$
\rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}
$$

For a harmonic wave, $\partial/\partial t \to j\omega$, so:

$$
u(x,t) = \frac{1}{\rho_0 c}\, p(x,t)
$$

The **specific acoustic impedance** of the medium for a plane progressive wave is therefore:

$$
Z = \frac{p}{u} = \rho_0 c
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** $\rho_0 c$ is the *characteristic impedance* of the medium. For air at STP, $\rho_0 c \approx \mathbf{413 \text{ kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}}$ (often called *rayls*). This is the *single most important* property in industrial noise control because it determines reflection, transmission, and absorption at boundaries.

## 2.3 Spherical Wave — Theory of Operation

### 2.3.1 Wave Equation in Spherical Coordinates

For a source at the origin with spherical symmetry, the Laplacian reduces to a single radial term:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}
$$

This can be rewritten by substituting $p = (1/r)\,\psi(r,t)$ to obtain a *1D* wave equation in $\psi$:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial r^2} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial t^2}
$$

### 2.3.2 General Outgoing Spherical Wave

The outgoing (radiating away from source) harmonic solution is:

$$
p(r,t) = \frac{A}{r} e^{j(\omega t - kr)}
$$

where $A$ is a complex constant (Pa·m) determined by the source strength.

### 2.3.3 Intensity in a Spherical Wave

The time-averaged intensity is:

$$
I(r) = \frac{|p_{rms}(r)|^2}{\rho_0 c} = \frac{|A|^2}{2\rho_0 c\, r^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Inverse Square Law (ISL):**
> $I \propto \dfrac{1}{r^2}$ — Sound intensity from a point source falls off as the square of the distance. Doubling the distance reduces intensity to **one-quarter** (a drop of **6 dB**).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3D Wave Equation | — | $\nabla^2 p = \dfrac{1}{c^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}$ | — |
| 2 | Speed of sound in air | $c$ | $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T}$ | m/s |
| 3 | Wavenumber | $k$ | $k = \dfrac{\omega}{c} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ | rad/m |
| 4 | Plane wave pressure | $p(x,t)$ | $\hat{p}\, e^{j(\omega t - kx)}$ | Pa |
| 5 | Particle velocity (plane) | $u(x,t)$ | $\dfrac{p(x,t)}{\rho_0 c}$ | m/s |
| 6 | Characteristic impedance | $Z_0$ | $Z_0 = \rho_0 c$ | Pa·s/m (rayl) |
| 7 | Plane wave intensity | $I$ | $I = \dfrac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho_0 c} = \dfrac{\vert \hat{p} \vert^2}{2\rho_0 c}$ | W/m² |
| 8 | Spherical wave pressure | $p(r,t)$ | $\dfrac{A}{r}\, e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$ | Pa |
| 9 | Spherical wave intensity | $I(r)$ | $\dfrac{I_0 r_0^2}{r^2}$ | W/m² |
| 10 | Inverse square law (dB) | $\Delta L$ | $\Delta L = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ | dB |
| 11 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p$ | $20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{rms}}{p_{ref}}\right)$ | dB |
| 12 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I$ | $10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_{ref}}\right)$ | dB |
| 13 | Reference pressure | $p_{ref}$ | $20 \times 10^{-6}$ | Pa |
| 14 | Reference intensity | $I_{ref}$ | $10^{-12}$ | W/m² |
| 15 | Density of air (STP) | $\rho_0$ | $1.21$ | kg/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Levels (Memorise!):**
> $p_{ref} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ and $I_{ref} = 10^{-12}\,\text{W/m}^2$ are the KTU-mandated reference quantities for **all** decibel calculations.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plane wave analysis** drives the design of **expansion chambers, dissipative silencers, and reactive mufflers** in HVAC and exhaust systems.
- **Spherical wave analysis** underpins **outdoor sound propagation models** (ISO 9613-2), **sound power determination**, and the placement of **noise barriers** around industrial sites.
- The **inverse square law** is the basis for the *6-dB rule* used in environmental noise assessments worldwide.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Plane Wave from the 1D Wave Equation

**Starting Point:** The 1D wave equation is

$$
\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}
$$

**Step 1 — Assume a separable (harmonic) solution** of the form

$$
p(x,t) = X(x) \, T(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the wave equation:**

$$
T(t) \frac{d^2 X}{dx^2} = \frac{1}{c^2} X(x) \frac{d^2 T}{dt^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by $X(x)\,T(t)$:**

$$
\frac{1}{X} \frac{d^2 X}{dx^2} = \frac{1}{c^2 T} \frac{d^2 T}{dt^2} = -k^2
$$

The LHS depends only on $x$, the RHS only on $t$. For the equality to hold for *all* $x$ and $t$, each side must equal a constant, which we set to $-k^2$ (a negative value gives oscillatory solutions).

**Step 4 — Solve the spatial ODE:**

$$
\frac{d^2 X}{dx^2} + k^2 X = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad X(x) = A e^{-jkx} + B e^{+jkx}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the temporal ODE:**

$$
\frac{d^2 T}{dt^2} + \omega^2 T = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T(t) = C e^{j\omega t} + D e^{-j\omega t}
$$

where $\omega = kc$.

**Step 6 — Combine and form the physical (real) pressure:**

$$
p(x,t) = \text{Re}\!\left\{ \hat{p}\, e^{j(\omega t - kx)} \right\} = \vert \hat{p} \vert \cos(\omega t - kx + \phi)
$$

The harmonic plane wave is fully established.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Particle Velocity and Impedance for a Plane Wave

**Starting Point:** Euler's linearised equation (no body forces):

$$
\rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}
$$

**Step 1 — Take the phasor form** $p(x,t) = \hat{p} e^{j(\omega t - kx)}$ and $u(x,t) = \hat{u} e^{j(\omega t - kx)}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the partial derivatives:**

$$
\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = -jk \hat{p}\, e^{j(\omega t - kx)} = -jk\, p(x,t)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = j\omega \hat{u}\, e^{j(\omega t - kx)} = j\omega\, u(x,t)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into Euler's equation:**

$$
\rho_0 \cdot j\omega\, u = -(-jk)\, p = jk\, p
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $u$:**

$$
u = \frac{k}{\rho_0 \omega} p = \frac{1}{\rho_0 c} p
$$

**Step 5 — Form the specific acoustic impedance:**

$$
Z = \frac{p}{u} = \rho_0 c
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** The impedance is **purely real** (no imaginary part) for a plane progressive wave. This means pressure and particle velocity are **in phase** and the wave transports energy without reactive storage. This is *unique* to the plane wave — spherical waves have additional near-field reactive components.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Spherical Wave from the 3D Wave Equation

**Starting Point:** Wave equation in spherical coordinates (radially symmetric):

$$
\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitution to remove the first-derivative term.** Let

$$
p(r,t) = \frac{1}{r}\psi(r,t)
$$

Compute the required derivatives:

$$
\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} = -\frac{1}{r^2}\psi + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial r^2} = \frac{2}{r^3}\psi - \frac{2}{r^2}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial r^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert into the spherical wave equation:**

$$
\left[\frac{2}{r^3}\psi - \frac{2}{r^2}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial r^2}\right] + \frac{2}{r}\left[-\frac{1}{r^2}\psi + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}\right] = \frac{1}{c^2 r}\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial t^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify (the first-derivative terms cancel):**

$$
\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial r^2} = \frac{1}{c^2 r}\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial t^2}
$$

Multiply by $r$:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial r^2} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial t^2}
$$

This is the *same* 1D wave equation as in the plane-wave case. By d'Alembert:

$$
\psi(r,t) = f(r - ct) + g(r + ct)
$$

**Step 4 — Form the physical pressure:**

$$
p(r,t) = \frac{f(r-ct)}{r} + \frac{g(r+ct)}{r}
$$

**Step 5 — The outgoing (radiating) harmonic solution** selects the $f(r-ct)$ branch with $f = A e^{-jkr}$:

$$
\boxed{\,p(r,t) = \frac{A}{r}\, e^{j(\omega t - kr)}\,}
$$

The crucial **$1/r$ amplitude decay** is now explicit.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: The Inverse Square Law

**Step 1 — Write the RMS pressure of a spherical wave:**

$$
p_{rms}(r) = \frac{\vert A \vert}{\sqrt{2}\, r}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the intensity formula:**

$$
I(r) = \frac{p_{rms}^2}{\rho_0 c} = \frac{\vert A \vert^2}{2 \rho_0 c\, r^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Express as a ratio at two distances:**

$$
\frac{I(r_2)}{I(r_1)} = \left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^2
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to decibels:**

$$
\Delta L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^2 = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)
$$

**Special case** — doubling the distance ($r_2 = 2r_1$):

$$
\Delta L_I = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -6.02 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "6 dB per doubling of distance" rule is the cornerstone of industrial noise control** — a fact the KTU board examines every year.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Plane Wave | Spherical Wave |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry of wavefront | Parallel planes | Concentric spheres |
| Coordinate dependence | $p = p(x,t)$ | $p = p(r,t)$ |
| Pressure amplitude vs distance | **Constant** | $\propto 1/r$ |
| Intensity vs distance | **Constant** | $\propto 1/r^2$ |
| Specific acoustic impedance | $Z = \rho_0 c$ (real) | $Z = \rho_0 c \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{jkr}\right)$ (complex) |
| Energy flow per unit area | Same at any $x$ | Falls as $1/r^2$ |
| Total power through a wavefront | $\infty$ (idealised) | Constant, $P = 4\pi r^2 I(r)$ |
| Typical industrial application | Ducts, silencers | Free-field noise sources |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Plane Wave vs Spherical Wave Generation and Propagation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Sound Source Plane Type] --> B1[Large Flat Vibrating Surface]
    A2[Sound Source Spherical Type] --> B2[Point Source Compact Pulsating Sphere]
    
    B1 --> C1[Wavefronts Parallel Planes]
    B2 --> C2[Wavefronts Concentric Spheres]
    
    C1 --> D1[Pressure p x t = A cos omega t minus kx]
    C2 --> D2[Pressure p r t = A over r times cos omega t minus kr]
    
    D1 --> E1[Intensity I = p rms squared over rho0 c]
    D2 --> E2[Intensity I r = I0 r0 squared over r squared]
    
    E1 --> F1[Distance Independence Constant Amplitude]
    E2 --> F2[Inverse Square Law 6 dB per Doubling]
    
    F1 --> G1[Application HVAC Ducts and Silencers]
    F2 --> G2[Application Outdoor Industrial Noise and Environmental Impact]
    
    G1 --> H1[Design Tool Impedance Matching and Transmission Loss]
    G2 --> H2[Design Tool ISO 9613-2 and Noise Barrier Sizing]
```

## 4.2 Wavefront Geometry Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PlaneWavefronts
    P1[Source Wall or Large Piston] --> P2[Wavefront 1]
    P2 --> P3[Wavefront 2]
    P3 --> P4[Wavefront 3]
    P4 --> P5[Wavefront 4]
    end
    
    subgraph SphericalWavefronts
    S1[Point Source] --> S2[Wavefront r1 Small Sphere]
    S2 --> S3[Wavefront r2 Larger Sphere]
    S3 --> S4[Wavefront r3 Even Larger Sphere]
    S4 --> S5[Wavefront r4 Largest Sphere]
    end
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: From Governing Equation to Industrial Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Linearised Continuity Equation] --> D[3D Wave Equation]
    B[Linearised Euler Equation] --> D
    C[Adiabatic Equation of State] --> D
    D --> E{Choose Geometry}
    E -->|Cartesian 1D| F[Plane Wave Solution]
    E -->|Spherical Radial| G[Spherical Wave Solution]
    F --> H[Z equals rho0 c real]
    G --> I[Z equals rho0 c times 1 plus 1 over jkr]
    H --> J[Industrial Use Duct Silencer Design]
    I --> K[Industrial Use Sound Power and Free Field Mapping]
    J --> L[Outcome Transmission Loss and Attenuation]
    K --> M[Outcome Noise Contour Map and Barrier Design]
```

## 4.4 Intensity Decay Schematic (Spherical Wave)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Point Source Power P watts] --> A1[At r1 Intensity I1 = P over 4 pi r1 squared]
    A1 --> A2[At r2 = 2 r1 Intensity I2 = P over 4 pi times 2 r1 squared]
    A2 --> A3[Intensity Ratio I1 over I2 = 4]
    A3 --> A4[In Decibels Delta L = 10 log10 4 = 6.02 dB]
    A4 --> A5[Conclusion 6 dB Drop per Doubling of Distance]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Use these schematics to *visually anchor* the equations in your written answers. A well-drawn block diagram alongside a derivation can earn **partial credit** even if a minor algebraic slip occurs — examiners value clear conceptual understanding.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a plane acoustic wave. State its key mathematical property. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A plane acoustic wave is one whose wavefronts are infinite parallel planes perpendicular to the direction of propagation. The sound pressure depends on only one spatial coordinate (say $x$) and time, and is given by

$$
p(x,t) = \hat{p}\, e^{j(\omega t - kx)}
$$

**Key mathematical property:** The specific acoustic impedance of a plane progressive wave is purely real and equals the characteristic impedance of the medium:

$$
Z = \frac{p}{u} = \rho_0 c
$$

For air, $\rho_0 c \approx 413 \,\text{rayls}$. The pressure amplitude is **independent of distance** from the source.

---

### Question 2: State and explain the inverse square law for sound propagation from a point source. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **inverse square law** states that the time-averaged intensity of a spherical wave radiating from a point source is inversely proportional to the square of the distance $r$ from the source:

$$
I(r) = \frac{I_0 r_0^2}{r^2}
$$

This arises because the total acoustic power $P$ emitted by the source is distributed uniformly over the surface of an expanding sphere of area $4\pi r^2$. In decibels, doubling the distance causes a drop of

$$
\Delta L = 20\log_{10}(2) \approx 6 \text{ dB}
$$

The law holds only in the **free far field** (no reflections, no absorption, source is acoustically small).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Derive the general solution of the 1D wave equation and obtain the expression for the specific acoustic impedance of a plane progressive wave. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Step 1 — State the 1D wave equation** (1 mark)

$$
\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Assume separable solution** $p = X(x)T(t)$ (1 mark)

$$
T \frac{d^2 X}{dx^2} = \frac{1}{c^2} X \frac{d^2 T}{dt^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables, set each side equal to $-k^2$** (1 mark)

$$
\frac{1}{X}\frac{d^2 X}{dx^2} = -k^2, \qquad \frac{1}{c^2 T}\frac{d^2 T}{dt^2} = -k^2
$$

**Step 4 — Solve both ODEs** (1 mark)

$$
X(x) = A e^{-jkx} + B e^{+jkx}, \quad T(t) = C e^{j\omega t} + D e^{-j\omega t}
$$

with $\omega = kc$.

**Step 5 — Combine and form the physical pressure** (1 mark)

$$
p(x,t) = \hat{p}\, \cos(\omega t - kx + \phi)
$$

**Step 6 — Apply Euler's equation and derive impedance** (2 marks)

$$
\rho_0 \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \quad \Rightarrow \quad u = \frac{p}{\rho_0 c}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Z = \frac{p}{u} = \rho_0 c\,}
$$

#### (b) A factory noise source radiates sound equally in all directions with a sound power of 80 W. Calculate the sound intensity and sound intensity level at distances of 5 m and 10 m. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Given:** $P = 80$ W, $r_1 = 5$ m, $r_2 = 10$ m

**Step 1 — Intensity at $r_1 = 5$ m** (2 marks)

$$
I_1 = \frac{P}{4\pi r_1^2} = \frac{80}{4\pi (5)^2} = \frac{80}{100\pi} = 0.2546 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Intensity at $r_2 = 10$ m** (2 marks)

$$
I_2 = \frac{80}{4\pi (10)^2} = \frac{80}{400\pi} = 0.0637 \text{ W/m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Sound Intensity Levels** (2 marks)

$$
L_{I1} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.2546}{10^{-12}}\right) = 114.06 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
L_{I2} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.0637}{10^{-12}}\right) = 108.04 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment** (1 mark)

The drop in level is $114.06 - 108.04 \approx 6.02$ dB, confirming the **inverse square law** (6 dB drop per doubling of distance). This is critical in industrial noise control: simply moving an employee 10 m away from an 80 W source reduces their noise exposure by 6 dB.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Alternative*

#### (a) Starting from the 3D wave equation in spherical coordinates, derive the solution for an outgoing spherical wave from a point source. Show clearly how the $1/r$ amplitude decay arises. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Step 1 — State the spherical wave equation** (1 mark)

$$
\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial r^2} + \frac{2}{r}\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $p = \psi/r$** and simplify (2 marks)

After substitution, all first-derivative terms in $r$ cancel, leaving:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial r^2} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 \psi}{\partial t^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the d'Alembert solution** (1 mark)

$$
\psi(r,t) = f(r - ct) + g(r + ct)
$$

**Step 4 — Select the outgoing branch and form the pressure** (1 mark)

$$
p(r,t) = \frac{f(r - ct)}{r}
$$

**Step 5 — Harmonic form and identification of $1/r$ decay** (2 marks)

$$
\boxed{\,p(r,t) = \frac{A}{r}\, e^{j(\omega t - kr)}\,}
$$

The pressure amplitude decays as $1/r$ **because** the total radiated power $P$ is conserved through each concentric spherical surface of area $4\pi r^2$. Since $I = p_{rms}^2/(\rho_0 c) \propto 1/r^2$, the pressure must scale as $1/r$.

#### (b) A measurement microphone records an SPL of 92 dB at 2 m from a point source. Estimate (i) the sound pressure at 2 m, (ii) the SPL at 8 m assuming free-field conditions, and (iii) the sound power of the source. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Given:** $L_{p1} = 92$ dB at $r_1 = 2$ m, $r_2 = 8$ m, $p_{ref} = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa

**Step 1 — Sound pressure at 2 m** (2 marks)

$$
p_1 = p_{ref} \cdot 10^{L_{p1}/20} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{92/20} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 39810.7
$$

$$
p_1 \approx 0.796 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — SPL at 8 m** (2 marks)

Using the inverse square law in dB:

$$
L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = 92 - 20\log_{10}(4)
$$

$$
L_{p2} = 92 - 12.04 \approx 79.96 \text{ dB} \approx 80 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Sound power of the source** (3 marks)

The intensity at $r_1 = 2$ m is:

$$
I_1 = \frac{p_1^2}{\rho_0 c} = \frac{(0.796)^2}{413} = 1.534 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W/m}^2
$$

Total power:

$$
P = 4\pi r_1^2 I_1 = 4\pi (2)^2 (1.534 \times 10^{-3}) = 7.71 \times 10^{-2} \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P \approx 77.1 \text{ mW}\,}
$$

Equivalently, the sound power level is

$$
L_W = L_{I1} + 10\log_{10}(4\pi r_1^2) = 121.86 \text{ dB} - 10\log_{10}(50.27) \approx 121.86 - 17.01 = 104.85 \text{ dB re 10}^{-12}\text{ W}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Valuator Feedback):**
>
> 1. **Mixing up $1/r$ and $1/r^2$ laws** — Pressure decays as $1/r$, intensity as $1/r^2$, and SPL/SIL as $1/r$ and $1/r^2$ in dB respectively. Examiners check the *distinction* explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the reference values** — Always state $p_{ref} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ and $I_{ref} = 10^{-12}\,\text{W/m}^2$. Omitting them loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing $\log$ instead of $\log_{10}$** — All decibel equations in acoustics use base-10 logarithm. Writing natural log is a frequent slip.
> 4. **Claiming plane waves exist in free field** — A plane wave is an *idealisation*; valid only in ducts, far fields, or near large surfaces. Examiners penalise this oversight.
> 5. **Forgetting units** — Always express the final answer with correct units (Pa, W/m², dB re 20 μPa).
> 6. **Skipping intermediate steps in the wave equation derivation** — The *substitution $p = \psi/r$* must be shown explicitly. The cancellation of first-derivative terms is what earns full credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Plane & Spherical Waves**
>
> ☐ **Plane wave** pressure: $p = \hat{p}\, e^{j(\omega t - kx)}$ — amplitude **constant** with distance.
>
> ☐ **Spherical wave** pressure: $p = (A/r)\, e^{j(\omega t - kr)}$ — amplitude decays as $1/r$.
>
> ☐ **Characteristic impedance**: $Z_0 = \rho_0 c \approx 413$ rayls in air — purely real for plane waves.
>
> ☐ **Plane wave intensity**: $I = p_{rms}^2 / (\rho_0 c)$ — **independent of $x$**.
>
> ☐ **Spherical wave intensity**: $I(r) = I_0 r_0^2 / r^2$ — **inverse square law**.
>
> ☐ **6 dB per doubling of distance** rule is valid for *spherical waves in free field*.
>
> ☐ **SPL formula**: $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p_{rms}/p_{ref})$, with $p_{ref} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$.
>
> ☐ **SIL formula**: $L_I = 10\log_{10}(I/I_{ref})$, with $I_{ref} = 10^{-12}\,\text{W/m}^2$.
>
> ☐ **Total acoustic power**: $P = 4\pi r^2 I(r)$ — constant for any closed spherical surface enclosing a point source.
>
> ☐ **d'Alembert's solution**: $f(x-ct)$ travels in $+x$ direction; $g(x+ct)$ travels in $-x$ direction.
>
> ☐ **Substitution trick** for spherical waves: $p = \psi/r$ converts the 3D equation into a 1D equation in $\psi$.
>
> ☐ **Wavenumber relation**: $k = \omega/c = 2\pi/\lambda$ — required in all harmonic solutions.
>
> ☐ **Real-world use**: Plane waves → duct acoustics, silencers; Spherical waves → environmental noise, sound power, barrier design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concepts of Free Field and Diffuse Field, Nearfield and Far Field
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT632 - INDUSTRIAL NOISE AND VIBRATION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concepts of Free Field and Diffuse Field, Nearfield and Far Field

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Free Field

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
A *Free Sound Field* is defined as a sound field in a homogeneous, isotropic medium bounded only by the source and an infinitely distant rigid boundary, in which the effects of the boundaries on sound waves may be neglected. In this idealized environment, sound waves propagate outward from a source in a uniform manner without any reflections, refractions, or interference from external boundaries.

**Mathematical Signature:** Sound Pressure Level (SPL) in a free field follows the **Inverse Square Law (ISL)**, meaning the sound intensity decreases by **6 dB** for every doubling of distance from the source.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight - Free Field Conditions:**
> - **No reflections** from any boundary (floor, walls, or ceiling)
> - **Homogeneous medium** (uniform air properties)
> - Sound propagates as a **spherical wave** in 3D space
> - Example Realizations: Open outdoor space (away from buildings), **Anechoic Chamber**, sound propagating high above the ground

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Flashlight in Fog" Analogy:**
> Imagine standing in an infinitely vast, pitch-black, empty field with a flashlight. The light beam travels outward unobstructed in all directions. The further you walk away from the flashlight, the dimmer the light becomes — not because light is being absorbed, but because the same amount of energy is being spread over a larger spherical surface area. A **Free Field** is the acoustic equivalent of this scenario: sound energy spreads over an ever-expanding sphere as it travels away from the source, and there are no walls to bounce the light (or sound) back.

---

### 1.2 The Diffuse Field

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
A *Diffuse Sound Field* is an idealized sound field in which the time-averaged mean square sound pressure is everywhere the same, and the sound energy flow is equally probable in all directions. In simpler terms, the sound waves are statistically uniform in space and direction, typically resulting from many overlapping reflections inside an enclosed space with highly reflective boundaries.

**Mathematical Signature:** In a perfectly diffuse field, the mean square sound pressure is uniform throughout the volume, and energy density is homogeneously distributed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight - Diffuse Field Properties:**
> - Sound energy is **uniformly distributed** in both position and direction
> - Dominated by **multipath reflections** from walls, ceiling, and floor
> - Typical in industrial enclosures, reverberant rooms, and large halls
> - Standing wave patterns are "smoothed out" statistically

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Bumper Car Arena" Analogy:**
> Picture a crowded bumper car arena with hard, reflective walls. Cars (sound waves) zoom in every direction, bouncing off every wall multiple times per second. From any single point inside the arena, cars are arriving at you from **all directions** at roughly **equal rates**. The chaos evens out — no matter where you stand, you experience statistically the same "bumper energy." This is a **Diffuse Field**: a state of maximum acoustic chaos where directionality is lost and energy is uniformly spread.

---

### 1.3 The Near Field

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
The *Near Field* (also called the **Fresnel Zone** or **Reactive Near Field**) is the region immediately surrounding a sound source where the sound field is dominated by the source's specific geometry and where the sound pressure and particle velocity oscillate **out of phase** with each other. The acoustic impedance in this region is reactive rather than resistive, meaning energy oscillates back and forth between the source and the surrounding medium without being effectively radiated away.

**Key Identifier:** The **critical near-field distance** is conventionally approximated as:

$$d_{nf} \approx \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$$

or conservatively estimated as $d_{nf} \geq 2\lambda$ to $3\lambda$ for most engineering applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Turbulent Whirlpool" Analogy:**
> Stand one inch away from a spinning washing machine agitator. The water churns violently, splashes in complex, unpredictable directions, and feels "thick" and reactive — you cannot describe it with a simple wave equation. The **Near Field** is acoustic turbulence: the source's own mechanical details dominate, and standard wave-propagation rules (like the inverse square law) **do not yet apply**. You must be farther away for the wave to "settle down" into a predictable pattern.

---

### 1.4 The Far Field

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
The *Far Field* (also called the **Fraunhofer Zone** or **Radiating Field**) is the region sufficiently far from the source where the sound field behaves as a coherent spherical wave (for a point source) or plane wave (at very large distances). In this region, the sound pressure and particle velocity are **in phase**, the acoustic impedance equals the characteristic impedance of the medium ($\rho_0 c$), and the **Inverse Square Law** rigorously applies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Calm Lake Horizon" Analogy:**
> Move 100 meters away from the washing machine. By now, all the turbulent churning has averaged out. You hear a steady, uniform hum. The wave fronts arriving at your ear are nearly flat (or gently spherical), and their amplitude follows a clean mathematical decay with distance. This is the **Far Field** — the predictable, "tidy" acoustic zone where textbook wave theory and the inverse square law work flawlessly.

---

### 1.5 Comparative Summary Table

| Property | Free Field | Diffuse Field | Near Field | Far Field |
|----------|-----------|---------------|------------|-----------|
| **Boundaries** | Absent (infinite) | Highly reflective | Source-dominated | Source-independent |
| **Wave Behavior** | Outgoing spherical | Statistical, omnidirectional | Turbulent, reactive | Coherent, propagating |
| **SPL vs Distance** | -6 dB per doubling | Approximately constant | Non-monotonic | -6 dB per doubling |
| **Pressure-Velocity Phase** | In phase | Statistically uncorrelated | Out of phase | In phase |
| **Typical Environment** | Anechoic chamber, outdoors | Reverberant room, factory | Right at the source | Most measurement positions |
| **Dominant Phenomenon** | Direct radiation | Multipath reflections | Source geometry | Wave propagation |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deep Dive — The Free Field

#### 2.1.1 Mathematical Foundation
Consider an ideal point source radiating power $W$ uniformly in all directions (monopole source). At distance $r$, this power is distributed over the surface area of a sphere of radius $r$:

$$I(r) = \frac{W}{4\pi r^2}$$

Since Sound Intensity Level (SIL) and Sound Pressure Level (SPL) are related by $I \propto p^2$, the sound pressure falls off as $\frac{1}{r}$, which corresponds to a **-6 dB** reduction per doubling of distance.

**Why does this happen?** Sound is a conserved quantity (energy cannot be created or destroyed in a lossless medium). As the wavefront expands spherically, the same total energy must be spread over a progressively larger surface area, diluting the local intensity.

#### 2.1.2 The Doubling-Distance Rule (Step-by-Step)
- Distance $r_1$ → SPL = $L_{p1}$
- Distance $r_2 = 2r_1$ → SPL = $L_{p2}$

$$L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2r_1}{r_1}\right) = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}(2) = L_{p1} - 6.02 \text{ dB}$$

Therefore, **every doubling of distance reduces SPL by 6 dB** in a free field.

#### 2.1.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Outdoor noise mapping** for environmental impact assessments
- **Open-air concert sound** design and PA system calibration
- **Wind turbine noise** propagation modeling
- **Aircraft flyover** noise certification
- **Calibration of microphones** in anechoic chambers (free-field microphones)

---

### 2.2 Deep Dive — The Diffuse Field

#### 2.2.1 Mathematical Foundation
In a perfectly diffuse field, the relationship between sound pressure and intensity is governed by the **Reverberant Field Equations**. The mean square sound pressure is uniform:

$$\langle p^2 \rangle = \text{constant throughout the volume}$$

The relationship between the **Reverberant Sound Pressure Level** ($L_{pr}$) and the **Total Acoustic Power** ($W$) inside an enclosure of volume $V$ and reverberation time $T_{60}$ is given by the classical Sabine-derived equation:

$$L_{pr} = L_W + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{R}\right) = L_W + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4 \cdot T_{60}}{0.161 \, V}\right)$$

where:
- $L_W$ is the **Sound Power Level** of the source (dB re 1 pW)
- $R$ is the **Room Constant** ($R = \frac{S \bar{\alpha}}{1 - \bar{\alpha}}$)
- $S$ = total surface area, $\bar{\alpha}$ = average absorption coefficient

#### 2.2.2 The Critical Distance
The **Critical Distance** ($r_c$ or $D_c$) is the distance from the source at which the **Direct Sound Level** equals the **Reverberant Sound Level**:

$$D_c = \sqrt{\frac{R \cdot Q}{16 \pi}} \approx 0.057 \sqrt{\frac{V}{T_{60}}}$$

for an omnidirectional source ($Q = 1$).

- If $r < D_c$ → **Direct Field Dominates** (Free Field behavior locally)
- If $r > D_c$ → **Reverberant Field Dominates** (Diffuse Field behavior)

#### 2.2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Industrial noise control** in factories and workshops
- **Audiometric booth** and **broadcast studio** design
- **Indoor HVAC** noise assessment
- **Workplace noise exposure** measurement (worker stands in reverberant field)
- **Concert hall acoustics**

---

### 2.3 Deep Dive — The Near Field vs Far Field Transition

#### 2.3.1 The Boundary Criterion
For a **piston source** of diameter $D$ radiating a single frequency $f$ (wavelength $\lambda$), the transition between near and far fields occurs approximately at:

$$r_{nf} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda} = \frac{2D^2 f}{c}$$

**Derivation Logic:** This formula arises from the condition that the phase difference between waves arriving from the center of the source and the edge of the source, at distance $r$, must be less than $\pi/2$ radians (a quarter wavelength) for the far-field approximation to hold.

#### 2.3.2 Engineering Approximations
- **Conservative rule:** $r_{nf} \approx 3\lambda$ (used when source dimensions are unknown)
- **Mid-frequency rule:** $r_{nf} \approx D$ (used for general industrial noise, 500 Hz – 2 kHz)
- **Strict acoustic rule:** $r_{nf} \approx 2D^2 / \lambda$

#### 2.3.3 Three Sub-Zones of the Source Region
1. **Reactive Near Field** ($r < 0.1 D$): Acoustic energy oscillates; no net radiation
2. **Fresnel (Radiating Near Field)** ($0.1 D < r < 2D^2/\lambda$): Transition zone
3. **Fraunhofer (Far Field)** ($r > 2D^2/\lambda$): Predictable spherical wave

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** | **Applicable Zone** |
|-------|-------------|-------------|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 | $I = \frac{W}{4\pi r^2}$ | Inverse square law for intensity | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Free Field, Far Field |
| 2 | $\Delta L_p = -20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ | SPL change with distance | dB | Free Field, Far Field |
| 3 | $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 6 \text{ dB}$ (per doubling) | 6 dB rule | dB | Free Field, Far Field |
| 4 | $L_{pr} = L_W + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{R}\right)$ | Reverberant SPL in diffuse field | dB | Diffuse Field |
| 5 | $R = \frac{S \bar{\alpha}}{1 - \bar{\alpha}}$ | Room constant | m² (sabins) | Diffuse Field |
| 6 | $D_c = \sqrt{\frac{R \cdot Q}{16\pi}}$ | Critical distance | m | Free + Diffuse combined |
| 7 | $r_{nf} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$ | Near-to-far field transition | m | Near / Far boundary |
| 8 | $r_{nf} \approx 3\lambda$ | Conservative near-field distance | m | Near / Far boundary |
| 9 | $\lambda = \frac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength (where $c \approx 343$ m/s in air) | m | All fields |
| 10 | $Z_0 = \rho_0 c \approx 413$ Rayl | Characteristic acoustic impedance of air | Pa·s/m | Far Field only |
| 11 | $L_I = L_p$ (in free field) | Intensity level equals SPL | dB | Free Field, Far Field |
| 12 | $L_I = L_p - 6$ (in diffuse field) | Intensity level relation in diffuse | dB | Diffuse Field |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Constants (Memorize for KTU Exams):**
> - **Speed of sound in air:** $c = 343$ m/s (at 20°C)
> - **Density of air:** $\rho_0 = 1.21$ kg/m³ (at 20°C)
> - **Reference pressure:** $p_0 = 20 \ \mu\text{Pa}$
> - **Reference power:** $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE Derivation: The Inverse Square Law for SPL

**Goal:** Prove that SPL decreases by 6 dB for every doubling of distance in a free field.

---

**Step 1 — Start with the Point Source Assumption**
An ideal point source (monopole) radiates acoustic power $W$ uniformly in all directions. This is the foundation of the free field model.

---

**Step 2 — Write the Geometric Spreading Equation**
The total power passing through an imaginary sphere of radius $r$ centered on the source equals $W$. Since the surface area of a sphere is $4\pi r^2$:

$$I(r) = \frac{W}{4\pi r^2}$$

where $I(r)$ is the sound intensity at distance $r$ in $\text{W/m}^2$.

---

**Step 3 — Relate Intensity to Sound Pressure**
For a plane wave, $I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho_0 c}$. For a spherical wave, the same relation holds locally (the wave is locally planar at small patches of the sphere). Therefore:

$$p_{\text{rms}}^2(r) = \rho_0 c \cdot I(r) = \frac{\rho_0 c \, W}{4\pi r^2}$$

---

**Step 4 — Take the Ratio of Pressures at Two Distances**

$$\frac{p_{\text{rms}}(r_2)}{p_{\text{rms}}(r_1)} = \frac{r_1}{r_2}$$

---

**Step 5 — Convert Pressure Ratio to Decibel Difference**
By definition, the Sound Pressure Level difference between two points is:

$$\Delta L_p = L_{p2} - L_{p1} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}(r_2)}{p_{\text{rms}}(r_1)}\right)$$

Substituting Step 4:

$$\Delta L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right) = -20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$

---

**Step 6 — Apply the Doubling Condition**
Let $r_2 = 2 r_1$:

$$\Delta L_p = -20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2 r_1}{r_1}\right) = -20 \log_{10}(2)$$

Since $\log_{10}(2) = 0.30103$:

$$\Delta L_p = -20 \times 0.30103 = -6.0206 \text{ dB}$$

---

**Step 7 — Conclusion**
**Q.E.D.:** Every doubling of distance in a free field reduces the SPL by exactly **6 dB** (often rounded to "6 dB per doubling"). The negative sign indicates attenuation.

---

### 3.2 EXHAUSTIVE Derivation: Critical Distance $D_c$

**Goal:** Derive the distance at which direct and reverberant sound levels are equal.

---

**Step 1 — Direct Sound Pressure Level**
For an omnidirectional source of power $W$ at distance $r$ in a free field:

$$L_{p,\text{direct}} = L_W + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{4\pi r^2}\right) = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 10 \log_{10}(4\pi)$$

---

**Step 2 — Reverberant (Diffuse) Sound Pressure Level**
In a diffuse field, the SPL is uniform and given by:

$$L_{p,\text{rev}} = L_W + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{R}\right)$$

where $R$ is the room constant.

---

**Step 3 — Equate the Two Levels at the Critical Distance**
At $r = D_c$, the direct and reverberant levels are equal:

$$L_W - 20 \log_{10}(D_c) - 10 \log_{10}(4\pi) = L_W + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{R}\right)$$

Cancel $L_W$ from both sides:

$$- 20 \log_{10}(D_c) - 10 \log_{10}(4\pi) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{R}\right)$$

---

**Step 4 — Rearrange to Solve for $D_c$**

$$- 20 \log_{10}(D_c) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{4}{R}\right) + 10 \log_{10}(4\pi)$$

$$- 20 \log_{10}(D_c) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{16\pi}{R}\right)$$

$$\log_{10}(D_c) = -\frac{1}{2} \log_{10}\left(\frac{16\pi}{R}\right) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{16\pi}{R}\right)^{-1/2}$$

$$D_c = \sqrt{\frac{R}{16\pi}}$$

For an omnidirectional source with directivity $Q = 1$:

$$\boxed{D_c = \sqrt{\frac{R}{16\pi}} \approx 0.141 \sqrt{R}}$$

---

**Step 5 — Express in Terms of Room Volume and Reverberation Time**
Using Sabine's formula $T_{60} = 0.161 \frac{V}{S\bar{\alpha}} = \frac{0.161 V}{A}$, we have $A = \frac{0.161 V}{T_{60}}$, and for moderate absorption $\bar{\alpha} < 0.2$, $R \approx A$:

$$D_c \approx 0.057 \sqrt{\frac{V}{T_{60}}}$$

with $V$ in m³ and $T_{60}$ in seconds.

---

### 3.3 EXHAUSTIVE Derivation: Near-Field Distance for a Piston Source

**Goal:** Show that the far field begins at approximately $r = 2D^2/\lambda$ for a circular piston of diameter $D$.

---

**Step 1 — Set Up the Geometry**
Consider a circular piston of diameter $D$ vibrating uniformly at frequency $f$. The wavelength is $\lambda = c/f$. We are interested in a point $P$ on the central axis at distance $r$ from the piston face.

---

**Step 2 — Compute the Path Difference**
The acoustic wave arriving at $P$ from the **edge** of the piston travels a longer path than the wave from the **center** by:

$$\Delta = \sqrt{r^2 + (D/2)^2} - r$$

---

**Step 3 — Apply the Far-Field Approximation**
The far-field approximation is valid when the path difference is less than $\lambda/2$ (a quarter wavelength each way), so that the resulting phase error is small. We also need $r \gg D$, so we can Taylor-expand the square root:

$$\sqrt{r^2 + (D/2)^2} \approx r + \frac{(D/2)^2}{2r} = r + \frac{D^2}{8r}$$

Thus:

$$\Delta = \frac{D^2}{8r}$$

---

**Step 4 — Apply the $\lambda/2$ Path Difference Limit**

$$\frac{D^2}{8r} = \frac{\lambda}{2}$$

Solving for $r$:

$$r = \frac{D^2}{4\lambda}$$

**But wait** — for the Rayleigh criterion (phase difference across the aperture $\leq \pi/2$ rad), we get:

$$r = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$$

The factor of 8 comes from squaring and the criterion (path difference $\lambda/16$ from edge to center). Hence the widely-used engineering formula:

$$\boxed{r_{nf} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}}$$

---

**Step 5 — Numerical Sanity Check**
For a 30 cm loudspeaker ($D = 0.3$ m) at 1 kHz ($\lambda = 0.343$ m):

$$r_{nf} = \frac{2 \times 0.09}{0.343} \approx 0.525 \text{ m}$$

So the **far field begins at about 52 cm** for this common case.

---

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Free Field Decay

**Problem (KTU-Style):** A machine produces a sound power level of $L_W = 90$ dB. Calculate the SPL at 2 m, 4 m, 8 m, and 16 m in a free field.

**Step 1 — Use the Direct Field Equation**

$$L_p(r) = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 10.99$$

(The constant $10.99 = 10\log_{10}(4\pi)$.)

**Step 2 — Calculate at Each Distance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_p(2) &= 90 - 20 \log_{10}(2) - 10.99 = 90 - 6.02 - 10.99 = 72.99 \text{ dB} \\
L_p(4) &= 90 - 20 \log_{10}(4) - 10.99 = 90 - 12.04 - 10.99 = 66.97 \text{ dB} \\
L_p(8) &= 90 - 20 \log_{10}(8) - 10.99 = 90 - 18.06 - 10.99 = 60.95 \text{ dB} \\
L_p(16) &= 90 - 20 \log_{10}(16) - 10.99 = 90 - 24.08 - 10.99 = 54.93 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Observe the Pattern**
Each doubling of distance ($2 \to 4 \to 8 \to 16$ m) produces a **6 dB drop**, confirming the inverse square law.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Examiners:** The 6 dB-per-doubling rule is the *signature* of free-field behavior. If measurements deviate from this, the field is either near-field (closer than $2D^2/\lambda$) or contains significant reverberant (diffuse) energy.
