## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
**Define the following terms with one practical example for each:**
(a) Free Field, (b) Diffuse Field

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Free Field:** A free sound field is an idealized sound field in a homogeneous, isotropic medium that is free from boundaries or in which the effects of boundaries on sound waves are negligible. Sound waves propagate outward from the source as spherical waves without any reflections, following the **inverse square law** (6 dB drop per doubling of distance).
*Example:* Sound propagation in an open outdoor environment away from buildings, or inside an anechoic chamber.

**(b) Diffuse Field:** A diffuse sound field is an idealized sound field in which the time-averaged mean square sound pressure is **uniform throughout the space** and the sound energy flow is equally probable in **all directions**. It is dominated by multipath reflections.
*Example:* A heavily reverberant industrial workshop or a reverberation chamber used for acoustic testing.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Paper]**
**Distinguish between Near Field and Far Field of a sound source. State the criterion used to identify the boundary between them.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Near Field | Far Field |
|--------|------------|-----------|
| Location | Close to source | Far from source |
| Wave nature | Complex, source-geometry dependent | Simple spherical/plane wave |
| Pressure-velocity phase | Out of phase | In phase |
| Acoustic impedance | Reactive | Resistive (= $\rho_0 c$) |
| Inverse square law | Does NOT apply | Applies accurately |

**Criterion (Boundary):** The far field begins approximately at:

$$r_{nf} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda}$$

For unknown source dimensions, the conservative rule $r_{nf} \approx 3\lambda$ is used. Inside this distance, the field is "near"; beyond it, "far."

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, End Semester Examination, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the inverse square law for sound propagation in a free field, and show that the SPL drops by 6 dB for every doubling of distance from the source. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine has a sound power level of $L_W = 95$ dB re 1 pW. Calculate the SPL at distances of 1 m, 2 m, 4 m, 8 m, and 16 m in a free-field environment. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of Inverse Square Law (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Consider an ideal point source (monopole) radiating total acoustic power $W$ uniformly in all directions into a homogeneous medium. This is the definition of a free field.

**Step 2:** At any distance $r$ from the source, this power is distributed uniformly over the surface area of a sphere of radius $r$, whose area is $4\pi r^2$. Hence the sound intensity is:

$$I(r) = \frac{W}{4\pi r^2}$$

**Step 3:** The acoustic intensity is related to the root-mean-square sound pressure by:

$$I(r) = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2(r)}{\rho_0 c}$$

Equating both expressions:

$$p_{\text{rms}}^2(r) = \frac{\rho_0 c \, W}{4\pi r^2}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, the sound pressure is inversely proportional to the distance:

$$p_{\text{rms}}(r) \propto \frac{1}{r}$$

**Step 5:** The SPL at distance $r$ is:

$$L_p(r) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2(r)}{p_0^2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\rho_0 c \, W}{4\pi r^2 \, p_0^2}\right)$$

**Step 6:** For two distances $r_1$ and $r_2$, the difference in SPL is:

$$L_{p2} - L_{p1} = -20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) \quad \text{(Equation 1)}$$

**Step 7:** Setting $r_2 = 2 r_1$ (doubling of distance):

$$L_{p2} - L_{p1} = -20 \log_{10}(2) = -20 \times 0.30103 = -6.02 \text{ dB}$$

**Conclusion:** Every doubling of distance in a free field reduces the SPL by approximately **6 dB**. This is the Inverse Square Law (ISL) of acoustics. The law holds because the same acoustic energy must be spread over a geometrically expanding spherical surface as it propagates outward from the source.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)**

**Given:** $L_W = 95$ dB re 1 pW.

**Formula:** For a point source in a free field:

$$L_p(r) = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 10.99 \text{ dB}$$

**Calculation at each distance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_p(1) &= 95 - 20 \log_{10}(1) - 10.99 = 95 - 0 - 10.99 = 84.01 \text{ dB} \\
L_p(2) &= 95 - 20 \log_{10}(2) - 10.99 = 95 - 6.02 - 10.99 = 77.99 \text{ dB} \\
L_p(4) &= 95 - 20 \log_{10}(4) - 10.99 = 95 - 12.04 - 10.99 = 71.97 \text{ dB} \\
L_p(8) &= 95 - 20 \log_{10}(8) - 10.99 = 95 - 18.06 - 10.99 = 75.95 \text{ dB (corrected: } 65.95 \text{)} \\
L_p(16) &= 95 - 20 \log_{10}(16) - 10.99 = 95 - 24.08 - 10.99 = 59.93 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Note: For $L_p(8)$, rechecking — $95 - 18.06 - 10.99 = 65.95$ dB.)*

**Tabulated Result:**

| Distance $r$ (m) | SPL $L_p$ (dB) | Drop from previous |
|------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | 84.01 | — |
| 2 | 77.99 | -6.02 dB |
| 4 | 71.97 | -6.02 dB |
| 8 | 65.95 | -6.02 dB |
| 16 | 59.93 | -6.02 dB |

**Comment:** The SPL drops by a constant **6.02 dB** for every doubling of distance, perfectly validating the Inverse Square Law. This confirms the free-field behavior of the environment. The total drop from 1 m to 16 m is **24.08 dB**, equivalent to halving the perceived loudness approximately 4 times.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End Semester Examination, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the concepts of **Near Field** and **Far Field** around a sound source. State the mathematical criterion for the boundary distance, and explain its significance in industrial noise measurement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of a **Diffuse Sound Field**. For a factory hall of volume $V = 2000 \text{ m}^3$ with a reverberation time $T_{60} = 2.0$ s, calculate the **critical distance** for an omnidirectional source. Discuss the practical implications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Near Field and Far Field (7 marks)**

**Near Field Definition:** The Near Field is the region immediately surrounding a sound source (typically within a distance $r < 2D^2/\lambda$, where $D$ is the characteristic source dimension and $\lambda$ is the wavelength). In this region:

- Sound pressure and particle velocity are **out of phase**
- The acoustic impedance is **reactive** (energy is stored, not radiated)
- The Inverse Square Law does **not** apply
- The field is dominated by the source's specific geometry

**Far Field Definition:** The Far Field is the region beyond the near field (typically $r > 2D^2/\lambda$) where:

- The sound field behaves as a coherent spherical wave
- Pressure and velocity are **in phase**
- Acoustic impedance equals the characteristic impedance $\rho_0 c \approx 413$ Rayl
- The Inverse Square Law applies accurately

**Mathematical Criterion:** The boundary between the two is given by:

$$r_{nf} = \frac{2D^2}{\lambda} = \frac{2D^2 f}{c}$$

For a conservative estimate: $r_{nf} \approx 3\lambda$.

**Significance in Industrial Noise Measurement:**

1. **Microphone placement:** Measurements should be taken in the far field to obtain source-representative SPL data. Near-field measurements overestimate SPL and depend strongly on probe position.

2. **Source characterization:** Sound power level (a property of the source) can only be measured in the far field.

3. **Hearing protection zones:** Worker positions should be assessed relative to the far-field distance to ensure the inverse square law can be applied for distance-based noise control.

4. **Standardization:** ISO 3744 and ISO 9613-2 measurement standards require the far-field condition for accuracy.

---

**Part (b) — Diffuse Field and Critical Distance (7 marks)**

**Diffuse Field Definition:** A diffuse sound field is an idealized sound field characterized by:
- Uniform mean square sound pressure at all points in the volume
- Sound energy flow equally probable in **all directions**
- Achieved statistically in highly reverberant enclosures (small absorption coefficient)

**Calculation of Critical Distance:**

**Given:**
- Volume $V = 2000$ m³
- Reverberation time $T_{60} = 2.0$ s
- Source directivity $Q = 1$ (omnidirectional)

**Step 1 — Estimate Room Constant $R$:**
For moderate absorption, $R \approx A$, where $A$ is the total absorption in sabins. Using Sabine's formula $A = 0.161 V / T_{60}$:

$$A = \frac{0.161 \times 2000}{2.0} = \frac{322}{2.0} = 161 \text{ sabins (m}^2 \text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Critical Distance Formula:**

$$D_c = \sqrt{\frac{R \cdot Q}{16\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{161 \times 1}{16\pi}}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate:**

$$D_c = \sqrt{\frac{161}{50.265}} = \sqrt{3.203} = 1.79 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Alternative Formula Verification:**
Using the simplified formula for $Q = 1$:

$$D_c \approx 0.057 \sqrt{\frac{V}{T_{60}}} = 0.057 \sqrt{\frac{2000}{2.0}} = 0.057 \times \sqrt{1000} = 0.057 \times 31.62 = 1.802 \text{ m}$$

Both methods agree: $D_c \approx 1.8$ m.

**Practical Implications:**

1. **Within 1.8 m of the source:** Direct sound dominates. The -6 dB/doubling rule applies for noise control by repositioning workers.

2. **Beyond 1.8 m:** Reverberant (diffuse) field dominates. Moving farther from the source yields **no SPL reduction**; noise control must be achieved by absorption treatment or enclosing the source.

3. **For a typical industrial worker:** If the worker stands 5 m from a machine, the diffuse field is the controlling environment. Reducing the reverberation time (adding absorbers) becomes a more effective strategy than asking the worker to move farther away.

4. **Workplace safety:** Since reverberant fields elevate the overall noise floor, treating ceilings and walls with acoustic panels is critical in factories with high $T_{60}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing SPL and SIL:** Many students mix Sound Pressure Level with Sound Intensity Level. In a free field they are equal ($L_p = L_I$); in a diffuse field, $L_I = L_p - 3$ dB. Examiners specifically check this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the constant $10\log_{10}(4\pi) = 10.99$ dB:** When asked to compute SPL from Sound Power Level, students often drop this constant and lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using 20 dB per doubling:** This applies only to pressure doubling; the **6 dB per doubling** is the SPL drop. Mixing these up is a common error.
> 4. **Ignoring the 2D²/λ boundary:** Students sometimes apply the inverse square law even in the near field, which is physically incorrect.
> 5. **Forgetting units:** Always quote $D_c$ in meters, $L_p$ in dB re 20 μPa, $L_W$ in dB re 1 pW.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

- [x] **Free Field** = infinite space, no reflections, -6 dB per doubling of distance (Inverse Square Law)
- [x] **Diffuse Field** = reverberant space, uniform mean square pressure, energy from all directions
- [x] **Near Field** = source-dominated region, $r < 2D^2/\lambda$, pressure-velocity out of phase
- [x] **Far Field** = propagating wave region, $r > 2D^2/\lambda$, pressure-velocity in phase
- [x] **Key Formula 1:** $L_p = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 10.99$ (point source in free field)
- [x] **Key Formula 2:** $D_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{R \cdot Q}{16\pi}} \approx 0.057\sqrt{\dfrac{V}{T_{60}}}$ (for $Q=1$)
- [x] **Key Formula 3:** $r_{nf} = \dfrac{2D^2}{\lambda} \approx 3\lambda$ (near-to-far field boundary)
- [x] **Key Formula 4:** $\Delta L_p = -20 \log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ (SPL change with distance)
- [x] **Memorize Constants:** $c = 343$ m/s, $\rho_0 = 1.21$ kg/m³, $Z_0 = 413$ Rayl, $p_0 = 20$ μPa
- [x] **Standard Environments:** Anechoic chamber = Free field; Reverberation chamber = Diffuse field
- [x] **Critical Distance Insight:** SPL drops steeply up to $D_c$, then flattens in the diffuse region
- [x] **Phase Relationship:** In phase = Far Field / Free Field; Out of phase = Near Field
- [x] **Industrial Relevance:** In a factory (diffuse field), moving workers farther may NOT reduce noise — you must treat the room acoustically (absorbers, enclosures, barriers)
- [x] **Always specify reference:** dB re 20 μPa for SPL, dB re 1 pW for SWL
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency Analysis and Vibration and Noise Spectrum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT632 - INDUSTRIAL NOISE AND VIBRATION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Frequency Analysis and Vibration and Noise Spectrum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Frequency Analysis** is the systematic decomposition of a complex time-domain signal (vibration or acoustic pressure) into its constituent sinusoidal components, characterized by their amplitude and phase as a function of frequency. It is the fundamental diagnostic tool in industrial noise and vibration control, allowing engineers to identify the dominant energy contributors within a measured signal.

A **Vibration Spectrum** is a graphical representation of vibration amplitude (displacement, velocity, or acceleration) plotted against frequency. It reveals how the vibrational energy of a machine or structure is distributed across the frequency domain.

A **Noise Spectrum** (or **Sound Spectrum**) is a graphical representation of sound pressure level (SPL) or sound power level plotted against frequency, demonstrating how acoustic energy is distributed across the audible frequency range (typically **20 Hz to 20 kHz**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Concept:** A signal in the time domain $x(t)$ contains *all* its information compressed along the time axis. Frequency analysis "unfolds" this information, revealing hidden periodicities, resonances, harmonics, and broadband noise components that are impossible to identify in raw oscilloscope or recorder traces.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Prism of Light" Model

Imagine shining white light through a glass prism. The white light looks pure and uniform to your eye, but the prism reveals it is actually a blend of red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, and violet — each a different frequency of electromagnetic radiation.

**Frequency Analysis is the "acoustic prism" of engineering:**

- The **white light** = the raw vibration or noise signal (looks like a tangled mess on a screen)
- The **prism** = the mathematical transform (Fourier Transform)
- The **rainbow output** = the spectrum (amplitude vs. frequency)

Just as a chemist can identify an element by its spectral "fingerprint," a vibration analyst can identify a faulty bearing, an unbalanced rotor, or a meshing gear by its spectral signature. A motor running at **3000 RPM (50 Hz)** will produce a strong spectral peak at 50 Hz; a worn bearing will produce characteristic high-frequency ringing around 5–10 kHz; a gear mesh will produce sidebands around the gear-tooth passing frequency.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Engineering Metrics Used in Frequency Analysis:**
> - **Frequency ($f$):** measured in **Hertz (Hz)** — cycles per second
> - **Angular Frequency ($\omega$):** $2\pi f$, measured in **rad/s**
> - **Speed ($N$):** revolutions per minute (**RPM**); $f = N/60$ Hz
> - **Reference Sound Pressure:** $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa}$
> - **Reference Acceleration:** $a_0 = 10^{-6}\ \text{m/s}^2$
> - **Reference Velocity:** $v_0 = 10^{-9}\ \text{m/s}$
> - **Reference Displacement:** $d_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{m}$

## 1.4 Why Frequency Analysis Matters in Industry

- **Fault Diagnosis:** Detect early-stage bearing defects, gear wear, cavitation, and imbalance.
- **Resonance Avoidance:** Identify structural natural frequencies to prevent catastrophic resonance with operational forcing frequencies.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Environmental noise standards (e.g., OSHA, ISO 1996, Indian CPCB) require **A-weighted octave-band** measurements, not just overall dB.
- **Control Strategy Selection:** A tonal noise at 500 Hz demands a different treatment (tuned absorber, quarter-wave resonator) than broadband hiss.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fourier Foundation

Any physically realizable periodic signal $x(t)$ of period $T$ can be expressed as an infinite sum of sines and cosines — the **Fourier Series**:

$$x(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left[a_n \cos(n\omega_0 t) + b_n \sin(n\omega_0 t)\right]$$

where $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T$ is the fundamental angular frequency, $a_n$ and $b_n$ are Fourier coefficients computed by integration over one period.

For **non-periodic** signals (typical of random vibration and ambient noise), the Fourier Series generalizes to the **Fourier Transform (FT)**:

$$X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j2\pi f t}\, dt$$

and the inverse transform:

$$x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f)\, e^{j2\pi f t}\, df$$

The magnitude spectrum $\vert X(f) \vert$ is the **amplitude spectrum**, and $\vert X(f) \vert^2$ is the **energy spectrum** (Parseval's theorem).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** Engineers never compute continuous FTs. In practice, the **Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT)** is applied to digitized samples, computed efficiently by the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) algorithm** developed by Cooley & Tukey (1965), reducing computation from $O(N^2)$ to $O(N \log_2 N)$.

## 2.2 Power Spectral Density (PSD)

For **stationary random signals** (the most common type in industrial vibration), the meaningful spectral quantity is the **Power Spectral Density** $G_x(f)$, defined as:

$$G_x(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty}\frac{1}{T}\,\mathbb{E}\left[\vert X_T(f) \vert^2\right]$$

Units: $\text{m}^2/\text{s}^3$ for acceleration PSD, $\text{Pa}^2/\text{Hz}$ for sound pressure PSD.

The **Root Mean Square (RMS)** value of the signal is recovered by integrating the PSD over frequency (Parseval's theorem for power):

$$x_{\text{rms}}^2 = \int_{0}^{\infty} G_x(f)\, df$$

## 2.3 Octave-Band and Fractional-Octave Analysis

Because human hearing and most machine spectra span many decades of frequency, **constant-percentage bandwidth filters** are used rather than fixed-Hz filters. An **octave band** is a frequency interval where the upper frequency $f_2$ is exactly **twice** the lower frequency $f_1$:

$$\frac{f_2}{f_1} = 2^{1/n}$$

where $n = 1$ gives a **full octave**, $n = 3$ gives a **1/3-octave band** (industry standard), $n = 6$ gives a **1/6-octave band**, and $n = 12$ gives a **1/12-octave band**.

The **geometric center frequency** is:

$$f_c = \sqrt{f_1 \cdot f_2} = f_1 \cdot 2^{1/(2n)}$$

Standard 1/1-octave center frequencies (Hz): 16, 31.5, 63, 125, 250, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000, 8000, 16000.

Standard 1/3-octave center frequencies are spaced every $2^{1/6} \approx 1.1225$ ratio.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CHEAT SHEET — Memorize these for ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $f = N/60$ | Frequency from RPM | Hz |
| 2 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular frequency | rad/s |
| 3 | $X(f) = \int x(t) e^{-j2\pi f t} dt$ | Forward Fourier Transform | (varies) |
| 4 | $x(t) = \int X(f) e^{j2\pi f t} df$ | Inverse Fourier Transform | (varies) |
| 5 | $G_x(f)$ | Power Spectral Density | varies/Hz |
| 6 | $x_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\int_0^{\infty} G_x(f) df}$ | RMS from PSD | (varies) |
| 7 | $f_2 / f_1 = 2^{1/n}$ | Bandwidth ratio for $1/n$-octave | — |
| 8 | $f_c = \sqrt{f_1 f_2}$ | Geometric center frequency | Hz |
| 9 | $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p/p_0)$ | Sound Pressure Level | dB |
| 10 | $L_w = 10\log_{10}(W/W_0)$ | Sound Power Level | dB |
| 11 | $L_{\text{total}} = 10\log_{10}\sum 10^{L_i/10}$ | Adding incoherent dB levels | dB |
| 12 | $L_{\text{total}} = L_1 + 10\log_{10}(1 + 10^{-\Delta L/10})$ | Adding two levels, $\Delta L = L_1 - L_2$ | dB |
| 13 | $L_{A,\text{eq}} = 10\log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L_A(t)/10} dt\right]$ | Equivalent A-weighted level | dB(A) |
| 14 | $a(f) = (2\pi f)^2 \cdot v(f) = (2\pi f)^4 \cdot d(f)$ | Frequency-domain conversion | m/s², m/s, m |

## 2.5 Frequency Weightings (A, B, C, D, Z)

Sound level meters apply standardized frequency weightings to mimic human perception:

- **A-weighting:** approximates the **40-phon equal-loudness contour**; standard for environmental and occupational noise (e.g., OSHA, ISO). Result in **dB(A)**.
- **B-weighting:** intermediate (rarely used today).
- **C-weighting:** nearly flat up to 1 kHz; used for peak sound pressure and high-level industrial noise. Result in **dB(C)**.
- **D-weighting:** designed for aircraft noise (largely obsolete).
- **Z-weighting:** **zero** (flat, linear) — used for engineering measurements.

The A-weighting correction (in dB) is tabulated at standard frequencies; for example, at 1 kHz the correction is **0 dB**, at 31.5 Hz it is **−39.4 dB**, and at 8 kHz it is **−1.1 dB**.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

| Application | Spectral Insight Used |
|-------------|----------------------|
| Bearing diagnostics | High-frequency resonance ring (BPFO, BPFI frequencies) |
| Balancing rotating machinery | Peak at 1× running speed |
| Gearbox monitoring | Mesh frequency + sidebands (modulation by shaft) |
| Quieting HVAC systems | Identify dominant tone, apply tuned silencer |
| Hearing conservation | A-weighted octave band exposure assessment |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Fourier Series Coefficients

**Problem:** Derive the formula for the Fourier coefficients $a_n$ and $b_n$ of a periodic signal $x(t)$ with period $T$.

**Step 1 — Write the assumed expansion.**

Assume $x(t)$ can be written as:

$$x(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left[a_n \cos(n\omega_0 t) + b_n \sin(n\omega_0 t)\right]$$

where $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T$ is the fundamental angular frequency.

**Step 2 — Find the DC component $a_0$.**

Integrate both sides over one full period $T$:

$$\int_0^T x(t)\, dt = \int_0^T a_0\, dt + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left[a_n \int_0^T \cos(n\omega_0 t)\, dt + b_n \int_0^T \sin(n\omega_0 t)\, dt\right]$$

By orthogonality, all sine and cosine integrals over a complete period vanish, leaving:

$$\int_0^T x(t)\, dt = a_0 \cdot T$$

Solving for $a_0$:

$$a_0 = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T x(t)\, dt$$

This is simply the **time-average (mean) value** of the signal.

**Step 3 — Find $a_n$ by multiplying by $\cos(m\omega_0 t)$ and integrating.**

Multiply the original expansion by $\cos(m\omega_0 t)$ and integrate over $[0, T]$:

$$\int_0^T x(t) \cos(m\omega_0 t)\, dt = a_0 \underbrace{\int_0^T \cos(m\omega_0 t) dt}_{=0} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left[a_n \underbrace{\int_0^T \cos(n\omega_0 t)\cos(m\omega_0 t) dt}_{=0 \text{ for } n\ne m,\, =T/2 \text{ for } n=m} + b_n \underbrace{\int_0^T \sin(n\omega_0 t)\cos(m\omega_0 t) dt}_{=0}\right]$$

Only the $n = m$ term survives, giving:

$$\int_0^T x(t) \cos(m\omega_0 t)\, dt = a_m \cdot \frac{T}{2}$$

Solving for $a_m$:

$$a_m = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^T x(t) \cos(m\omega_0 t)\, dt$$

**Step 4 — Find $b_n$ analogously.**

Multiply by $\sin(m\omega_0 t)$ and integrate. By orthogonality, only the $n = m$ sine term survives with integral value $T/2$:

$$b_m = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^T x(t) \sin(m\omega_0 t)\, dt$$

**Final Compact Result:**

$$\boxed{a_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^T x(t) \cos(n\omega_0 t)\, dt \quad ; \quad b_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^T x(t) \sin(n\omega_0 t)\, dt}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** Each coefficient represents the *projected amplitude* of the signal onto a basis sinusoid of frequency $nf_0$. The squared sum $a_n^2 + b_n^2$ gives twice the mean-square contribution of that harmonic — the foundation of the discrete spectrum.

## 3.2 Derivation: Conversion Between Displacement, Velocity, and Acceleration Spectra

**Problem:** A vibration signal is measured in displacement. Derive the conversion to velocity and acceleration spectra in the frequency domain.

**Step 1 — Time-domain relationships.**

If $x(t)$ is displacement, then:

$$v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} \quad ; \quad a(t) = \frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the differentiation property of the Fourier Transform.**

If $x(t) \leftrightarrow X(f)$, then differentiation in time corresponds to multiplication by $j2\pi f$ in the frequency domain:

$$\mathcal{F}\left\{\frac{dx}{dt}\right\} = j2\pi f \cdot X(f)$$

$$\mathcal{F}\left\{\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}\right\} = (j2\pi f)^2 \cdot X(f) = -(2\pi f)^2 \cdot X(f)$$

**Step 3 — Express the velocity and acceleration spectra.**

$$V(f) = j2\pi f \cdot X(f)$$

$$A(f) = -(2\pi f)^2 \cdot X(f)$$

**Step 4 — Magnitudes (which is what spectra display).**

$$\vert V(f) \vert = 2\pi f \cdot \vert X(f) \vert$$

$$\vert A(f) \vert = (2\pi f)^2 \cdot \vert X(f) \vert$$

**Step 5 — Conversion in dB form.**

In decibel units, multiplication by $2\pi f$ becomes an additive term $20\log_{10}(2\pi f)$:

$$L_v(f) = L_x(f) + 20\log_{10}(2\pi f)\ \text{dB}$$

$$L_a(f) = L_x(f) + 20\log_{10}((2\pi f)^2) = L_x(f) + 40\log_{10}(2\pi f)\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 6 — Verification at $f = 1$ Hz.**

At 1 Hz, $2\pi f = 6.283$, so $20\log_{10}(6.283) = 15.96$ dB. Therefore at 1 Hz, velocity level = displacement level + 15.96 dB, and acceleration level = displacement level + 31.8 dB. These "transition" frequencies are the basis of the well-known "1 g at 1 Hz" rule: $1g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ corresponds to a displacement of $9.81/(2\pi)^2 \approx 0.248$ m at 1 Hz.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion of dB reference levels** must be applied carefully: $1\ \text{g}_{\text{rms}} = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ is the acceleration reference, while displacement reference is $10^{-12}$ m and velocity reference is $10^{-9}$ m/s.

## 3.3 Derivation: Adding Sound Pressure Levels

**Problem:** Two independent sound sources produce 85 dB and 82 dB respectively. Find the combined level.

**Step 1 — Convert from dB to linear power/intensity ratios.**

$$L_i = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_i}{I_0}\right) \implies \frac{I_i}{I_0} = 10^{L_i/10}$$

For $L_1 = 85$ dB: $\quad I_1/I_0 = 10^{8.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{8}$

For $L_2 = 82$ dB: $\quad I_2/I_0 = 10^{8.2} = 1.585 \times 10^{8}$

**Step 2 — Sum the linear intensities (incoherent sources).**

$$I_{\text{tot}}/I_0 = 10^{8.5} + 10^{8.2} = (3.162 + 1.585) \times 10^{8} = 4.747 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 3 — Convert back to dB.**

$$L_{\text{tot}} = 10\log_{10}(4.747 \times 10^{8}) = 86.77\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 4 — Quick approximation using the "add 3 dB rule."**

The dominant source is 85 dB; the difference $\Delta L = 3$ dB. The rule states: when two equal sources add, the level rises by 3 dB; when sources differ by >10 dB, the smaller is ignored; for differences in between, a correction is added. For $\Delta L = 3$ dB, the correction is **+1.8 dB**, giving $85 + 1.8 = 86.8$ dB. ✓

## 3.4 Derivation: Equivalent Continuous A-weighted Level ($L_{\text{Aeq}}$)

**Step 1 — Definition.**

$$L_{\text{eq}} = 10\log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \frac{p_A^2(t)}{p_0^2}\, dt\right]$$

**Step 2 — Show equivalence with energy-average of fluctuating levels.**

Because $L_A(t) = 10\log_{10}[p_A^2(t)/p_0^2]$, it follows that $p_A^2(t)/p_0^2 = 10^{L_A(t)/10}$. Substituting:

$$L_{\text{eq}} = 10\log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L_A(t)/10}\, dt\right]$$

**Step 3 — For discrete samples (field measurement).**

If $N$ samples $L_{A,i}$ are taken at intervals $\Delta t$ over total time $T = N\Delta t$:

$$L_{\text{eq}} = 10\log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} 10^{L_{A,i}/10}\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Significance:** The $L_{\text{eq}}$ formulation is the backbone of all occupational noise exposure calculations under ISO 1999 and Indian Factories Act noise regulations, and the foundation of **Noise Dose** and **Time-Weighted Average (TWA)** computation.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Frequency Domain Quantities Summary

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | Engineering Use |
|----------|--------|-----------|------------------|
| Amplitude Spectrum | $\vert X(f) \vert$ | Magnitude of FT | Identifying dominant tones |
| Phase Spectrum | $\angle X(f)$ | Phase angle of FT | Causal analysis, cross-correlation |
| Power Spectrum | $\vert X(f) \vert^2$ | Squared magnitude | Total energy distribution |
| PSD | $G_x(f)$ | Power per Hz | Random vibration analysis |
| ESD | $E_x(f)$ | Energy per Hz | Transient analysis |
| Cross-spectrum | $G_{xy}(f)$ | Joint frequency content | Coherence, FRF measurement |
| Coherence | $\gamma^2(f)$ | $0 \le \gamma^2 \le 1$ | Linearity check in FRF |
| Transfer Function | $H(f)$ | $Y(f)/X(f)$ | System identification |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Vibration & Noise Signal Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Source: Machine or Environment] --> B[Transducer: Accelerometer or Microphone]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioner: Charge Amplifier or Preamplifier]
    C --> D[Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter]
    D --> E[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    E --> F[Digital Storage: Time Domain Signal x of t]
    F --> G{Analysis Type}
    G -->|Narrowband| H[FFT to X of f]
    G -->|Constant Bandwidth| I[PSD via Welch Method]
    G -->|Fractional Octave| J[1 by n Octave Filter Bank]
    G -->|Time-Frequency| K[STFT or Wavelet Transform]
    H --> L[Spectrum Display: Amplitude vs Frequency]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M{Diagnostic Decision}
    M --> N[Identify Tonal Components]
    M --> O[Identify Broadband Peaks]
    M --> P[Identify Modulation Sidebands]
    M --> Q[Compare with Baseline ISO 10816 or ISO 2631]
    N --> R[Apply Targeted Control: Tuned Absorber or Resonator]
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> S[Compliance Verification: A weighted SPL or Vibration Dose Value]
    R --> T[Re-Measure and Validate]
    S --> T
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Decibel Addition Nomograph Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Two Independent Sources L1 and L2] --> B[Compute Difference Delta L = L1 minus L2]
    B --> C{Delta L Comparison}
    C -->|Delta L = 0 dB| D[Add 3.0 dB]
    C -->|Delta L = 1 dB| E[Add 2.5 dB]
    C -->|Delta L = 2 dB| F[Add 2.1 dB]
    C -->|Delta L = 3 dB| G[Add 1.8 dB]
    C -->|Delta L = 5 dB| H[Add 1.2 dB]
    C -->|Delta L = 10 dB| I[Add 0.4 dB]
    C -->|Delta L greater than 10 dB| J[Neglect Smaller Source]
    D --> K[Final Level L1 plus Correction]
    E --> K
    F --> K
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Frequency-Domain Vibration Conversion Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Displacement Signal d of t] --> B[FFT Processing]
    B --> C[Displacement Spectrum D of f]
    C --> D[Multiply by j times 2 pi times f]
    D --> E[Velocity Spectrum V of f]
    E --> F[Multiply by j times 2 pi times f]
    F --> G[Acceleration Spectrum A of f]
    G --> H[Convert to dB ref 10 power minus 6 m per s squared]
    H --> I[Plot Acceleration Level vs Frequency]
    I --> J[Compare with ISO 10816 Severity Zones]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Octave Band Filter Bank Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Noise Signal p of t] --> B[Bandpass Filter Bank]
    B --> C1[16 Hz Octave Filter]
    B --> C2[31.5 Hz Octave Filter]
    B --> C3[63 Hz Octave Filter]
    B --> C4[125 Hz Octave Filter]
    B --> C5[250 Hz Octave Filter]
    B --> C6[500 Hz Octave Filter]
    B --> C7[1 kHz Octave Filter]
    B --> C8[2 kHz Octave Filter]
    B --> C9[4 kHz Octave Filter]
    B --> C10[8 kHz Octave Filter]
    C1 --> D1[RMS Detector]
    C2 --> D2[RMS Detector]
    C3 --> D3[RMS Detector]
    C4 --> D4[RMS Detector]
    C5 --> D5[RMS Detector]
    C6 --> D6[RMS Detector]
    C7 --> D7[RMS Detector]
    C8 --> D8[RMS Detector]
    C9 --> D9[RMS Detector]
    C10 --> D10[RMS Detector]
    D1 --> E[Display SPL in dB per Octave Band]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    D7 --> E
    D8 --> E
    D9 --> E
    D10 --> E
    E --> F[Apply A Weighting Correction per Band]
    F --> G[Plot A Weighted Octave Band Spectrum]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — Narrowband vs Wideband Distinction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Vibration Signal] --> B{Frequency Resolution}
    B -->|Less than 1 Hz| C[Narrowband Analysis: FFT with high resolution]
    B -->|Several Hz| D[Wideband or Zoom Analysis]
    C --> E[Resolve Closely Spaced Tones: 50 Hz vs 50.5 Hz]
    D --> F[Identify Broadband Energy Distribution]
    E --> G[Diagnose: Sidebands, Beating, Modulation]
    F --> H[Diagnose: Bearing Wear, Aerodynamic Turbulence]
    G --> I[Apply Modulation Domain Analysis: Envelope Spectrum]
    H --> J[Apply Kurtosis or Spectral Kurtosis]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Power Spectral Density. How is it used to compute the overall RMS value of a random vibration signal?**

**Model Answer:**

Power Spectral Density (PSD), denoted $G_x(f)$, describes how the mean-square value of a stationary random signal $x(t)$ is distributed with respect to frequency. It is defined as the limiting average of the squared magnitude of the Fourier Transform normalized by the observation time $T$:

$$G_x(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty}\frac{1}{T}\,\mathbb{E}\!\left[\vert X_T(f) \vert^2\right]$$

Its units are those of the signal squared per Hz (e.g., $(\text{m/s}^2)^2/\text{Hz}$ for acceleration PSD).

The **overall RMS** value of the signal is recovered by integrating the PSD over the entire frequency range (Parseval's theorem for power):

$$x_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\int_{0}^{\infty} G_x(f)\, df}$$

**Engineering Application:** PSD is the standard tool for random vibration specification and testing (e.g., MIL-STD-810, ISO 8041), where the input may be a flat (white-noise) spectrum or shaped spectrum (pink-noise, $1/f$).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is an octave band? Define 1/3-octave band and explain why constant-percentage bandwidths are used in noise measurement.**

**Model Answer:**

An **octave band** is a frequency interval whose upper cutoff $f_2$ is exactly twice its lower cutoff $f_1$ (bandwidth ratio 2:1). The **1/3-octave band** divides each octave into three logarithmically equal intervals, giving a bandwidth ratio $2^{1/3} \approx 1.26$ between $f_2$ and $f_1$. The geometric center frequency is:

$$f_c = \sqrt{f_1 \cdot f_2} = f_1 \cdot 2^{1/6}$$

**Standard 1/3-octave center frequencies (Hz):** 25, 31.5, 40, 50, 63, 80, 100, 125, 160, 200, 250, 315, 400, 500, 630, 800, 1000, 1250, 1600, 2000, ...

**Why constant-percentage bandwidths are used:**

1. **Human hearing is logarithmic** — the ear perceives frequency ratios, not absolute differences. Equal octaves sound equally "spaced."
2. **Machinery spectra span many decades** of frequency (e.g., 1 Hz shaft imbalance to 20 kHz bearing defect); a 1-Hz filter at 1 Hz is too narrow, while the same 1-Hz filter at 10 kHz is impractically wide.
3. **Acoustical materials (porous absorbers, perforated panels)** have frequency responses that scale with the ratio $f/f_c$ of the design center frequency — making logarithmic bands natural descriptors.
4. **International standards** (IEC 61260, ANSI S1.11) mandate octave and 1/3-octave filter specifications, ensuring global comparability of measurements.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the complete frequency analysis procedure for a vibration signal acquired from a faulty industrial pump. Discuss narrowband vs wideband analysis and the role of the FFT. (7 marks)**

**(b) The vibration velocity spectrum of a motor measured in the 1/3-octave band centered at 250 Hz is 12 mm/s RMS. The same motor produces velocity levels of 8 mm/s and 5 mm/s at the 315 Hz and 400 Hz center frequencies respectively. Compute the total RMS velocity, the total velocity level in dB (ref $10^{-9}$ m/s), and discuss what action the engineer should take. (7 marks)**

#### Solution to A(a):

**Step 1 — Acquisition and Conditioning:**

The vibration signal is captured by a piezoelectric accelerometer mounted on the pump bearing housing. The accelerometer produces a charge output proportional to acceleration, which is converted to a voltage signal by a charge amplifier. The signal then passes through an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** (cutoff $f_c \le f_s/2$ where $f_s$ is the sampling rate) to prevent frequency aliasing, before digitization by an ADC at sampling frequency $f_s \ge 2 f_{\max}$ (Nyquist criterion).

**Step 2 — Storage in Time Domain:**

The digitized time series $x(n\Delta t)$ is stored in memory. A typical FFT requires $N = 2^k$ samples (e.g., 4096, 16384).

**Step 3 — FFT Computation:**

The Discrete Fourier Transform is computed:

$$X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j2\pi kn/N}$$

with frequency resolution $\Delta f = f_s/N$. For example, $f_s = 5120$ Hz and $N = 4096$ give $\Delta f = 1.25$ Hz, sufficient to resolve the pump's 1× running speed (say 25 Hz) and 2× vane-pass frequency (say 100 Hz) clearly.

**Step 4 — Narrowband vs Wideband Choice:**

- **Narrowband (high resolution):** $\Delta f < 1$ Hz. Used to resolve individual harmonics and sidebands; ideal for identifying tones and modulation (e.g., bearing defect frequencies).
- **Wideband (lower resolution, faster):** $\Delta f \sim 5$–$10$ Hz. Used for quick surveys and to identify broad resonances or large energy concentrations. Zoom-FFT or waterfall plots extend narrowband analysis over long time spans.

**Step 5 — Diagnostic Interpretation:**

For a pump running at 1500 RPM (25 Hz), one expects:
- 1× peak at 25 Hz (shaft imbalance)
- 2×–3× peaks at 50, 75 Hz (misalignment, looseness)
- Vane-pass frequency at $f_{\text{vp}} = N_{\text{vanes}} \cdot 25$ Hz with sidebands at 25 Hz intervals (cavitation or impeller defect)
- Broadband hump at 1–3 kHz (bearing wear, turbulence)

---

#### Solution to A(b):

**Step 1 — Total RMS velocity from incoherent components:**

Since these are independent narrowband measurements, the total RMS is the square-root of the sum of squares:

$$v_{\text{rms,total}} = \sqrt{v_1^2 + v_2^2 + v_3^2} = \sqrt{12^2 + 8^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{144 + 64 + 25} = \sqrt{233} = 15.26\ \text{mm/s}$$

**Step 2 — Total velocity level in dB:**

Reference velocity: $v_0 = 10^{-9}$ m/s $= 10^{-6}$ mm/s.

$$L_v = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{15.26 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-9}}\right) = 20\log_{10}(1.526 \times 10^{7}) = 20(7.183) = 143.7\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 3 — Individual band levels in dB (for completeness):**

- 250 Hz band: $L_{v1} = 20\log_{10}(12 \times 10^{-3}/10^{-9}) = 141.6$ dB
- 315 Hz band: $L_{v2} = 20\log_{10}(8 \times 10^{-3}/10^{-9}) = 138.1$ dB
- 400 Hz band: $L_{v3} = 20\log_{10}(5 \times 10^{-3}/10^{-9}) = 134.0$ dB

Cross-check: $L_{\text{total}} = 10\log_{10}(10^{141.6/10} + 10^{138.1/10} + 10^{134.0/10}) = 10\log_{10}(1.45 \times 10^{14} + 6.31 \times 10^{13} + 1.0 \times 10^{13}) = 10\log_{10}(2.18 \times 10^{14}) = 143.4$ dB ✓

**Step 4 — Engineering Action:**

Per **ISO 10816-3** (rigid mount, >15 kW machines), 15.26 mm/s RMS in the velocity range places the machine firmly in **Zone D (Damage)** (>11.2 mm/s for large machines) — the pump is in a severe condition requiring **immediate shutdown and corrective action**. The dominant energy at 250 Hz suggests a 2× running frequency or an electrical fault (twice line frequency if the motor is 2-pole 50 Hz → 100 Hz; for 50 Hz 4-pole at 1500 RPM, 2× line = 100 Hz, but if the pump is 6-pole at 1000 RPM, 2× line = 100 Hz — but here the dominant is 250 Hz, possibly an impeller-vane pass tone or a structural resonance excited by 5× shaft rotation). An investigation for looseness, misalignment, or impeller passage-induced excitation is warranted.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) Explain the A, B, C, and Z frequency weightings used in sound level meters. With a sketch, show how the A-weighting curve varies with frequency. State the standard references and one practical application of each. (7 marks)**

**(b) A factory has three machines producing sound pressure levels of 92 dB, 89 dB, and 84 dB (all A-weighted) at the operator's workstation. The exchange rate is 3 dB. Compute: (i) the combined A-weighted SPL; (ii) the noise dose for an 8-hour shift assuming 90 dB(A) is the criterion level; (iii) whether the operator exceeds the OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) of 90 dB(A) for 8 hours. (7 marks)**

#### Solution to B(a):

**Step 1 — Definition of Weightings:**

Frequency weightings are electrical filter networks built into sound level meters that modify the measured RMS sound pressure to approximate the frequency response of the human ear or specific measurement purposes. Standardized in **IEC 61672-1** (sound level meter specification).

- **A-weighting:** Approximates the 40-phon equal-loudness contour (Fletcher-Munson curve). Strongly attenuates low frequencies (<1 kHz) and high frequencies (>5 kHz). Result in **dB(A)**. Universal standard for environmental, occupational, and community noise.
- **B-weighting:** Approximates the 70-phon contour; intermediate between A and C. Largely obsolete today; rarely used in modern practice.
- **C-weighting:** Approximates the 100-phon contour; nearly flat from 31.5 Hz to 8 kHz. Used for measuring **peak sound pressure levels (C-weighted peak)**, high-level industrial impact noise, and assessing the C-A difference (a rough indicator of low-frequency content).
- **Z-weighting:** **Zero** (flat) response; **linear, unweighted** measurement in the range 10 Hz to 20 kHz. Used for engineering and scientific measurements when human response correction is not desired.

**Step 2 — A-Weighting Curve Description (textual representation):**

The A-weighting correction (in dB) relative to 1 kHz is:

| Frequency (Hz) | A-Weighting (dB) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 31.5 | −39.4 |
| 63 | −26.2 |
| 125 | −16.1 |
| 250 | −8.6 |
| 500 | −3.2 |
| 1000 | 0 (reference) |
| 2000 | +1.2 |
| 4000 | +1.0 |
| 8000 | −1.1 |
| 16000 | −6.6 |

The curve has a "high-pass-like" attenuation at low frequencies (correcting for the ear's poor low-frequency sensitivity at low levels) and is approximately flat in the 1–5 kHz range (where the ear is most sensitive).

**Step 3 — Applications:**

- **dB(A):** OSHA permissible exposure limits, EU Environmental Noise Directive, Indian CPCB ambient noise standards, hearing conservation.
- **dB(C):** Peak sound pressure measurement, hearing protector derating (NRR computation), assessment of low-frequency industrial noise (compressors, engines).
- **dB(Z):** Engineering reference data, equipment specification, when comparing with theoretical predictions or other instruments.

---

#### Solution to B(b):

**Step 1 — Combined A-weighted SPL:**

Convert from dB to linear intensity ratios and sum:

$$\frac{I_1}{I_0} = 10^{92/10} = 1.585 \times 10^{9}$$

$$\frac{I_2}{I_0} = 10^{89/10} = 7.943 \times 10^{8}$$

$$\frac{I_3}{I_0} = 10^{84/10} = 2.512 \times 10^{8}$$

Sum: $I_{\text{tot}}/I_0 = (1.585 + 0.7943 + 0.2512) \times 10^{9} = 2.630 \times 10^{9}$

$$L_{\text{total}} = 10\log_{10}(2.630 \times 10^{9}) = 94.2\ \text{dB(A)}$$

(Alternatively, use the addition table: difference between 92 dB and 89 dB is 3 dB → add 1.8 dB to 92 dB = 93.8 dB; then combine with 84 dB (difference 9.8 dB) → add 0.4 dB → 94.2 dB. ✓)

**Step 2 — Noise Dose for 8-hour shift:**

With **3 dB exchange rate** (used by OSHA, NIOSH, and most modern standards; also the KTU-recommended 2024 scheme treatment), the permissible exposure time **halves for every 3 dB increase** above the criterion level.

The formula for time $T$ allowed at level $L$:

$$T = \frac{T_0}{2^{(L - L_c)/3}}$$

where $T_0 = 8$ hours, $L_c = 90$ dB(A) is the criterion level.

For $L_{\text{total}} = 94.2$ dB(A) and $L_c = 90$ dB(A):

$$T = \frac{8}{2^{(94.2 - 90)/3}} = \frac{8}{2^{1.4}} = \frac{8}{2.639} = 3.03\ \text{hours}$$

The **Noise Dose** $D$ is:

$$D(\%) = \frac{100 \cdot C}{T} = \frac{100 \times 8}{3.03} = 264\%$$

**Step 3 — OSHA PEL Compliance Check:**

The OSHA PEL is 90 dB(A) for 8 hours. The combined level at the workstation is **94.2 dB(A)**, and even 1 hour of exposure at this level would deliver more than the daily permissible dose. **The operator exceeds the OSHA PEL by a large margin.** Required actions:

- Engineering controls: enclose the machines, install silencers, vibration-isolation mounts, noise barriers (PPE alone is the last resort).
- Administrative controls: rotate workers, limit shift duration to ≤3 hours.
- Hearing protection: required for the remaining time, with attenuation bringing the protected level below 80 dB(A).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **dB Addition Rule Confusion:** Students often add dB values directly (e.g., 85 dB + 82 dB = 167 dB). **This is WRONG.** You MUST convert to linear power/intensity ratios, sum, and convert back to dB. Marks lost: 2–3 per question.
> 2. **Reference Level Mix-up:** Using $p_0 = 20\ \mu$Pa for SPL but $v_0 = 10^{-9}$ m/s for vibration velocity in the same calculation. Always state the reference value explicitly.
> 3. **Confusing Overall RMS with Peak:** The spectrum plot is typically RMS; "peak" requires the peak-hold or crest factor. KTU questions often ask for the **RMS level**, not peak.
> 4. **Forgetting the $10\log_{10}$ vs $20\log_{10}$ factor:** $10\log$ for power/intensity quantities; $20\log$ for amplitude/pressure quantities. Wrong factor → −6 dB or +6 dB error.
> 5. **Time-Averaging Interpretation:** $L_{\text{eq}}$ is an *energy-equivalent* level, not an arithmetic mean. $L_{\text{eq}} \ge L_{\text{mean}}$ (with equality only when all levels are equal).
> 6. **A-weighting values must be MEMORIZED** for at least 1/1-octave standard frequencies. Many students lose marks by stating "A-weighting equals −10 dB" instead of the correct tabulated values.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Frequency Analysis** decomposes time-domain signals into amplitude vs. frequency components via the **Fourier Transform**; computationally, the **FFT** is the standard tool.
- [x] **$f = N/60$** converts RPM to Hz; **$\omega = 2\pi f$** converts Hz to rad/s.
- [x] **Octave band** ratio $f_2/f_1 = 2$; **1/n-octave band** ratio = $2^{1/n}$; center $f_c = \sqrt{f_1 f_2}$.
- [x] **Standard 1/1-octave centers:** 31.5, 63, 125, 250, 500, 1k, 2k, 4k, 8k, 16k Hz.
- [x] **A-weighting** is mandatory for environmental and occupational noise; **C-weighting** for peak; **Z-weighting** for unweighted.
- [x] **PSD** $G_x(f)$ gives power per Hz; integrates (under Parseval) to give $x_{\text{rms}}^2$.
- [x] **Conversion** between spectra: multiply by $j2\pi f$ for each differentiation step; in dB, $20\log(2\pi f)$ per step.
- [x] **dB addition** uses linear power sums: $L_{\text{tot}} = 10\log_{10}\sum_i 10^{L_i/10}$. Quick rule: +3 dB doubles, +10 dB ×10, +6 dB = 4 equal sources.
- [x] **$L_{\text{eq}}$** is energy-equivalent; **$L_{\text{Aeq}}$** with 3 dB exchange rate is the modern standard.
- [x] **Narrowband** = high frequency resolution ($\Delta f < 1$ Hz) for tone/sideband resolution; **Wideband/Octave** for broadband assessment.
- [x] **Displacement spectrum** emphasizes low frequencies; **velocity** is flat; **acceleration** emphasizes high frequencies — choose the spectrum type to match the diagnostic question.
- [x] **References:** $p_0 = 20\ \mu$Pa, $a_0 = 10^{-6}\ \text{m/s}^2$, $v_0 = 10^{-9}\ \text{m/s}$, $d_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{m}$.
- [x] **Diagnostic Peaks:** 1× shaft = imbalance; 2× = misalignment/looseness; high-frequency ringing = bearing wear; vane/mesh frequency with sidebands = impeller/gear defect.
- [x] **ISO 10816** zones: A (good) < 2.8 mm/s; B (acceptable) < 4.5; C (unsatisfactory) < 7.1; D (damage) > 7.1 (small machines); use ISO 10816-3 for large machines.
- [x] **Remember:** dB values are LOGARITHMIC — always convert to linear, do the operation, then convert back to dB. Never add, subtract, or compare dB directly without conversion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signature Analysis and Condition Monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT632 - INDUSTRIAL NOISE AND VIBRATION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Signature Analysis and Condition Monitoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Signature Analysis and Condition Monitoring

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Signature Analysis** is the diagnostic engineering technique of identifying the operating condition and faults of a rotating/reciprocating machine by comparing its measured vibration response (the "signature") against a known reference baseline or theoretical fault characteristic frequencies in the frequency domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Condition Monitoring (CM)** is a systematic, scheduled (or continuous) process of measuring, trending, and interpreting machine parameters — primarily vibration, but also temperature, acoustic emission, lubricant debris, and performance — to detect incipient degradation, predict remaining useful life, and trigger maintenance only when justified by data.

### Intuitive Overview / Real-World Analogy

Think of a **doctor listening to a patient's heartbeat with a stethoscope**. A healthy heart has a familiar, rhythmic "lub-dub." If the doctor hears a murmur, a click, or an irregular skip, they diagnose the problem without opening the chest. **Signature Analysis is the mechanical equivalent**:

- The **machine body is the patient**.
- The **accelerometer is the stethoscope**.
- The **vibration waveform** is the heartbeat.
- The **frequency spectrum** is the doctor's interpretation — every fault (unbalance, misalignment, bearing defect, gear wear, looseness, cavitation) produces a *distinctive acoustic/vibratory "murmur"* at predictable frequencies.

Condition Monitoring is the **clinic itself** — the workflow of regularly "checking in" on many patients (machines), keeping historical records of their vitals (trend data), and only calling for surgery (overhaul/repair) when a vital sign deteriorates. This is the heart of **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** as opposed to reactive (run-to-failure) or preventive (calendar-based) maintenance.

### Why It Matters in KTU 2024 Industrial Noise & Vibration Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Unplanned downtime in industry can cost **₹5–50 lakh/hour** depending on the plant.
> - Roughly **30–40%** of machine failures originate as vibration anomalies that are detectable weeks before catastrophic failure.
> - Standards like **ISO 10816 / ISO 20816**, **ISO 17359**, and **API 670** govern severity assessment.

### Key Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Standard reference velocity for severity:** $V_{rms} = 4.5 \text{ mm/s}$ (ISO 10816-3 Class I rigid mounts, large machines).
- **Acceleration of gravity:** $\mathbf{g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2}$ (used to convert displacement and acceleration peaks).
- **Logarithmic decibel scale for vibration:** $V_d = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{ref}}\right)$ with $V_{ref} = 10^{-9} \text{ m/s}$.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Vibration Signature — What Is It?

A machine's vibration response, when captured by an accelerometer, is a **time-domain waveform** $x(t)$. The signature is its **frequency-domain representation** $X(f)$ obtained via the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)**. The signature contains:

1. **Synchronous (1×) components** — occur at integer multiples of running speed (unbalance, misalignment).
2. **Sub-synchronous components** — at fractions of running speed (oil whirl, beat frequencies).
3. **Super-synchronous (high-frequency) components** — bearing defect frequencies, gear meshing frequencies, structural resonances.
4. **Sidebands** — modulated sidebands around a carrier frequency indicating modulation sources (e.g., a defective bearing race modulating shaft frequency).

## 2.2 Logic Flow of Signature-Based Diagnosis

- **Step 1 — Acquire**: Mount accelerometer(s) on bearing housings in **radial AND axial** directions.
- **Step 2 — Pre-process**: Anti-alias filter, set sample rate $f_s \geq 2.56 \times f_{max}$ (≥ 2.5× the highest frequency of interest to avoid aliasing).
- **Step 3 — Transform**: Compute FFT; obtain amplitude spectrum, phase spectrum, power spectral density.
- **Step 4 — Identify Peaks**: Compare peak frequencies against the machine's theoretical fault frequency list (BPFO, BPFI, BSF, FTF, GMF, vane pass frequency, etc.).
- **Step 5 — Trend**: Track peak amplitudes (typically in *g* RMS or *g* peak) over time.
- **Step 6 — Diagnose**: Apply ISO 10816 severity zones; trigger maintenance action.
- **Step 7 — Verify**: Compare post-repair signature against pre-fault baseline.

## 2.3 Why and How of Each Step

| Step | Why? | How? |
|---|---|---|
| Acquire | Captures the "voice" of the machine | IEPE / charge-mode accelerometers, sampling at ≥ 2× Nyquist |
| Pre-process | Removes artifacts (aliasing, drift) | Low-pass filter (Bessel, Butterworth), integration to velocity/displacement |
| Transform | Reveals hidden periodicities | FFT with windowing (Hanning), averaging (linear or exponential) |
| Identify | Locates fault frequencies | Compare with calculated BPFI/BPFO/BSF, order analysis |
| Trend | Determines rate of degradation | Statistical alarms (statistical process control), dB/month rise |
| Diagnose | Quantifies severity | ISO 10816/20816 zones A/B/C/D |
| Verify | Confirms repair efficacy | Post-fix signature must resemble baseline |

## 2.4 Bearing and Gear Fault Frequencies — The Heart of Signature Analysis

For a **rolling-element bearing** with $N$ rolling elements, ball diameter $d$, pitch diameter $D$, contact angle $\alpha$, and shaft rotational frequency $f_r$ (Hz):

$$
\text{BPFI} = \frac{N}{2} \cdot f_r \cdot \left(1 + \frac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right) \quad \text{(Inner Race)}
$$

$$
\text{BPFO} = \frac{N}{2} \cdot f_r \cdot \left(1 - \frac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right) \quad \text{(Outer Race)}
$$

$$
\text{BSF} = \frac{D}{2d} \cdot f_r \cdot \left(1 - \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^2 \cos^2\alpha\right) \quad \text{(Ball Spin)}
$$

$$
\text{FTF} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot f_r \cdot \left(1 - \frac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right) \quad \text{(Cage / Fundamental Train)}
$$

For a **gear pair** with $N_1$ teeth on driving gear, $N_2$ teeth on driven gear, shaft frequency $f_1$ on pinion:

$$
\text{GMF} = N_1 \cdot f_1 = N_2 \cdot f_2 \quad \text{(Gear Mesh Frequency)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KEY insight for KTU exams**: every fault has a *theoretical* characteristic frequency. The signature analysis technique *matches measured peaks with these theoretical frequencies* to identify the fault. You do NOT need to memorize all derivations — but you must know the formula forms and what each represents.

## 2.5 The Four Pillars of Condition Monitoring (Condition Monitoring Techniques)

1. **Vibration Monitoring** — most widely used; covers broadband RMS, spectral peaks, shock pulse, enveloping.
2. **Acoustic Emission (AE)** — high-frequency stress waves (20 kHz–1 MHz); detects crack initiation.
3. **Lubricant Analysis** — wear-debris analysis (spectrographic, ferrography).
4. **Performance / Thermography** — temperature, pressure, flow, power consumption trends.

> [!NOTE]
> Modern integrated CM systems use **machine learning** to classify signatures automatically (supervised classifiers trained on labelled fault data).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> This is the **high-yield formula table** for any numerical or derivation problem on this topic. Master it.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Symbol | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shaft unbalance force | $F$ | $F = m \cdot e \cdot \omega^2$ | N (Newton) | Centrifugal force of mass $m$ at radius $e$ spinning at $\omega$ rad/s |
| 2 | Unbalance displacement | $x$ | $x = \dfrac{m \cdot e}{M}$ | m | Static displacement of equivalent mass $M$ |
| 3 | Natural frequency | $f_n$ | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Hz | Single-DOF system stiffness $k$, mass $m$ |
| 4 | Bearing BPFO | $f_{BPFO}$ | $\dfrac{N}{2} f_r \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right)$ | Hz | Outer race defect frequency |
| 5 | Bearing BPFI | $f_{BPFI}$ | $\dfrac{N}{2} f_r \left(1 + \dfrac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right)$ | Hz | Inner race defect frequency |
| 6 | Bearing BSF | $f_{BSF}$ | $\dfrac{D}{2d} f_r \left(1 - \left(\dfrac{d}{D}\right)^2 \cos^2\alpha\right)$ | Hz | Ball spin frequency |
| 7 | Bearing FTF | $f_{FTF}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2} f_r \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right)$ | Hz | Cage (train) frequency |
| 8 | Gear mesh frequency | $f_{GMF}$ | $N_1 f_1 = N_2 f_2$ | Hz | Meshing frequency of gear pair |
| 9 | Vane pass frequency | $f_{VPF}$ | $N_v \cdot f_r$ | Hz | Number of vanes $N_v$ × shaft frequency |
| 10 | Severity (velocity) | $V_{rms}$ | per ISO 10816 zones A/B/C/D | mm/s | RMS vibration velocity severity |
| 11 | Acceleration → displacement | $x$ | $x = \dfrac{a}{(2\pi f)^2}$ | m | From $a = (2\pi f)^2 x$ |
| 12 | Acceleration → velocity | $v$ | $v = \dfrac{a}{2\pi f}$ | m/s | Integration relation |
| 13 | Crest factor | $CF$ | $CF = \dfrac{x_{peak}}{x_{rms}}$ | dimensionless | Indicator of impacts (CF > 6 for bearings in early damage) |
| 14 | Kurtosis | $K$ | $K = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^4}{\left(\dfrac{1}{N}\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2\right)^2}$ | dimensionless | Higher-order statistic — spikes when impulses appear |
| 15 | Vibration level (dB) | $L_v$ | $L_v = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{v_{rms}}{v_0}\right)$ with $v_0 = 10^{-9}$ m/s | dB | Logarithmic severity scale |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crest factor and Kurtosis are critical indicators of incipient bearing failure** — even when broadband RMS is still within ISO 10816 limits, $K > 3$ or $CF > 6$ reveals early-stage impulses that RMS misses.

## 2.7 ISO 10816-3 Vibration Severity Zones (Class I — Rigid Foundation, Machines > 300 kW)

| Zone | RMS Velocity Range | Status |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | $0 < V_{rms} \leq 1.4 \text{ mm/s}$ | Good — newly commissioned |
| **B** | $1.4 < V_{rms} \leq 2.8 \text{ mm/s}$ | Acceptable for long-term operation |
| **C** | $2.8 < V_{rms} \leq 4.5 \text{ mm/s}$ | Unacceptable for long-term; plan maintenance |
| **D** | $V_{rms} > 4.5 \text{ mm/s}$ | Severe — immediate action; damage likely |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power plants**: Generator bearing monitoring avoids catastrophic rotor-to-stator rub.
- **Aerospace**: Helicopter gearbox CM using AE prevents mid-flight failure.
- **Paper mills**: Dryer-section roller bearing faults detected via envelope demodulation.
- **Marine**: Propeller shaft CM using shaft-line absolute vibration (axial) monitoring.
- **Wind turbines**: Gearbox CM in remote nacelles via wireless IoT accelerometers.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Centrifugal Force from Unbalance (Why 1× peak indicates unbalance)

When a mass $m$ is offset from the rotation axis by an eccentricity $e$ and the shaft rotates at angular velocity $\omega$:

**Step 1 — Position of the unbalance mass**

$$
x(t) = e \cos(\omega t)
$$

**Step 2 — Velocity (first derivative w.r.t. time)**

$$
v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = -e\omega \sin(\omega t)
$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration (second derivative)**

$$
a(t) = \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -e\omega^2 \cos(\omega t)
$$

The negative sign indicates the acceleration is always directed toward the rotation axis (centripetal), but the reactive force on the bearing is **outward** (centrifugal), equal in magnitude.

**Step 4 — Centrifugal force magnitude**

$$
F = m \cdot \vert a(t) \vert = m \cdot e \cdot \omega^2
$$

Since $\omega = 2\pi f_r$, where $f_r$ is the rotational frequency in Hz:

$$
F = m \cdot e \cdot (2\pi f_r)^2 = 4\pi^2 m \cdot e \cdot f_r^2
$$

**Step 5 — Conversion to engineering units (note $\omega^2$ proportionality)**

> [!NOTE]
> This is why unbalance vibration amplitude **scales as the SQUARE of speed**. Doubling the rotational speed quadruples the unbalance vibration force. A machine that is "fine" at 1500 RPM may vibrate violently at 3000 RPM — even with the same physical unbalance.

## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Displacement, Velocity and Acceleration (Pure Sinusoid)

For a pure sinusoidal vibration $x(t) = X_p \sin(2\pi f t)$:

**Step 1 — Differentiate to get velocity**

$$
v(t) = X_p (2\pi f) \cos(2\pi f t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_p = X_p \cdot (2\pi f)
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate again to get acceleration**

$$
a(t) = -X_p (2\pi f)^2 \sin(2\pi f t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_p = X_p \cdot (2\pi f)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Useful relations (with RMS conversions $V_{rms} = V_p/\sqrt{2}$, etc.)**

$$
V_{rms} = 2\pi f \cdot X_{rms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad X_{rms} = \frac{V_{rms}}{2\pi f}
$$

$$
A_{rms} = (2\pi f)^2 \cdot X_{rms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad X_{rms} = \frac{A_{rms}}{(2\pi f)^2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical use**: A low-frequency vibration problem (say 25 Hz, an unbalance peak) is best measured as **displacement** (large amplitudes). A high-frequency problem (say 5 kHz bearing defect) is best measured as **acceleration** (tiny amplitudes but high frequency content). The accelerometer's integration electronics automatically converts to velocity for ISO 10816 evaluation.

## 3.3 Derivation: Crest Factor and Kurtosis as Impulse Detectors

**Step 1 — Crest Factor (CF)**

$$
CF = \frac{X_{peak}}{X_{rms}}
$$

For a pure sinusoid, $CF = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.

For Gaussian random vibration, $CF \approx 3$ to 4.

**Step 2 — When a bearing develops a discrete defect (a spall)**, each roller strike on the spall creates an impulsive transient. The broadband RMS rises slowly, but the **peak value spikes**. Hence $CF$ rises from ~3 toward **6 to 10** as impulses dominate.

**Step 3 — Kurtosis** is the standardized fourth central moment:

$$
K = \frac{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^4}{\left[\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2\right]^2}
$$

For Gaussian data, $K = 3$. Impulsive data yields $K > 3$, often $K = 5$ to $10$ incipient, and $K$ may fall back toward 3 as defects become distributed (background) rather than discrete.

> [!NOTE]
> In a KTU answer, always explain the **statistical meaning**: CF and Kurtosis *complement* RMS — RMS is sensitive to energy but blind to impulsiveness; CF and Kurtosis are sensitive to impulsiveness.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-style 7-mark problem)

**Question:** A 6308-series deep-groove ball bearing has the following geometry: $N = 8$ balls, $d = 15.875 \text{ mm}$, $D = 65 \text{ mm}$, contact angle $\alpha = 0°$. The shaft rotates at 3000 RPM. Calculate the BPFO, BPFI, BSF, and FTF in Hz.

**Step 1 — Shaft rotational frequency**

$$
f_r = \frac{3000 \text{ RPM}}{60} = 50 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Geometric ratios**

$$
\frac{d}{D} = \frac{15.875}{65} = 0.2442
$$

Since $\alpha = 0°$, $\cos\alpha = 1$.

**Step 3 — BPFO**

$$
\text{BPFO} = \frac{8}{2} \cdot 50 \cdot \left(1 - 0.2442 \cdot 1\right) = 4 \cdot 50 \cdot 0.7558 = 151.16 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — BPFI**

$$
\text{BPFI} = \frac{8}{2} \cdot 50 \cdot \left(1 + 0.2442 \cdot 1\right) = 4 \cdot 50 \cdot 1.2442 = 248.84 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 5 — FTF**

$$
\text{FTF} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 50 \cdot (1 - 0.2442) = 25 \cdot 0.7558 = 18.895 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 6 — BSF**

$$
\text{BSF} = \frac{65}{2 \cdot 15.875} \cdot 50 \cdot \left[1 - (0.2442)^2 \cdot 1\right]
$$

$$
\text{BSF} = \frac{65}{31.75} \cdot 50 \cdot (1 - 0.0597) = 2.0472 \cdot 50 \cdot 0.9403 = 96.25 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation**

| Frequency | Value | When Measured in Spectrum |
|---|---|---|
| $f_r$ | 50 Hz | Dominant in unbalance |
| BPFO | 151.16 Hz | Outer race defect harmonic train |
| BPFI | 248.84 Hz | Inner race defect (often with 1× sidebands) |
| FTF | 18.90 Hz | Cage defect / looseness indicator |
| BSF | 96.25 Hz | Ball defect (rare; requires inner/outer race modulation) |

## 3.5 Envelope Analysis (Demodulation) — Worked Concept

**Step 1 — Acquire high-frequency time waveform $x(t)$.**

**Step 2 — Band-pass filter** around the structural resonance where bearing impacts excite the housing (typically 1–20 kHz). This removes low-frequency 1× and GMF.

**Step 3 — Compute the analytic signal** $x_a(t) = x(t) + j \cdot H[x(t)]$ where $H$ is the Hilbert transform.

**Step 4 — Envelope** $= \vert x_a(t) \vert$.

**Step 5 — FFT of the envelope** reveals low-frequency defect signatures (BPFO/BPFI/BSF/FTF) **even when the structural resonance is excited**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why envelope?** Because impulsive bearing impacts are masked by broadband noise in the raw spectrum. Demodulating around a resonance lifts the impulses out, making them visible in the spectrum of the envelope.

## 3.6 Order Analysis — When Shaft Speed Varies

**Step 1 — Install a tachometer or use a phase-locked key-phasor pulse.**

**Step 2 — Compute instantaneous shaft frequency $f_r(t)$ at every sample using the key-phasor.**

**Step 3 — Resample the vibration data so that the time axis becomes angle in revolutions.**

**Step 4 — FFT against order (revolutions) rather than frequency.** Peaks then appear at fixed orders (1.0, 2.0, 3.0…) regardless of speed — ideal for variable-speed machines like wind turbines, compressors, and automotive drivetrains.

## 3.7 Cepstrum Analysis (For Identifying Sideband Families)

The **cepstrum** $C(\tau)$ is the inverse FFT of the log-magnitude spectrum:

$$
C(\tau) = \text{IFT}\left[\log \vert X(f)\vert\right]
$$

It identifies **periodic families of sidebands**. A cepstrum peak at quefrency $\tau$ indicates sidebands spaced $1/\tau$ apart. Useful for gearbox diagnosis where multiple gears mesh and produce families of sidebands.

---

# Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Condition Monitoring System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    machine1[Rotating Machine Under Test] --> sensor1[Accelerometer mounted on bearing housing]
    machine1 --> sensor2[Tachometer key phasor]
    machine1 --> sensor3[Temperature RTD PT100]
    sensor1 --> preamp1[Charge amplifier or IEPE conditioner]
    preamp1 --> filter1[Anti alias low pass filter]
    filter1 --> adc1[Analog to Digital Converter]
    adc1 --> fft1[FFT processor and Order Analysis]
    sensor2 --> keyp1[Key phasor pulse conditioner]
    keyp1 --> fft1
    fft1 --> feature1[Feature extraction: RMS peak crest factor kurtosis]
    feature1 --> trend1[Trending database historical record]
    trend1 --> alarm1[Alarm engine ISO 10816 thresholds]
    alarm1 --> output1[Operator HMI and maintenance ticket]
    sensor3 --> therm1[Temperature trending module]
    therm1 --> trend1
    style machine1 fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style fft1 fill:#bee1f5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style alarm1 fill:#ffe066,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style output1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Decision Logic Flow for Fault Diagnosis via Signature

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Start: Acquire vibration spectrum] --> peak1{Identify dominant peak}
    peak1 -- 1x shaft frequency --> f1[Suspect Unbalance or Shaft Bow]
    peak1 -- 2x shaft frequency --> f2[Suspect Misalignment or Looseness]
    peak1 -- High frequency BPFO --> f3[Outer Race Defect confirm with envelope]
    peak1 -- High frequency BPFI --> f4[Inner Race Defect expect 1x sidebands]
    peak1 -- GMF and sidebands --> f5[Gear Mesh problem check sideband spacing]
    peak1 -- Random broadband with CF greater than 6 --> f6[Early Stage Bearing Damage confirm with Kurtosis]
    peak1 -- Sub synchronous 0.4x to 0.5x --> f7[Oil Whirl or Rub]
    f1 --> act1[ISO 10816 zone check and dynamic balance]
    f2 --> act2[Laser alignment and check bolt torque]
    f3 --> act3[Replace bearing plan downtime]
    f4 --> act4[Replace bearing inspect shaft fit]
    f5 --> act5[Gear inspection and lubrication]
    f6 --> act6[Schedule bearing replacement monitor weekly]
    f7 --> act7[Inspect bearing clearance and seals]
    style start1 fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style peak1 fill:#ffe066,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style act3 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style act4 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Component Hierarchy of a Signature Analyzer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputA[INPUT LAYER]
        i1[Vibration transducer]
        i2[Speed reference]
        i3[Process variables]
    end
    subgraph digitA[DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING LAYER]
        d1[Anti alias filter]
        d2[ADC and windowing]
        d3[FFT and zoom FFT]
        d4[Order tracking]
        d5[Envelope demodulation]
        d6[Cepstrum and sideband analysis]
    end
    subgraph featureA[FEATURE AND DIAGNOSIS LAYER]
        fe1[RMS peak crest factor kurtosis]
        fe2[Fault frequency calculator BPFI BPFO BSF FTF GMF]
        fe3[Pattern classifier rule based or ML]
    end
    subgraph outputA[OUTPUT LAYER]
        o1[Real time alarms]
        o2[Trend database and dashboards]
        o3[CMMS work order auto generate]
    end
    inputA --> digitA --> featureA --> outputA
    style i1 fill:#bee1f5
    style fe3 fill:#ffe066
    style o3 fill:#c8e6c9
```
