# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define **signature analysis** in the context of industrial vibration monitoring.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Signature analysis is the diagnostic engineering technique used to assess the operating condition of a rotating or reciprocating machine by examining its characteristic vibration response — the "signature" — in the frequency domain. Each type of mechanical fault (unbalance, misalignment, bearing defect, gear wear, looseness, cavitation, etc.) generates vibration energy at a predictable, theoretically calculable frequency. By acquiring the vibration time waveform with an accelerometer and converting it to a frequency spectrum via the **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)**, an engineer compares the observed spectral peaks against the theoretical fault frequency list and the machine's baseline. A match confirms the fault. Signature analysis thus converts a complex vibration signal into actionable fault-specific information.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] State the **ISO 10816-3** vibration severity zones for a Class I rigid-mounted machine of rated power above 300 kW. Mention the corrective action for each zone.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

ISO 10816-3 Class I severity zones based on RMS vibration velocity $V_{rms}$:

- **Zone A** — $0 < V_{rms} \leq 1.4 \text{ mm/s}$: Newly commissioned machines; **no action** required.
- **Zone B** — $1.4 < V_{rms} \leq 2.8 \text{ mm/s}$: Acceptable for **unrestricted long-term operation**; routine trend monitoring.
- **Zone C** — $2.8 < V_{rms} \leq 4.5 \text{ mm/s}$: **Unacceptable for long-term**; corrective action (re-balance, alignment) should be planned within a defined maintenance window.
- **Zone D** — $V_{rms} > 4.5 \text{ mm/s}$: **Severe vibration causing damage**; immediate shutdown and remedial action to prevent catastrophic failure.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Part (a)** [7 marks]

A 6205-ZZ deep-groove ball bearing has $N = 9$ rolling elements, ball diameter $d = 7.94 \text{ mm}$, pitch diameter $D = 39 \text{ mm}$, contact angle $\alpha = 0°$. The shaft runs at 1450 RPM. Determine (i) BPFO, (ii) BPFI, (iii) FTF, and (iv) BSF in Hz. Identify which harmonic peak in the spectrum would correspond to an **outer race defect** and which to an **inner race defect**.

**Step 1 — Shaft frequency**

$$
f_r = \frac{1450}{60} = 24.167 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Geometric ratio**

$$
\frac{d}{D} = \frac{7.94}{39} = 0.2036
$$

**Step 3 — BPFO**

$$
f_{BPFO} = \frac{9}{2} \cdot 24.167 \cdot (1 - 0.2036) = 4.5 \cdot 24.167 \cdot 0.7964 = 86.62 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — BPFI**

$$
f_{BPFI} = \frac{9}{2} \cdot 24.167 \cdot (1 + 0.2036) = 4.5 \cdot 24.167 \cdot 1.2036 = 130.93 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 5 — FTF**

$$
f_{FTF} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 24.167 \cdot (1 - 0.2036) = 12.083 \cdot 0.7964 = 9.62 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 6 — BSF**

$$
f_{BSF} = \frac{39}{2 \cdot 7.94} \cdot 24.167 \cdot \left[1 - (0.2036)^2\right]
$$

$$
f_{BSF} = \frac{39}{15.88} \cdot 24.167 \cdot (1 - 0.0415) = 2.456 \cdot 24.167 \cdot 0.9585 = 56.90 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 7 — Identification**

A vibration peak at **86.62 Hz** (and its harmonics: $2 \times 86.62 = 173.24$ Hz, $3 \times 86.62 = 259.86$ Hz, etc.) indicates an **outer race defect**. A peak at **130.93 Hz** (typically accompanied by $1 \times f_r$ sidebands because the defect location on the inner race moves with the shaft) indicates an **inner race defect**.

**Part (b)** [7 marks]

Explain with suitable diagrams the procedure of **envelope analysis (amplitude demodulation)** for detecting incipient bearing defects. Why is the RMS broadband vibration value alone insufficient for early-stage bearing fault detection?

**Step 1 — Statement of the Problem**

In its early stage, a localized bearing defect produces short, low-energy impulsive impacts at the defect pass-rate (BPFO/BPFI). These impacts excite the structural resonances of the bearing housing and machine frame. However, the impacts are masked by much larger broadband background vibration in the raw spectrum, and broadband RMS rises only slowly because the defect impulses contribute very little total energy. Therefore RMS alone cannot detect the early stage.

**Step 2 — Envelope Procedure**

1. Acquire high-frequency time-domain waveform (sampling rate ≥ 25 kHz).
2. Apply a **band-pass filter** around the chosen structural resonance frequency (commonly between 1–20 kHz, depending on machine).
3. Compute the **analytic signal** $x_a(t) = x(t) + jH[x(t)]$ where $H[\cdot]$ is the Hilbert transform.
4. Extract the **envelope** $E(t) = \vert x_a(t)\vert$.
5. Take the FFT of $E(t)$. The spectrum now reveals the **defect frequency** (BPFO/BPFI) and its harmonics as clear low-frequency peaks, free of the structural resonance carrier.

**Step 3 — Reasoning**

Because the impulsive impacts modulate the structural resonance amplitude (at the defect pass-rate), demodulation (rectification + low-pass) recovers the modulation envelope, which contains the defect frequency information. This converts a "hidden" impact signal into a clear low-frequency peak in the envelope spectrum.

**Step 4 — Supplementary Indicators**

Modern analyzers also use **crest factor** ($CF = x_{peak}/x_{rms}$, normal ≈ 3 for Gaussian, > 6 indicates impulses) and **kurtosis** (normal 3 for Gaussian, > 5 indicates impulsiveness). These statistical indicators, together with envelope spectrum, allow bearing defects to be detected 3–6 months before broadband RMS crosses ISO 10816 alarm thresholds.

**Diagram reference:** See SECTION 4.2 — Decision Logic Flow.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b)**: Most students lose marks by describing envelope analysis but **forgetting to justify why RMS fails**. Always include the sentence: *"RMS is dominated by total signal energy, so a few low-energy impulsive impacts barely change the RMS value; however crest factor and the envelope spectrum expose these impulses clearly."* This single statement is worth 2 marks.

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

**Part (a)** [7 marks]

With a labelled block diagram, describe the major components of a **vibration-based condition monitoring system** for an industrial motor-pump set. Explain the role of each block.

**Step 1 — Sensor Layer**

- **Accelerometers** (piezoelectric IEPE type) mounted radially and axially on the motor and pump bearing housings.
- **Tachometer / key-phasor** providing once-per-revolution pulse for speed reference and order analysis.
- **Temperature sensors (RTD)** on bearing housings as auxiliary indicator.

**Step 2 — Signal Conditioning**

- Charge amplifiers or IEPE signal conditioners to amplify and normalize the accelerometer output.
- Anti-alias low-pass filter (Bessel or Butterworth, cut-off = 0.4 × sampling rate).

**Step 3 — Data Acquisition and DSP**

- ADC with sampling rate satisfying $f_s \geq 2.56 \times f_{max}$.
- FFT analyzer computing amplitude spectrum, phase spectrum, and order spectrum.

**Step 4 — Feature Extraction**

- Computation of RMS, peak, crest factor, kurtosis over user-defined frequency bands.

**Step 5 — Fault-Frequency Reference Module**

- Calculator generating BPFO, BPFI, BSF, FTF, GMF, and vane pass frequency for the entered machine geometry.

**Step 6 — Trending and Alarm**

- Time-series database storing historical features.
- Alarm engine comparing features against ISO 10816 zones and customizable thresholds.

**Step 7 — Output**

- HMI dashboard, alarm log, and CMMS (Computerized Maintenance Management System) auto-generation of work orders.

**Diagram reference:** See SECTION 4.1 and SECTION 4.3 — the block diagrams already satisfy this requirement.

**Part (b)** [7 marks]

A centrifugal pump operating at **3000 RPM** is fitted with two bearings identical to the one in Part A of Question B (same geometry). The radial vibration measured on the drive-end bearing housing shows spectral peaks at the following frequencies (with amplitudes in $\text{mm/s}$ RMS): **50 Hz = 1.2, 100 Hz = 4.6, 151 Hz = 0.8, 249 Hz = 2.1, 248 Hz sidebands of ±50 Hz = 1.5.** Apply ISO 10816-3 severity criteria and signature analysis rules to diagnose the machine condition. Justify your answer.

**Step 1 — Shaft frequency**

$$
f_r = \frac{3000}{60} = 50 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute expected fault frequencies (using Part A geometry)**

$$
f_{BPFO} \approx 151 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_{BPFI} \approx 249 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — ISO 10816-3 severity for total RMS velocity**

Dominant peak is $4.6 \text{ mm/s}$ at $2 \times f_r$ → RMS velocity in Zone C/D boundary. The **total RMS vibration magnitude** (broadband) is approximately $\sqrt{1.2^2 + 4.6^2 + 0.8^2 + 2.1^2 + 1.5^2} \approx 5.43 \text{ mm/s}$ → **Zone D (severe)**.

**Step 4 — Signature interpretation**

| Peak | Diagnosis |
|---|---|
| 50 Hz (1.2 mm/s) | 1× shaft = minor unbalance or shaft bow |
| 100 Hz (4.6 mm/s, dominant) | 2× shaft = **angular misalignment** of coupling OR mechanical looseness |
| 151 Hz (0.8 mm/s) | Matches BPFO → early **outer race defect** on drive-end bearing |
| 249 Hz (2.1 mm/s) with ±50 Hz sidebands (1.5 mm/s) | Matches BPFI with $1 \times f_r$ sidebands → **inner race defect** on drive-end bearing |

**Step 5 — Conclusion**

The machine exhibits **multiple simultaneous faults**: (i) angular misalignment (dominant 2× peak), (ii) inner race defect (BPFI + sidebands), (iii) outer race defect (BPFO), (iv) minor unbalance. ISO 10816-3 velocity severity is **Zone D**, demanding **immediate shutdown** for inspection. Recommended actions in order: (a) laser-align the coupling, (b) replace the drive-end bearing, (c) dynamically balance the rotor, (d) re-test and trend.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b)**: Students commonly forget to **sum the RMS contributions** (using root-sum-of-squares, not arithmetic sum) to obtain the broadband ISO 10816 severity value. Showing the calculation $\sqrt{\sum V_i^2}$ earns you 1 mark. Also, mentioning that **BPFI produces shaft-frequency sidebands** while BPFO does not (because the inner race rotates with the shaft) is a high-yield distinction worth 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist** for Signature Analysis and Condition Monitoring:

- **Signature analysis** = FFT-based identification of fault peaks in machine vibration spectrum.
- **Condition monitoring** = the umbrella workflow; signature analysis is one tool inside it.
- The **four key bearing fault frequencies**: BPFO (outer race), BPFI (inner race), BSF (ball defect), FTF (cage).
- BPFI is accompanied by **$1 \times f_r$ sidebands**; BPFO is generally **not sidebanded** (since outer race is stationary).
- **Gear mesh frequency (GMF)** = $N_1 f_1 = N_2 f_2$. Sidebands around GMF indicate modulation by gear-shaft or gear-gear interactions.
- **Crest factor** rises from ~3 to > 6 in early bearing damage; **kurtosis** rises from 3 to > 5.
- **ISO 10816-3 Class I zones**: A: 0–1.4, B: 1.4–2.8, C: 2.8–4.5, D: > 4.5 mm/s RMS velocity.
- **Envelope analysis (demodulation)** is the standard technique for early-stage bearing defect detection.
- **Order analysis** is essential for variable-speed machines (resample against shaft angle).
- **Cepstrum analysis** detects periodic sideband families (gearbox diagnosis).
- **Centrifugal force** $F = m e \omega^2$ explains why unbalance scales with **$\omega^2$** (squared).
- **Acceleration, velocity, displacement** are related by factors of $2\pi f$ — pick the right metric for the fault frequency range (acceleration for high, displacement for low).
- **Acoustic emission (AE)** catches cracks earlier than vibration; **oil/ferrography** catches wear particles.
- **Statistical indicators** (crest factor, kurtosis) *complement* RMS — they reveal impulsiveness that RMS hides.
- **Trending** the same metric over months (not just spot-checking) is what distinguishes condition monitoring from mere vibration measurement.
- **ISO 20816** is the modern replacement nomenclature for ISO 10816 (same zone structure, broader machine class coverage).
- **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** relies entirely on condition monitoring data; it replaces calendar-based preventive maintenance.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU exam mnemonic for fault frequencies**:
> **"OPALS-B"** → **O**uter race, **P**inion-side race (Inner), **A**xial (cage), **L**ocked (BSF — ball spin), **S**haft frequency. Use this to remember which formula is which!

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors Used in Vibration and Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT632 - INDUSTRIAL NOISE AND VIBRATION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensors Used in Vibration and Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **vibration sensor** (or **vibration transducer**) is an electromechanical device that converts a mechanical vibratory input — displacement, velocity, or acceleration — into a proportional electrical output signal, enabling quantitative evaluation of vibration amplitude, frequency, and phase in machines, structures, and industrial equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Classification Mandate:** Vibration sensors are categorised into two top-level families:
> 1. **Absolute (Seismic) Sensors** — measure motion *relative to an inertial frame* (e.g., accelerometers, velocity pickups).
> 2. **Relative (Non-Seismic) Sensors** — measure motion of a target *relative to the sensor housing* (e.g., eddy-current proximity probes, LVDTs, capacitive probes).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Floating Cork Analogy"** — Imagine a cork floating inside a sealed glass jar of water mounted on a boat. When the boat rocks on waves, the cork *appears* to move *opposite* to the boat's motion inside the jar. If you can measure the cork's position *relative to the jar's wall*, you have an **absolute motion sensor** for the boat. This is exactly how a **seismic mass** inside an accelerometer works: the mass is the "cork" (held by a spring-damper), and the case is the "jar" (mounted on the vibrating machine).

## 1.3 Why Industrial Vibration Measurement Matters

- **Condition monitoring** of rotating machinery (motors, pumps, turbines, gearboxes).
- **Predictive maintenance** to detect imbalance, misalignment, looseness, and bearing defects.
- **Structural health monitoring** of buildings, bridges, and aerospace components.
- **Quality control** in manufacturing (surface finish, machining chatter).
- **Regulatory compliance** for occupational health and safety against hand-arm or whole-body vibration exposure.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold-Coded for Recall)

- **Sensitivity:** V/(m/s²) or pC/(m/s²) — output per unit input.
- **Frequency Range:** Hz to kHz — operating bandwidth.
- **Dynamic Range:** dB — ratio of largest to smallest measurable signal.
- **Resonant Frequency:** Hz — mechanical natural frequency of the sensing element.
- **Cross-Axis Sensitivity:** % — error from vibration orthogonal to the sensing axis.
- **Operating Temperature:** °C — environmental tolerance.
- **Linearity:** % — deviation from ideal linear output.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Principle — The Seismic Mass Model

Almost all absolute vibration sensors are based on a **mass–spring–damper** system. The key insight is that a proof mass suspended by a spring and dashpot inside a rigid case will exhibit a measurable **relative displacement** when the case is attached to a vibrating structure.

Let:
- $m$ = proof mass (kg)
- $c$ = damping coefficient (N·s/m)
- $k$ = spring stiffness (N/m)
- $y(t)$ = absolute displacement of the vibrating structure (input)
- $x(t)$ = absolute displacement of the proof mass
- $z(t) = x(t) - y(t)$ = relative displacement across the spring (output)

### Newton's Second Law on the Mass

The mass experiences spring and damper forces from the case (which moves with $y$):

$$m \ddot{x} = -k(x - y) - c(\dot{x} - \dot{y})$$

Substituting $z = x - y$ and noting $\ddot{x} = \ddot{z} + \ddot{y}$:

$$m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = -m \ddot{y}$$

This is the **canonical seismic-mass equation**: the relative displacement $z$ is forced by the inertial term $-m\ddot{y}$, which is the structure's acceleration.

## 2.2 Frequency Response Characterisation

Define the **undamped natural frequency** $\omega_n$ and the **damping ratio** $\zeta$:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}, \qquad \zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$$

For a harmonic input $y = Y \sin(\omega t)$, the steady-state amplitude of $z$ is:

$$\frac{Z}{Y} = \frac{(\omega/\omega_n)^2}{\sqrt{\left[1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2\right]^2 + \left[2\zeta (\omega/\omega_n)\right]^2}}$$

This transfer function has three distinct operating regimes that define the **sensor's identity**:

| Operating Regime | Frequency Condition | What $z$ Measures | Sensor Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Displacement-sensitive** | $\omega \ll \omega_n$ | $z \approx (\omega/\omega_n)^2 Y$ (very small) | Displacement sensor (low $\omega_n$) |
| **Velocity-sensitive** | $\omega \approx \omega_n$ (with $\zeta \approx 1$) | $z \approx Y \cdot \omega/\omega_n$ | Velocity pickup (geophone) |
| **Acceleration-sensitive** | $\omega \gg \omega_n$ | $z \approx -Y$ (mass stationary in space) | Accelerometer (high $\omega_n$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** To build an *accelerometer*, design $\omega_n$ to be **3–5 times** the highest frequency of interest. To build a *velocity pickup*, deliberately tune $\omega_n$ to be close to the measurement band and set $\zeta \approx 0.7$. To build a *displacement sensor*, make $\omega_n$ much *lower* than the measurement band — but this is rarely used because it yields very small signals.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s | Determines upper useful frequency |
| 2 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{km})$ | dimensionless | $\zeta = 0.7$ critical for geophones |
| 3 | Accelerometer sensitivity (high freq) | $S_a = z / \ddot{y} = -1/\omega^2$ | s²/m | Frequency-dependent! |
| 4 | Accelerometer output voltage (piezo) | $V_{\text{out}} = S_a \cdot a \cdot G$ | V | $G$ = charge amplifier gain |
| 5 | Piezoelectric charge | $Q = d \cdot F$ | C | $d$ = piezoelectric strain constant |
| 6 | LVDT output (differential) | $V_{\text{out}} = k_{\text{LVDT}} (N_s - N_p) \, dV/dt$ | V | $N_s, N_p$ secondary/primary turns |
| 7 | Eddy current probe sensitivity | $S_{\text{eddy}} = V_0 / x$ | V/m | Non-linear; needs linearisation |
| 8 | Strain gauge gauge factor | $G_f = (\Delta R/R) / \varepsilon$ | dimensionless | Typical: 2.0 (constantan) |
| 9 | Dynamic range | $\text{DR} = 20 \log_{10}(a_{\max} / a_{\min})$ | dB | Ratio of largest to smallest measurable |
| 10 | Cross-axis rejection | $\text{CR} = 20 \log_{10}(S_a / S_{\perp})$ | dB | Typical > 30 dB for quality sensors |

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Industry

A wrong choice of sensor destroys the validity of vibration data. For example, mounting a low-frequency displacement sensor on a turbine running at 50 Hz (3000 rpm) yields only noise, while mounting a high-frequency accelerometer on a slow conveyor would miss the very-low-frequency dynamics entirely. The seismic-mass framework provides a single, rigorous basis for *all* such decisions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Transfer Function of a Seismic-Mass Accelerometer

**Starting premise:** A proof mass $m$ is connected to a rigid case through a spring of stiffness $k$ and a dashpot of damping $c$. The case is rigidly attached to a structure vibrating with absolute displacement $y(t)$.

### Step 1 — Free-Body Equation of the Mass

Summing forces on the mass (spring force opposing relative displacement, damper force opposing relative velocity):

$$m \ddot{x} = -k(x - y) - c(\dot{x} - \dot{y})$$

*Rearranged:* The mass sees the case as moving, so the restoring force depends on *relative* motion.

### Step 2 — Change of Variable to Relative Displacement

Define $z = x - y$. Then:

$$\dot{z} = \dot{x} - \dot{y}, \qquad \ddot{z} = \ddot{x} - \ddot{y} \implies \ddot{x} = \ddot{z} + \ddot{y}$$

Substituting into Step 1:

$$m(\ddot{z} + \ddot{y}) = -k z - c \dot{z}$$

### Step 3 — Isolating the Sensor Output Equation

Rearranging the terms involving $z$ to the left-hand side:

$$m \ddot{z} + c \dot{z} + k z = -m \ddot{y}$$

*Interpretation:* The right-hand side is the **inertial force** $-m\ddot{y}$, which acts as the effective forcing function. Thus, a displacement $y$ of the structure drives the sensor *as if* through an inertial force proportional to acceleration $\ddot{y}$.

### Step 4 — Steady-State Sinusoidal Response

Let the structure vibrate sinusoidally: $y = Y \sin(\omega t)$. The forcing term becomes:

$$-m \ddot{y} = -m (-\omega^2 Y \sin \omega t) = m \omega^2 Y \sin \omega t$$

Assume steady-state solution $z = Z \sin(\omega t - \phi)$. Substituting into the differential equation:

$$(-m\omega^2 + k) Z \sin(\omega t - \phi) + c \omega Z \cos(\omega t - \phi) = m \omega^2 Y \sin \omega t$$

### Step 5 — Phasor / Complex-Amplitude Form

In the phasor domain, $\sin \omega t \to 1$ and $\cos(\omega t - \phi) \to -j \sin \phi \cdot (\text{factor})$. Equating complex amplitudes:

$$(k - m\omega^2 + j c \omega) Z = m \omega^2 Y$$

### Step 6 — Magnitude of the Transfer Function

$$Z = \frac{m \omega^2 Y}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $k$ and using $\omega_n^2 = k/m$:

$$\frac{Z}{Y} = \frac{(\omega/\omega_n)^2}{\sqrt{\left[1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2\right]^2 + \left[2\zeta(\omega/\omega_n)\right]^2}}$$

### Step 7 — High-Frequency Limit (Accelerometer Regime)

When $\omega \gg \omega_n$, the denominator $\to (\omega/\omega_n)^2$, so:

$$\frac{Z}{Y} \to -1 \quad (\text{with the 180° phase inversion accounted for})$$

Multiplying both sides by $\omega^2$:

$$Z \omega^2 = -Y \omega^2 = \ddot{y} \quad \text{(in magnitude sense)}$$

Therefore, the **relative displacement $z$ of the proof mass is proportional to the acceleration $\ddot{y}$ of the structure** in the high-frequency regime. This is the operating principle of every seismic accelerometer.

### Step 8 — Acceleration Sensitivity

The sensitivity (ratio of output to input) in this regime is:

$$S_a = \frac{z}{\ddot{y}} = -\frac{1}{\omega^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Rule:** For an accelerometer to have a flat response up to frequency $f_{\max}$, its natural frequency $f_n$ must satisfy $f_n \geq (3 \text{ to } 5) \cdot f_{\max}$. Typical industrial accelerometers have $f_n$ between 1 kHz and 50 kHz.

## 3.2 Derivation: LVDT Displacement Sensor Output

A **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** is a passive inductive transducer for measuring **relative displacement**.

**Construction:** A primary coil $N_p$ and two identical secondary coils $N_{s1}, N_{s2}$ are wound on a cylindrical former. A movable ferromagnetic core links the flux between them.

**Step 1 — Excite the primary** with an AC voltage $V_p = V_0 \sin(\omega_e t)$, where $\omega_e$ is the excitation frequency (typically 1–10 kHz).

**Step 2 — When the core is centred**, the two secondaries induce equal and opposite EMFs: $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$, so the differential output $V_{\text{out}} = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = 0$.

**Step 3 — When the core displaces by $x$** from centre, the flux linkage in one secondary increases and the other decreases linearly with $x$:

$$E_{s1} - E_{s2} = K_{\text{LVDT}} \cdot x \cdot \sin(\omega_e t)$$

The output amplitude is **linear in displacement** and **phase-sensitive** (phase indicates direction of motion). This is the KTU-standard derivation for LVDT-based displacement sensing.

## 3.3 Piezoelectric Accelerometer Charge Equation

The piezoelectric element generates charge proportional to applied force:

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F = d_{33} \cdot m_{\text{seismic}} \cdot \ddot{y}$$

where $d_{33}$ is the piezoelectric charge coefficient (C/N). A charge amplifier converts this to voltage:

$$V_{\text{out}} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{d_{33} \cdot m_{\text{seismic}} \cdot \ddot{y}}{C_f}$$

Sensitivity in pC/(m/s²) is quoted in manufacturer datasheets; common ranges are 10–1000 pC/g.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vibration Measurement Chain — Block Diagram

This block-level functional architecture maps the end-to-end signal flow from a vibrating machine surface to an analysable electrical output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Machine Surface] --> B[Vibration Sensor]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioner]
    C --> D[Amplifier]
    D --> E[Filter]
    E --> F[ADC Converter]
    F --> G[Digital Signal Processor]
    G --> H[Display and Alarm]

    subgraph "Sensing Element"
        B1[Proof Mass] --> B2[Spring Damper]
        B2 --> B3[Transduction Mechanism]
    end

    B -.-> B1
```

**Description of the flow:**

- **A → B:** Mechanical vibration of the structure is physically coupled to the sensor case.
- **B → C:** The sensor (piezoelectric, LVDT, eddy-current, etc.) converts mechanical energy to an electrical signal — typically very low in amplitude (mV or pC range).
- **C → D:** A charge amplifier (for piezo) or instrumentation amplifier (for LVDT/strain gauge) boosts the signal.
- **D → E:** Anti-aliasing low-pass filter removes frequencies above the Nyquist limit.
- **E → F:** ADC digitises the analogue signal.
- **F → G:** DSP performs FFT, RMS, or envelope analysis.
- **G → H:** Result is displayed numerically and/or used to trigger alarms.

## 4.2 Sensor Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Vibration Sensor] --> S1{Absolute or Relative}
    S1 -->|Absolute| S2[Seismic Mass Type]
    S1 -->|Relative| S3[Contactless Probe Type]

    S2 --> S2a[Piezoelectric Accelerometer]
    S2 --> S2b[Piezoresistive Accelerometer]
    S2 --> S2c[Capacitive Accelerometer]
    S2 --> S2d[Electrodynamic Velocity Pickup]

    S3 --> S3a[Eddy Current Probe]
    S3 --> S3b[Capacitive Probe]
    S3 --> S3c[Laser Doppler Vibrometer]
    S3 --> S3d[Strain Gauge]
```

## 4.3 Sensor Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Measurement Type} -->|Displacement| Q2
    Q1 -->|Velocity| Q3
    Q1 -->|Acceleration| Q4

    Q2{Range} -->|Large mm| R1[LV DT or Eddy Current]
    Q2 -->|Small micron| R2[Capacitive or Laser Doppler]

    Q3{Frequency Band} -->|Low 1 to 500 Hz| R3[Geophone Electrodynamic]
    Q3 -->|High gt 500 Hz| R4[Piezoelectric with Integrator]

    Q4{Environment} -->|Hot above 200C| R5[IEPE Piezo with Cooling]
    Q4 -->|Standard Industrial| R6[Standard Piezo Accelerometer]
    Q4 -->|High g Impact| R7[Piezoresistive MEMS]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block View of the Three Seismic Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LF["Low Frequency omega much less than omega_n"]
        LF1[Input Displacement] --> LF2[Relative z is small]
        LF2 --> LF3[Output measures quasi static displacement]
    end

    subgraph MF["Mid Frequency omega near omega_n zeta near 0.7"]
        MF1[Input Displacement] --> MF2[Relative z is proportional to velocity]
        MF2 --> MF3[Output is velocity pickup]
    end

    subgraph HF["High Frequency omega much greater than omega_n"]
        HF1[Input Displacement] --> HF2[Relative z is proportional to negative input]
        HF2 --> HF3[Output is acceleration]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
Define a vibration sensor. Distinguish clearly between **absolute** and **relative** vibration measurement with one industrial example of each.

**Model Answer (10 lines):**
A vibration sensor is a transducer that converts mechanical vibratory motion (displacement, velocity, or acceleration) into a proportional electrical signal for analysis. **Absolute measurement** determines motion with respect to a *fixed inertial frame* (independent of the machine's own position) — implemented using a **seismic mass** that is inertially referenced. Example: a piezoelectric accelerometer mounted on a motor casing measures the casing's vibration *in space*, not relative to ground. **Relative measurement** determines motion of a target *with respect to the sensor body* itself. Example: an **eddy-current proximity probe** mounted near a rotating shaft measures the shaft's *radial motion relative to the probe tip*, which includes both shaft vibration and probe motion. Absolute sensors dominate industrial condition monitoring because they directly quantify dynamic stress in the structure.

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
State the governing differential equation of a seismic mass transducer and identify the physical meaning of each term.

**Model Answer (8 lines):**
The governing equation is $m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m\ddot{y}$, where $z$ is the relative displacement of the proof mass, $y$ is the absolute displacement of the structure, $m$ is the proof mass, $c$ is the damping coefficient, and $k$ is the spring stiffness. The term $m\ddot{z}$ represents the **inertial force on the mass**, $c\dot{z}$ is the **viscous damping force** (energy dissipation), $kz$ is the **spring restoring force**, and the right-hand side $-m\ddot{y}$ is the **inertial forcing** arising from the structure's acceleration — this is the *input* to the sensor. The relative displacement $z$ is what is transduced into an electrical output.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for KTU 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **piezoelectric accelerometer**. Discuss its sensitivity, frequency range, and typical industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the transfer function of a seismic mass transducer and show that at high frequencies the relative displacement is proportional to the acceleration of the base. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part A(a)

**Construction (3 marks):**
A piezoelectric accelerometer consists of a **proof mass $m$** clamped against a **piezoelectric crystal disc** (typically lead zirconate titanate, PZT) by a preload bolt, all enclosed in a rigid metal case with a mounting stud at the base. A seismic mass rests on top of the crystal; the crystal itself acts as the spring (very stiff) and provides the electromechanical transduction.

**Working (2 marks):**
When the case accelerates with the vibrating structure, the inertial force $F = m \cdot a$ acts on the piezoelectric crystal. The crystal deforms by a small amount, generating a charge $Q = d_{33} \cdot F = d_{33} \cdot m \cdot a$ on its electrodes. A charge amplifier or IEPE (Integrated Electronic Piezo-Electric) circuit converts this to a low-impedance voltage output $V_{\text{out}} \propto a$.

**Sensitivity, Frequency, Applications (2 marks):**
Sensitivity: typically 10–1000 pC/g or 1–100 mV/g. Frequency range: 0.5 Hz to 30 kHz. Applications: condition monitoring of rotating machinery, modal analysis, bearing diagnostics, impact testing, and aerospace structural testing. Advantages: wide bandwidth, high sensitivity, small size, robust. Limitations: cannot measure true DC (static) acceleration.

#### Model Solution to Part A(b)

This is the **complete derivation** from Section 3.1 of these notes. The student must:

1. Write the FBD equation: $m\ddot{x} = -k(x-y) - c(\dot{x}-\dot{y})$.
2. Change variable to $z = x - y$.
3. Obtain $m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m\ddot{y}$.
4. Convert to phasor form for sinusoidal input.
5. Derive $Z/Y = (\omega/\omega_n)^2 / \sqrt{[1-(\omega/\omega_n)^2]^2 + [2\zeta(\omega/\omega_n)]^2}$.
6. Take the high-frequency limit $\omega \gg \omega_n$ to show $Z \to -Y$.
7. Conclude that $z \propto \ddot{y}/\omega^2$ in this limit, confirming acceleration sensitivity $S_a = -1/\omega^2$.

**Key remark to score full marks:** The student should explicitly state that this is why piezoelectric accelerometers are designed with very high $f_n$ (typically 30 kHz for general-purpose units), so the operating range $\omega \ll \omega_n$ yields a flat acceleration response.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of an **eddy-current proximity probe** and a **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** for vibration displacement measurement. Compare their ranges, accuracies, and contact requirements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A seismic mass transducer has $m = 0.05$ kg, $k = 5000$ N/m, and $c = 5$ N·s/m. Calculate the natural frequency, damping ratio, and determine the type of sensor (displacement, velocity, or acceleration) suitable for measuring a vibration of 50 Hz. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part B(a)

**Eddy-Current Probe (3 marks):**
Construction: a coil of wire wound on a probe tip, energised with a high-frequency AC (1–2 MHz). Working: when a conductive target (e.g., a steel shaft) approaches the probe tip, eddy currents induced in the target oppose the coil's magnetic field, changing the coil's inductance. This inductance change is detected by a bridge or oscillator circuit and converted to a voltage proportional to the gap. **Non-contact**, range 0.5–5 mm, accuracy ±1 µm. Used for relative shaft vibration in turbomachinery.

**LVDT (2 marks):**
Construction: a primary coil and two secondary coils wound on a cylindrical former, with a movable ferromagnetic core. The primary is AC-excited at 1–10 kHz. When the core moves, the flux linkage in the secondaries becomes asymmetric, producing a differential output voltage proportional (and phase-sensitive) to core displacement. **Contact-type**, range ±1 mm to ±100 mm, accuracy ±0.1%.

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Eddy-Current Probe | LVDT |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Contact | Non-contact | Contact (core touches target via rod) |
| Range | 0.5 – 5 mm | Up to ±100 mm |
| Bandwidth | DC to 50 kHz | DC to 1 kHz (limited by excitation) |
| Target | Conductive metals only | Ferromagnetic materials |
| Application | Shaft vibration in turbomachinery | Slow large-displacement actuators, machine tool slides |

#### Model Solution to Part B(b)

**Given:** $m = 0.05$ kg, $k = 5000$ N/m, $c = 5$ N·s/m, $f = 50$ Hz.

**Step 1 — Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{5000}{0.05}} = \sqrt{100000} = 316.23 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{316.23}{2\pi} = 50.33 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Damping ratio:**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{5}{2 \times \sqrt{5000 \times 0.05}} = \frac{5}{2 \times \sqrt{250}} = \frac{5}{2 \times 15.811} = \frac{5}{31.623} = 0.158$$

**Step 3 — Operating regime at 50 Hz:**

Frequency ratio: $\omega / \omega_n = 1.0$ (i.e., $f = f_n$).

**Conclusion:**
Since the operating frequency $f = 50$ Hz is *equal to* the natural frequency $f_n = 50.33$ Hz, the sensor operates in the **velocity-sensitive regime** (mid-frequency, near resonance). With $\zeta = 0.158$ (well below critical damping), the output amplitude will be **amplified** by the resonance. To obtain a flat velocity response, the damping ratio should be raised to $\zeta \approx 0.7$ by adding more damping (e.g., viscous fluid dashpot). This calculation is a typical KTU numerical problem (4–5 marks) combined with a design interpretation (2–3 marks).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** "natural frequency" with "operating frequency". The natural frequency $f_n$ is a *property* of the sensor, not the input.
> - **Do NOT use** $f$ instead of $\omega$ in the $\omega_n$ formula. The formula $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ uses **radians per second**, not Hz. Convert at the end: $f_n = \omega_n / 2\pi$.
> - **Do NOT forget** the phase inversion: at high frequency, $Z/Y \to -1$, meaning the sensor output is 180° out of phase with the structure's actual displacement.
> - **Do NOT state** the unit of $d_{33}$ as C/m. It is **C/N** (Coulomb per Newton of force).
> - **Common mark-loss trap:** Students often write the transfer function of an accelerometer as $Z/Y = -1$ *without* justifying the high-frequency limit. Marks require showing the *denominator approximation*: $(k - m\omega^2)^2 \approx (m\omega^2)^2$ for $\omega \gg \omega_n$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Vibration sensor** = electromechanical transducer converting mechanical motion → electrical signal.
- **Two classes:** **Absolute (seismic)** vs **Relative (non-seismic)**.
- **Seismic-mass equation:** $m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m\ddot{y}$ — the foundation of all accelerometer theory.
- **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ — sets the *upper useful frequency limit* of any seismic sensor.
- **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$ — for accelerometers, low $\zeta$ ($\lt 0.1$); for velocity pickups, $\zeta \approx 0.7$.
- **Three operating regimes of a seismic sensor:**
  - $\omega \ll \omega_n$ → displacement sensor (rarely used)
  - $\omega \approx \omega_n$ → velocity pickup (geophone)
  - $\omega \gg \omega_n$ → accelerometer (most common)
- **Piezoelectric accelerometer** uses $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$; sensitivity 10–1000 pC/g; cannot measure DC; $f_n$ in kHz range.
- **Piezoresistive accelerometer** uses strain gauges on a silicon cantilever; can measure DC; lower bandwidth.
- **Capacitive accelerometer** uses gap-change in a capacitor; high sensitivity at low frequency; MEMS-compatible.
- **Electrodynamic / geophone** uses a coil moving in a magnetic field; output ∝ velocity; rugged, low cost.
- **Eddy-current probe** is non-contact, measures relative displacement; range 0.5–5 mm; for turbomachinery shafts.
- **LVDT** is contact-type, AC-excited, linear output, high accuracy for slow displacements.
- **Strain gauge** measures *local strain* and infers vibration indirectly; gauge factor $G_f \approx 2$.
- **Design rule of thumb:** $f_n \geq (3-5) \times f_{\max}$ for accelerometers; $f_n \leq f_{\min}$ for displacement sensors.
- **Always** choose mounting method carefully: stud-mount > adhesive-mount > magnetic-mount (in terms of high-frequency fidelity).
- **Cross-axis sensitivity** must be < 5% for quality measurements; < 1% for precision lab work.
- **Dynamic range** is typically 80–120 dB for industrial accelerometers.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Quick Recall Box:**
> - *Accelerometer* = high $f_n$, low $\zeta$, measures **acceleration**.
> - *Velocity pickup* = $f_n$ inside the band, $\zeta \approx 0.7$, measures **velocity**.
> - *Displacement probe* = low $f_n$, usually **non-seismic (LVDT/eddy-current)**, measures **displacement** directly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency and Spectrum Analysers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT632 - INDUSTRIAL NOISE AND VIBRATION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Frequency and Spectrum Analysers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Frequency Analyser** is an electronic instrument designed to measure and display the amplitude (magnitude) of a signal's frequency components, decomposing a complex time-domain waveform into its individual sinusoidal constituents. In the context of Industrial Noise and Vibration Control, these instruments form the cornerstone of every acoustic and vibration diagnostic exercise, translating raw microphone or accelerometer voltages into meaningful spectral information expressed in **Hz**, **dB**, or **g**.

A **Spectrum Analyser** is the broader, more capable superset of the frequency analyser family. It not only resolves frequencies but also quantifies the **power spectral density (PSD)**, **phase relationships**, **harmonic structure**, and **sideband content** of dynamic signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> A frequency analyser is a device that measures the relative amplitudes of the components of a complex waveform, while a spectrum analyser is an instrument that displays the distribution of signal energy as a function of frequency. Both are indispensable for evaluating rotating machinery, structural resonances, and environmental noise sources.

## 1.2 The Physical Need — Why We Cannot Use an Oscilloscope

A time-domain oscilloscope shows **how a signal varies with time**. It cannot tell you *why* a bearing is failing, *which* gear tooth is defective, or *at what* speed a fan blade is resonating. Industrial noise is almost always the **superposition of multiple tones, harmonics, and broadband noise**. To control it, one must isolate, identify, and quantify each contributor — this is the exclusive domain of frequency/spectrum analysis.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sound Recipe"

Imagine a complex industrial sound (e.g., a gearbox under load) as a **cake**. To a listener, it just tastes like "cake." An oscilloscope is like a photograph of the whole cake — you can see its shape, but not its ingredients.

A **Frequency Analyser** is like a sieve that separates the flour, sugar, eggs, and butter, weighing each one separately.

A **Spectrum Analyser** is the full chemical laboratory — it tells you the exact proportion of each ingredient, the order they were added, and even traces of contaminants (sidebands). In engineering terms, this means identifying:
- **Fundamental shaft frequency** (e.g., 25 Hz at 1500 RPM)
- **Gear meshing frequency** (e.g., 437 Hz)
- **Bearing defect frequencies** (e.g., 162 Hz BPFO)
- **Broadband turbulence noise** (continuous spectrum)

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:**
> - *Frequency Analyser* → narrow-band amplitude at specific bands.
> - *Spectrum Analyser* → continuous spectral distribution, often with phase/PSD capability.
> - In modern practice, the two terms are largely interchangeable since digital FFT technology serves both functions.

## 1.4 The Two Fundamental Domains

| Domain | Variable | What You See | Typical Instrument |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Time Domain** | Amplitude vs. Time $x(t)$ | Waveform shape, RMS, peak | Oscilloscope, Sound Level Meter (SLM) |
| **Frequency Domain** | Amplitude vs. Frequency $X(f)$ | Spectral peaks, harmonics, sidebands | Frequency/Spectrum Analyser |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Equivalence (Parseval's Theorem):**
> The total energy in the time domain equals the total energy in the frequency domain. This means *no information is lost* in spectral analysis — it is simply re-expressed in a more diagnostically useful coordinate system.

## 1.5 Standard Units Used in KTU Examinations

- **Frequency:** $f$ in **Hz** (cycles/second) or **kHz**
- **Angular frequency:** $\omega = 2\pi f$ in **rad/s**
- **Amplitude (vibration):** acceleration in **m/s²** or **g** ($1\,g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$)
- **Amplitude (acoustic):** Sound Pressure Level in **dB re 20 μPa**
- **Bandwidth:** $\Delta f$ in **Hz**
- **Spectral density:** $G(f)$ in **g²/Hz** or **Pa²/Hz**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fourier Foundation

Any periodic signal $x(t)$ of period $T$ can be expressed as a sum of harmonically related sines and cosines — this is the **Fourier Series**:

$$
x(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ a_n \cos(2\pi n f_0 t) + b_n \sin(2\pi n f_0 t) \right]
$$

where $f_0 = 1/T$ is the **fundamental frequency**, and $a_n$, $b_n$ are the Fourier coefficients.

For **non-periodic, transient** signals (e.g., impact noise, impulsive bearing knocks), we use the **Fourier Transform**:

$$
X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{-j 2\pi f t} \, dt
$$

and its inverse:

$$
x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) \, e^{j 2\pi f t} \, df
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Most industrial signals (gearbox vibration, motor noise) are *quasi-periodic* and contain both periodic and random components. The spectrum analyser mathematically performs these integrals in real-time.

## 2.2 Classification of Frequency/Spectrum Analysers

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to differentiate the following architectures:

### (A) Analogue Swept-Filter (Heterodyne / Superheterodyne) Analyser
- Uses a **local oscillator (LO)** that sweeps across the frequency range of interest.
- A **mixer** combines the input signal with the LO output.
- A **band-pass filter (IF filter)** of fixed intermediate frequency (IF) extracts each frequency slice.
- A **detector** (RMS, peak, average) measures the amplitude in that slice.
- Output: amplitude vs. frequency curve on a CRT or plotter.

### (B) Digital Filter Bank Analyser
- Input signal is split into multiple parallel **band-pass digital filters**.
- Each filter covers a fixed octave or fractional-octave band.
- Output: simultaneous amplitude in all bands → "real-time" capability.
- Common resolution: **1/1 octave**, **1/3 octave**, **1/6 octave**, **1/12 octave**.

### (C) Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) Analyser
- Input analogue signal is **anti-alias filtered** and **digitised by an ADC**.
- A **block of $N$ samples** is processed by the FFT algorithm.
- Output: complete magnitude and phase spectrum from $f_{\min}$ to $f_{\max}$.
- Modern industry standard; underlies every modern data acquisition system.

### (D) Real-Time Analyser (RTA)
- A subset of FFT analysers that processes samples **faster than real-time** within the chosen frequency span.
- Bandwidth: spans from DC up to typically **20 kHz** (audio) or **100 kHz** (vibration).
- Updates the display continuously at frame rates of **5–60 Hz**.

### (E) Swept-Spectrum Analyser (Microwave/RF)
- Used for very high frequencies (kHz to GHz).
- Wide dynamic range (>100 dB).
- Less relevant for typical industrial acoustic/vibration work, but mentioned in KTU's classification of "types of spectrum analysers."

## 2.3 Critical Analyser Specifications (KTU Favourite)

| Specification | Symbol | Definition | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Range** | $f_{\min}$ to $f_{\max}$ | Span over which the instrument operates | 0.1 Hz – 100 kHz |
| **Resolution Bandwidth** | RBW or $\Delta f$ | Smallest frequency increment distinguishable | 1 mHz – 1 kHz |
| **Analysis Bandwidth** | ABW | Width of each filter in a filter bank | 1 Hz – 1 kHz |
| **Dynamic Range** | DR | Ratio of largest to smallest measurable signal | 80 – 150 dB |
| **Display Range** | — | Vertical axis span | 60 – 200 dB |
| **Time Window / Block Size** | $T_w$ | Duration of sampled record | 1 ms – 100 s |
| **Number of Lines** | $N_L$ | Number of frequency bins in the spectrum | 400 – 6400 |
| **Sample Rate** | $f_s$ | ADC sampling frequency | 1 kHz – 1 MHz |
| **Window Function** | — | Taper applied to reduce spectral leakage | Hanning, Hamming, Flat-top, Blackman-Harris |
| **Averaging** | — | Method to reduce noise in repeated measurements | Linear, exponential, peak-hold |

## 2.4 Key Engineering Relationships

### 2.4.1 Frequency Resolution

$$
\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N} = \frac{1}{T_w}
$$

where $N$ is the number of samples and $T_w$ is the time-window length.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off:** Better (smaller) $\Delta f$ requires either more samples $N$ or a longer record $T_w$. This is the **resolution vs. time-record** dilemma — to resolve 1 Hz you need at least 1 second of data.

### 2.4.2 Maximum Analysable Frequency (Nyquist Limit)

$$
f_{\max} = \frac{f_s}{2}
$$

Signals above $f_{\max}$ "fold back" and corrupt the spectrum — this is **aliasing**.

### 2.4.3 Octave and Fractional-Octave Band Centre Frequencies

For an octave band whose centre frequency is $f_c$, the upper and lower cutoff frequencies are:

$$
f_u = f_c \cdot 2^{1/(2n)}, \quad f_l = f_c \cdot 2^{-1/(2n)}
$$

where $n$ is the band number. For $n=1$ (1/1 octave), $f_u = \sqrt{2}\, f_c$ and $f_l = f_c / \sqrt{2}$.

Standard 1/1 octave centre frequencies (Hz):
**16, 31.5, 63, 125, 250, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000, 8000, 16000**

For **1/3 octave**, the band centres follow:

$$
f_c = 1000 \cdot 10^{(k/10)}
$$

where $k = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$ (i.e., one band per ~23% frequency change).

### 2.4.4 Power Spectral Density (PSD)

For a random signal $x(t)$, the PSD is defined as:

$$
G_x(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{2 \vert X_T(f) \vert^2}{T}
$$

with units of $\text{(signal units)}^2 / \text{Hz}$ (e.g., $\text{g}^2/\text{Hz}$).

### 2.4.5 Overall Level from Spectral Data

The overall (broadband) RMS level is recovered by summing all spectral components:

$$
x_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N_L} \vert X_i \vert^2}
$$

In dB form, this is equivalent to the **logarithmic summation**:

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{N_L} 10^{L_i/10} \right)
$$

where each $L_i$ is the band level in dB.

### 2.4.6 FFT Computational Cost

The computational load of an $N$-point FFT is:

$$
\text{Operations} = O(N \log_2 N)
$$

Radix-2 FFT requires $N$ to be a power of 2 (e.g., 1024, 2048, 4096).

## 2.5 Analyser Type Selection — Engineering Utility

| Industrial Application | Recommended Analyser Type | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Octave-band noise survey (OSHA/ISO compliance) | Digital filter bank (1/1 or 1/3 octave) | Direct comparability with regulatory bands |
| Bearing/gearbox diagnostics | FFT analyser with high RBW (≤ 1 Hz) and zoom capability | Need to resolve closely spaced sidebands |
| Acoustic intensity mapping | Real-time FFT with cross-spectrum capability | Time-coherence required |
| Impact/hammer test (modal analysis) | FFT analyser with transient capture & force-acceleration FRF | Transient events need time-windowing |
| Rotating machinery health monitoring | FFT + order tracking (shaft-synchronous sampling) | Speed changes mean non-stationary signals |
| Environmental noise monitoring | 1/3-octave RTA with Leq integration | Long-term averaged broadband metrics |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Time-Record Length and Frequency Resolution

**Given:** An FFT analyser samples a vibration signal at $f_s = 10,240$ Hz using a record length of $N = 1024$ samples with a Hanning window applied.

**Step 1 — Identify the time duration of the sampled block.**

The sample period is the reciprocal of the sample rate:

$$
T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{10{,}240} = 9.766 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{s}
$$

The total record time is therefore:

$$
T_w = N \cdot T_s = 1024 \times 9.766 \times 10^{-5} = 0.1\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the frequency resolution (line spacing) of the FFT.**

The frequency spacing between adjacent spectral lines is the inverse of the record time:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_w} = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10\,\text{Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum analysable (Nyquist) frequency.**

By the Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem, the highest frequency that can be unambiguously analysed is half the sample rate:

$$
f_{\max} = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{10{,}240}{2} = 5120\,\text{Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the number of independent frequency lines.**

The number of useful frequency bins (one-sided spectrum) is:

$$
N_L = \frac{N}{2} = \frac{1024}{2} = 512
$$

**Step 5 — Verification using the fundamental FFT relation.**

Cross-check using the equivalent expression:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N} = \frac{10{,}240}{1024} = 10\,\text{Hz} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** To improve the resolution from 10 Hz to 1 Hz (a 10× improvement), we must either increase the record length to $T_w = 1$ s, or decrease the sample rate to $f_s = 1024$ Hz (which also reduces $f_{\max}$ to 512 Hz). The choice depends on the highest frequency of interest in the signal.

## 3.2 Derivation: Anti-Aliasing Filter Cut-off Frequency

**Given:** A vibration signal contains significant energy up to 5 kHz. We wish to sample and analyse it with an FFT analyser.

**Step 1 — Determine the minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing (Nyquist criterion).**

The sample rate must exceed twice the highest signal frequency:

$$
f_s > 2 f_{\max} \Rightarrow f_s > 2 \times 5000 = 10{,}000\,\text{Hz}
$$

In practice, we choose a comfortable margin (say 2.56× rather than exactly 2×) to ease the anti-alias filter design:

$$
f_s = 2.56 \times f_{\max} = 2.56 \times 5000 = 12{,}800\,\text{Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Set the anti-alias filter cut-off.**

The low-pass anti-alias filter must pass signals up to $f_{\max}$ and reject signals above $f_s/2$ with at least 80 dB attenuation:

$$
f_c = f_{\max} = 5\,\text{kHz}, \quad \text{Stopband starts at } \frac{f_s}{2} = 6.4\,\text{kHz}
$$

The filter's roll-off must be steep enough — typically an **8th-order Butterworth** (48 dB/octave) is selected to achieve the required rejection in the narrow 1.4 kHz transition band.

## 3.3 Derivation: Overall Level from Octave-Band Data

**Given:** A 1/1 octave band sound pressure level measurement on a fan yields the following:

| Octave Centre (Hz) | SPL (dB) |
|---|---|
| 125 | 78 |
| 250 | 84 |
| 500 | 89 |
| 1000 | 92 |
| 2000 | 87 |
| 4000 | 80 |

**Step 1 — Convert each dB value to linear power ratio.**

Using the relation $P_i = 10^{L_i/10}$:

$$
P_{125} = 10^{7.8}, \quad P_{250} = 10^{8.4}, \quad P_{500} = 10^{8.9}, \quad P_{1000} = 10^{9.2}, \quad P_{2000} = 10^{8.7}, \quad P_{4000} = 10^{8.0}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the linear powers.**

$$
\sum P_i = 10^{7.8} + 10^{8.4} + 10^{8.9} + 10^{9.2} + 10^{8.7} + 10^{8.0}
$$

Computing each term in scientific notation:

$$
\sum P_i = (6.31 + 25.12 + 79.43 + 158.49 + 50.12 + 10.00) \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
\sum P_i = 329.47 \times 10^{6}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert the summed power back to dB.**

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(329.47 \times 10^{6}) = 10 \log_{10}(3.2947 \times 10^{8})
$$

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 10 (8 + \log_{10} 3.2947) = 10 (8 + 0.518) = 85.18\,\text{dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The 1000 Hz band alone contributes 92 dB → linear 158.49 × 10⁶. Yet the overall level is only 85.18 dB because we are *summing powers*, not dB. Always remember: **dB values cannot be added arithmetically.**

## 3.4 Derivation: Windowing Effect on Spectral Leakage

**Given:** A pure 27.3 Hz tone is sampled with $f_s = 1024$ Hz and $N = 1024$ (so $T_w = 1$ s, $\Delta f = 1$ Hz). The 27.3 Hz signal does not fall exactly on an FFT bin.

**Step 1 — Express the signal mathematically.**

$$
x(t) = A \sin(2\pi \cdot 27.3 \cdot t)
$$

**Step 2 — Without windowing (rectangular window).**

The signal energy "leaks" into adjacent bins because the finite record $T_w$ acts as a rectangular window $w(t) = 1$ for $0 \le t \le T_w$ and 0 elsewhere. The spectrum of the product is the convolution of the pure tone's delta function with $\text{sinc}(f T_w)$, producing a main lobe surrounded by sidelobes.

The main lobe's $-3$ dB width is approximately $\Delta f = 1$ Hz, but sidelobes can be only $-13$ dB below the peak — masking nearby weak tones.

**Step 3 — With Hanning window applied.**

The Hanning window has the form:

$$
w_H(t) = 0.5 \left[ 1 - \cos\left(\frac{2\pi t}{T_w}\right) \right]
$$

Its spectral main lobe broadens to approximately $1.5 \Delta f = 1.5$ Hz, but the first sidelobe is suppressed to **$-31.5$ dB** below the peak.

**Step 4 — Trade-off summary.**

| Window | Main lobe width | First sidelobe level | Best use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | $1.0 \Delta f$ | $-13$ dB | Transient capture, short bursts |
| Hanning | $1.5 \Delta f$ | $-31$ dB | General vibration / acoustic spectra |
| Hamming | $1.3 \Delta f$ | $-43$ dB | Close-tone separation |
| Flat-top | $3.8 \Delta f$ | $-93$ dB | Accurate amplitude (poor resolution) |
| Blackman-Harris | $2.0 \Delta f$ | $-92$ dB | Detecting very weak tones near strong ones |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering judgment:** Always default to **Hanning** for general noise/vibration spectra. Switch to **Flat-top** when amplitude accuracy is paramount (e.g., calibration checks). Use **Force/Exponential** windows for impulsive (transient) signals.

## 3.5 Derivation: Order Tracking and Shaft-Synchronous Analysis

**Given:** A motor runs at variable speed. The shaft frequency $f_{\text{shaft}}$ is not constant. We wish to analyse vibration in the **order domain** rather than the frequency domain.

**Step 1 — Define "order."**

One order = 1× shaft rotation. So if the shaft is at 1500 RPM = 25 Hz, then 2nd order = 50 Hz, 3rd order = 75 Hz, etc.

**Step 2 — Establish the angular relationship.**

$$
f_{\text{order } n} = n \cdot f_{\text{shaft}}(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Resample the vibration signal synchronously.**

The tachometer pulse triggers the ADC to acquire $K$ samples per shaft revolution, rather than at a fixed time interval. This converts the order domain into a fixed frequency grid regardless of speed.

**Step 4 — Apply FFT in the order domain.**

After resampling, the FFT yields a spectrum in *orders* rather than Hz — this is **order analysis**. It is the standard tool for variable-speed rotating machinery diagnostics.

## 3.6 Analyser Selection Decision Matrix

| If you need to … | Use … | Critical specification |
|---|---|---|
| Measure noise compliance (ISO 1996, OSHA) | 1/3-octave filter bank | Class 1 accuracy, $\pm 0.7$ dB tolerance |
| Diagnose bearing defects | Narrow-band FFT with envelope analysis | RBW ≤ 1 Hz, peak-hold averaging |
| Modal analysis (impact hammer) | Dual-channel FFT with FRF | Coherence > 0.9, exponential window |
| Monitor rotating machinery | Order tracking analyser | Shaft-synchronous sampling, tach input |
| Measure acoustic intensity | Two-microphone cross-spectrum FFT | Phase match ±0.05°, real-time |
| Field survey of large plant | Portable sound level meter (SLM) with FFT option | Battery life, weight, dynamic range |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic FFT-Based Spectrum Analyser

This diagram illustrates the complete signal-flow architecture of a modern digital spectrum analyser as used in industrial noise & vibration work.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sig1[Transducer Signal] --> aa[Anti Alias Low Pass Filter]
    aa --> pga[Programmable Gain Amplifier]
    pga --> adc[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    adc --> buf[Sample Buffer N points]
    buf --> win[Window Function Multiplier]
    win --> fft[FFT Processor Radix 2]
    fft --> det[Detector RMS Peak Average]
    det --> avg[Averaging Engine Linear Exponential Peak Hold]
    avg --> dsp[Display and Post Processing]
    dsp --> scr[Screen Magnitude vs Frequency]
    dsp --> usb[USB Ethernet Output to PC]
    usb --> pc[Analysis Software MATLAB or Python Diagnostics]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-function interpretation:**
> - The **transducer** (microphone, accelerometer) produces a voltage proportional to acoustic pressure or vibration.
> - The **anti-alias filter** is an analogue low-pass filter with cut-off $f_c \le f_s / 2$.
> - The **PGA** sets the input range dynamically to maximise ADC dynamic range.
> - The **ADC** samples at $f_s$ and quantises with 12–24-bit resolution.
> - The **window function** minimises spectral leakage.
> - The **FFT processor** computes the discrete Fourier transform in real time.
> - The **detector** extracts magnitude and optionally phase.
> - The **averager** improves SNR for stationary or periodic signals.
> - The **display** plots magnitude (dB) vs frequency (Hz).
> - The **output interface** exports data for archival and post-analysis.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Heterodyne (Swept-Filter) Spectrum Analyser

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp[RF or Acoustic Input] --> att[Input Attenuator]
    att --> mix[Mixer]
    lo[Sweeping Local Oscillator] --> mix
    mix --> ifb[IF Band Pass Filter fixed centre]
    ifb --> det[Envelope Detector]
    det --> log[Logarithmic Amplifier]
    log --> vg[Vertical Deflection of CRT]
    lo2[Same Sweep Voltage from LO] --> hd[Horizontal Deflection of CRT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating principle:**
> The local oscillator (LO) frequency is swept linearly with time. The mixer output produces sum and difference frequencies; the IF filter passes only the difference (i.e., $f_{\text{IF}} = \vert f_{\text{LO}} - f_{\text{in}} \vert$). At any instant, the analyser is "looking at" one frequency slice of width equal to the IF filter's bandwidth (RBW).

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Which Analyser to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Start What do you need to measure] --> q1{Is the signal broadband or narrow band}
    q1 -- Broadband noise compliance --> oct[Use Octave or Third Octave Filter Bank Analyser]
    q1 -- Narrow band tones or harmonics --> q2{Do you need to resolve closely spaced frequencies}
    q2 -- Yes high resolution --> fft[Use FFT Analyser with small RBW]
    q2 -- No --> sweep[Use Swept Heterodyne Analyser]
    start --> q3{Is the signal transient or impulsive}
    q3 -- Yes --> tr[Use FFT with Force or Exponential Window]
    q3 -- No --> std[Use FFT with Hanning Window]
    start --> q4{Is the machine speed varying}
    q4 -- Yes --> ord[Use Order Tracking Analyser]
    q4 -- No --> std2[Use Standard FFT Analyser]
```

## 4.4 Time-Domain to Frequency-Domain Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    td[Time Domain Signal x of t] --> fft[Forward Fourier Transform]
    fft --> fd[Frequency Domain Spectrum X of f]
    fd --> ifft[Inverse Fourier Transform]
    ifft --> td2[Reconstructed Time Signal x of t]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key theorem:** Forward and inverse transforms form a perfect reversible pair (Parseval's theorem guarantees energy conservation). The information content is identical — only the representation changes.

## 4.5 Windowing Effect — Visual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rect[Rectangular Window] -- applies --> rectS[Spectrum Wide main lobe 13 dB sidelobes]
    han[Hanning Window] -- applies --> hanS[Spectrum Narrower sidelobes 31 dB below peak]
    ham[Hamming Window] -- applies --> hamS[Spectrum First sidelobe at 43 dB]
    ft[Flat Top Window] -- applies --> ftS[Spectrum Very wide main lobe 93 dB sidelobes amplitude accurate]
```

## 4.6 Analyser System Block Diagram — Complete Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    source[Noise or Vibration Source Machine] --> trans[Transducer Microphone or Accelerometer]
    trans --> pre[Pre Amplifier and IEPE Power Supply]
    pre --> ana[Spectrum Analyser or FFT Data Acquisition System]
    ana --> pc[PC with Diagnostic Software]
    pc --> disp[Display Magnitude vs Frequency vs Time vs Order]
    pc --> report[Report Generation Compliance Documentation]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: Differentiate between a frequency analyser and a spectrum analyser. State one typical industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Frequency Analyser | Spectrum Analyser |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Measures amplitude of discrete frequency components within defined bands | Displays the complete distribution of signal energy across a continuous frequency range |
| **Output** | Discrete band levels (octave/1/3-octave) | Continuous spectrum (FFT) with magnitude and phase |
| **Typical use** | Octave-band noise compliance per ISO 1996 | Bearing/gearbox fault diagnosis via FFT peak analysis |

> A *frequency analyser* is used in **occupational noise surveys** to compute octave-band SPLs for PPE selection. A *spectrum analyser* is used in **condition monitoring** to detect bearing defect frequencies in rotating machinery.

*(1 mark for definition of frequency analyser, 1 mark for spectrum analyser, 1 mark for application)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: What is meant by "Resolution Bandwidth (RBW)" in a spectrum analyser? How does it affect the displayed spectrum?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Resolution Bandwidth (RBW) is the smallest frequency increment that a spectrum analyser can distinguish between two closely spaced tones. It is set by the width of the IF filter in analogue analysers, or by the FFT line spacing $\Delta f = f_s / N$ in digital analysers.

**Effect (1.5 marks):**
- **Smaller RBW** → better ability to resolve closely spaced frequencies, but longer sweep time and lower SNR per bin.
- **Larger RBW** → faster measurements, higher SNR per bin, but adjacent peaks may merge into a single broad hump.

> **Example:** To separate the 162 Hz BPFO from a 165 Hz structural resonance in a bearing housing, an RBW $\le 2$ Hz is mandatory.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Standard:** *Answer ANY ONE of the following:*

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**
**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **digital FFT-based spectrum analyser**. Discuss the role of the anti-aliasing filter, windowing, and averaging in achieving accurate spectral estimates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vibration signal is sampled at $f_s = 2048$ Hz with a record length of $N = 2048$ samples. Compute:
   (i) The frequency resolution $\Delta f$
   (ii) The maximum analysable frequency $f_{\max}$
   (iii) The number of frequency bins in the one-sided spectrum
   (iv) Comment on what change you would make to improve the resolution to 0.5 Hz. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Working Principle of FFT Spectrum Analyser (7 marks)

**1. Block diagram (3 marks):** Draw the full signal chain as in Section 4.1 of these notes. The key blocks are:
- Transducer → Anti-alias filter → PGA → ADC → Sample buffer → Window multiplier → FFT → Detector → Averager → Display

**2. Anti-aliasing filter (1.5 marks):**
This is an analogue low-pass filter placed *before* the ADC. It rejects all frequency components above $f_s/2$ (the Nyquist limit) to prevent **aliasing**, the phenomenon where high-frequency components fold back into the spectrum and appear as false low-frequency tones. Typical design: 8th-order Butterworth with $f_c = 0.4 f_s$.

**3. Windowing (1.5 marks):**
The finite record $T_w$ acts as a rectangular truncation of an ideally infinite signal, producing spectral leakage. Multiplying the time record by a window function (Hanning, Hamming, Flat-top) tapers the endpoints to zero, reducing sidelobe levels at the cost of slightly broadening the main lobe. Hanning is the default choice for vibration/acoustic spectra.

**4. Averaging (1 mark):**
For stationary random signals, averaging $M$ independent spectra reduces the variance of the estimate by a factor of $M$ (for linear averaging) or $10 \log_{10} M$ in dB. Peak-hold averaging is used for transient capture, exponential for slowly varying signals.

### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:** $f_s = 2048$ Hz, $N = 2048$ samples.

**(i) Frequency resolution $\Delta f$ (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta f = \frac{f_s}{N} = \frac{2048}{2048} = 1\,\text{Hz}
$$

**(ii) Maximum analysable frequency $f_{\max}$ (2 marks):**

$$
f_{\max} = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{2048}{2} = 1024\,\text{Hz}
$$

**(iii) Number of frequency bins (one-sided) (2 marks):**

$$
N_L = \frac{N}{2} = \frac{2048}{2} = 1024\,\text{bins}
$$

**(iv) Modification to achieve $\Delta f = 0.5$ Hz (1 mark):**

We must double the record length while keeping $f_{\max}$ the same. So increase $N$ from 2048 to 4096, with $T_w$ now = 2 s. The sample rate remains 2048 Hz.

> **Alternative method:** Halve $f_s$ to 1024 Hz (this also halves $f_{\max}$ to 512 Hz, which may be unacceptable).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Many students write $f_{\max} = f_s$ (wrong) — it is $f_s/2$, the **Nyquist limit**.
> - Some students confuse $\Delta f$ with $f_{\max}$ — they are *not* the same. $\Delta f$ is the **bin spacing**, $f_{\max}$ is the **highest frequency**.
> - For (iv), many answer "increase $N$" without specifying that $T_w$ doubles and $f_s$ must be held constant.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**Q: (a)** Explain with a block diagram the working of a **superheterodyne spectrum analyser**. Compare its performance with a digital FFT analyser. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1/1 octave band sound pressure level measurement on a cooling tower fan gives the following readings:

| Octave Centre (Hz) | 125 | 250 | 500 | 1000 | 2000 | 4000 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SPL (dB) | 76 | 82 | 87 | 90 | 85 | 78 |

Calculate the **overall sound pressure level** in dB re 20 μPa. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Superheterodyne Spectrum Analyser (7 marks)

**1. Block diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes (Input → Attenuator → Mixer → IF filter → Detector → Log amp → CRT).

**2. Working principle (2 marks):**
- The input signal $f_{\text{in}}$ is mixed with a sweeping local oscillator $f_{\text{LO}}$.
- The mixer produces sum ($f_{\text{in}} + f_{\text{LO}}$) and difference ($f_{\text{in}} - f_{\text{LO}}$) frequencies.
- The IF (Intermediate Frequency) band-pass filter, centred at a fixed frequency (typically 21.4 MHz in RF analysers, or 100 kHz in audio analysers), passes only the difference.
- The detector produces an output voltage proportional to the signal amplitude in the slice $\pm$ RBW/2 around $f_{\text{in}}$.
- As $f_{\text{LO}}$ sweeps, the entire spectrum is scanned and displayed on the CRT.

**3. Comparison with FFT analyser (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Superheterodyne | FFT Analyser |
|---|---|---|
| **Processing** | Sequential, one frequency at a time | Parallel, all frequencies simultaneously |
| **Speed** | Slow (sweep-limited) | Fast (real-time possible) |
| **Dynamic range** | Very high (>100 dB) | Moderate (limited by ADC bits) |
| **Frequency range** | Very high (up to GHz) | Limited by ADC speed (typically ≤ 100 kHz) |
| **Phase information** | Lost (envelope detector) | Preserved (complex FFT output) |
| **Best suited for** | RF/microwave, EMI testing | Audio/acoustic, vibration diagnostics |

### Part (b) — Overall SPL Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** 1/1 octave SPL data:

| $f_c$ (Hz) | 125 | 250 | 500 | 1000 | 2000 | 4000 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $L_i$ (dB) | 76 | 82 | 87 | 90 | 85 | 78 |

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Convert each dB value to linear power ratio using $P_i = 10^{L_i/10}$:

$$
P_{125} = 10^{7.6} = 3.98 \times 10^{7}
$$
$$
P_{250} = 10^{8.2} = 1.585 \times 10^{8}
$$
$$
P_{500} = 10^{8.7} = 5.01 \times 10^{8}
$$
$$
P_{1000} = 10^{9.0} = 1.000 \times 10^{9}
$$
$$
P_{2000} = 10^{8.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{8}
$$
$$
P_{4000} = 10^{7.8} = 6.31 \times 10^{7}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Sum the linear powers:

$$
\sum P_i = (0.398 + 1.585 + 5.01 + 10.00 + 3.162 + 0.631) \times 10^{8}
$$
$$
\sum P_i = 20.786 \times 10^{8} = 2.0786 \times 10^{9}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Convert summed power back to dB:

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(2.0786 \times 10^{9})
$$
$$
L_{\text{total}} = 10 (9 + \log_{10} 2.0786) = 10 (9 + 0.3179)
$$
$$
L_{\text{total}} = 93.18\,\text{dB re 20 μPa}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Final answer with units and one line of physical interpretation:

> The overall SPL of the cooling tower fan is **93.2 dB re 20 μPa**. The dominant contributor is the 1000 Hz band (90 dB), indicating that the broadband aerodynamic noise is concentrated at mid-frequencies — typical of axial-fan blade-passing tones.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Part B):**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Adding dB values directly (e.g., $76+82+87+90+85+78 = 498$ dB) — this is **wrong** because dB is a logarithmic measure.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Averaging the dB values: $498/6 = 83$ dB — also wrong, since energy addition is *not* linear in dB.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Forgetting to multiply by $10^{6}$ or $10^{9}$ when converting large/small linear ratios.
> - **Correct method:** Convert → sum in linear → convert back. Marks are awarded for showing all three steps explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Frequency and Spectrum Analysers**

### Core Concepts
- **Frequency analyser** → discrete band levels (octave, 1/3 octave).
- **Spectrum analyser** → continuous magnitude/phase spectrum (FFT or heterodyne).
- **Time domain** $x(t)$ and **frequency domain** $X(f)$ are equivalent representations (Fourier pair).
- **Parseval's theorem:** total energy is identical in both domains.

### Key Formulas
- Frequency resolution: $\Delta f = f_s / N = 1 / T_w$
- Nyquist limit: $f_{\max} = f_s / 2$
- One-sided bins: $N_L = N / 2$
- Octave band edges: $f_u = f_c \sqrt{2}$, $f_l = f_c / \sqrt{2}$
- Overall level: $L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10} \sum 10^{L_i/10}$
- PSD units: $\text{g}^2/\text{Hz}$ or $\text{Pa}^2/\text{Hz}$
- FFT cost: $O(N \log_2 N)$

### Analyser Types
- **Analogue swept-filter (heterodyne)** — sequential, high dynamic range, RF/microwave.
- **Digital filter bank** — octave/1/3-octave, real-time, regulatory compliance.
- **FFT analyser** — full spectrum, transient capture, vibration diagnostics.
- **Order tracker** — shaft-synchronous, variable-speed machines.

### Critical Concepts for Examinations
- **Aliasing** — prevented by anti-alias filter with $f_c \le f_s/2$.
- **Spectral leakage** — minimised by windowing (default = Hanning).
- **Resolution vs. time-record** — to halve $\Delta f$, double $T_w$ (or double $N$ at constant $f_s$).
- **Window trade-off** — narrower main lobe ↔ higher sidelobes; Hanning is the safe default.
- **Dynamic range** — limited by ADC bits; 24-bit ADC ≈ 144 dB theoretical.
- **Averaging** — improves SNR by $10 \log_{10} M$ dB for $M$ averages.
- **dB addition** — *always* convert to linear first, sum, then convert back.

### Standard Centre Frequencies (1/1 octave, Hz)
**16 — 31.5 — 63 — 125 — 250 — 500 — 1000 — 2000 — 4000 — 8000 — 16000**

### Standard Centre Frequencies (1/3 octave, Hz, base-10 series)
**16, 20, 25, 31.5, 40, 50, 63, 80, 100, 125, 160, 200, 250, 315, 400, 500, 630, 800, 1000, 1250, 1600, 2000, 2500, 3150, 4000, 5000, 6300, 8000, 10000, 12500, 16000, 20000**

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line takeaway for the exam hall:**
> *An FFT analyser trades time-record length for frequency resolution ($\Delta f = 1/T_w$); a window suppresses leakage at the cost of main-lobe width; the overall broadband level is the log-sum of spectral band levels, not their arithmetic mean.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weighting Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT632 - INDUSTRIAL NOISE AND VIBRATION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Weighting Networks # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Weighting Networks** are standardized, frequency-dependent electronic filter circuits (analog or digital) embedded in sound level meters that modify the amplitude of an incoming acoustic signal according to prescribed frequency-response curves. They are used to mimic the variable sensitivity of the human auditory system across the audible spectrum, thereby producing a single-number metric (e.g., **dBA**, **dBC**) that correlates with the perceived loudness, hazard, or annoyance of the sound. The most widely accepted standards governing these networks are: - **IEC 61672-1:2013** – Sound level meters (current international standard) - **IEC 60651** (legacy) and **ANSI S1.4** (US equivalent) - **ISO 226:2003** – Equal-loudness contours (the psychoacoustic basis for A and C weightings) ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "Tinted Spectacles for Sound":** > Imagine you are judging the brightness of a room. Your eyes are naturally most sensitive to yellow-green light and less sensitive to deep red and violet. If you wore a pair of *yellow-tinted glasses*, the room would look more uniform — what was previously dim red light and dim violet light would now both appear to contribute proportionally to your sense of brightness. > > A **weighting network does exactly this for the ear.** It "tints" the sound spectrum so that frequencies the human ear finds less important (very low bass < 50 Hz and very high treble > 10 kHz) are attenuated before the level is read on the meter. The reading on the display therefore better reflects what a person actually *hears* and is bothered by. ## 1.3 Why Weighting Networks Exist The human ear is **not** a flat microphone. Two pure tones of equal sound pressure but different frequencies are perceived with very different loudness. The **equal-loudness contours** of Fletcher–Munson (1933) and the modern ISO 226 standard show that: - At low SPL (~20 dB), the ear is most sensitive near **3–4 kHz**. - At high SPL (~100 dB), the contours flatten considerably. - Below ~500 Hz and above ~5 kHz, sensitivity drops sharply. A single broadband SPL reading in **dB (flat / linear / Z)** would therefore misrepresent human perception. Weighting networks mathematically approximate these contours so that industrial hygienists, audiologists, and engineers can quote *one meaningful number*. ## 1.4 Classification of Weighting Networks | Network | Design Loudness Level | Primary Use | Status | |---|---|---|---| | **A-weighting (dBA)** | ~40 phons | General noise, occupational exposure, environmental noise | **Current standard** | | **B-weighting (dBB)** | ~70 phons | Intermediate loudness (historical) | **Obsolete** (removed in IEC 61672) | | **C-weighting (dBC)** | ~100 phons | Peak sound pressure, high-level impact noise | **Current standard** | | **D-weighting (dBD)** | Aircraft noise | Military & civil aviation | **Specialized** | | **Z-weighting (linear)** | None (flat 10 Hz – 20 kHz) | Reference, FFT, octave-band data | **Current standard** | --- > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, you are expected to know the *shape* of the A-, C-, and Z-weighting curves, the *numerical octave-band corrections*, the *transfer-function poles*, and how to compute a *weighted overall level* from octave-band data. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The A-Weighting Filter — Engineering Anatomy The A-weighting network is approximated by a **4th-order analogue filter** with three high-pass sections and one low-pass shelving section. Its squared magnitude response is standardized as: $$ |H_A(f)|^{2} \;=\; \frac{(2\pi f)^{4}\, K_{A}^{2}}{\left[(2\pi f)^{2}+(2\pi f_{1})^{2}\right]\left[(2\pi f)^{2}+(2\pi f_{2})^{2}\right]\left[(2\pi f)^{2}+(2\pi f_{3})^{2}\right]\left[(2\pi f)^{2}+(2\pi f_{4})^{2}\right]} $$ The four **pole frequencies** are tabulated below.
