These are the *exact* values from IEC 61672-1 to ensure worldwide uniformity between instruments. | Pole Index | Frequency $f_i$ (Hz) | Time Constant $\tau_i = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f_i}$ (s) | Function | |---|---|---|---| | $f_1$ | **20.598997** | $7.733 \times 10^{-3}$ | First high-pass roll-off | | $f_2$ | **107.65265** | $1.478 \times 10^{-3}$ | Second high-pass roll-off | | $f_3$ | **737.86223** | $2.157 \times 10^{-4}$ | Third high-pass roll-off | | $f_4$ | **12 194.217** | $1.305 \times 10^{-5}$ | Low-pass roll-off | The normalization constant is fixed at: $$ K_{A} \;\approx\; 10^{2.00} \;\;(\text{so that } \vert H_A \vert = 1 \text{ at } 1\;\text{kHz}) $$ ### 2.1.1 Logarithmic (dB) Form The response in decibels relative to 1 kHz is: $$ \Delta L_{A}(f) \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left(\vert H_A(f)\vert^{2}\right) \;\text{dB} $$ which expands to: $$ \Delta L_{A}(f) \;=\; 20\,\log_{10}(2\pi f) \;-\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left[(2\pi f)^{2}+(2\pi f_{1})^{2}\right] \;-\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left[(2\pi f)^{2}+(2\pi f_{2})^{2}\right] $$ $$ -\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left[(2\pi f)^{2}+(2\pi f_{3})^{2}\right] \;-\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left[(2\pi f)^{2}+(2\pi f_{4})^{2}\right] \;+\; \text{const} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why 4 poles?** Each pole-pair contributes a 12 dB/octave slope. Three pole-pairs in cascade at the low end give a 36 dB/octave high-pass rise, while the upper pole-pair produces a 12 dB/octave high-frequency roll-off — exactly matching the shape of the 40-phon equal-loudness contour (inverted). --- ## 2.2 The C-Weighting Filter C-weighting is a **2-pole analogue** designed to approximate the **100-phon** equal-loudness contour. It has only high-pass action at very low frequencies: $$ \vert H_C(f)\vert^{2} \;=\; \frac{(2\pi f)^{2}\,K_{C}^{2}}{\left[(2\pi f)^{2}+(2\pi f_{L})^{2}\right]\left[(2\pi f)^{2}+(2\pi f_{H})^{2}\right]} $$ | Pole | Frequency (Hz) | |---|---| | $f_L$ | **20.598997** (identical to A) | | $f_H$ | **12 194.217** (identical to A) | | $K_C \approx 10^{0.06}$ | — | Because C-weighting is essentially flat from ~31.5 Hz to ~8 kHz, it is excellent for measuring **peak / impact** noise (where unweighted peak capture matters). ## 2.3 Z-Weighting (Linear / "Zero" Weighting) $$ \vert H_Z(f)\vert \;=\; 1 \;\;\text{for}\;\; 10\;\text{Hz} \le f \le 20\;\text{kHz} $$ Out-of-band, the response rolls off at 6 dB/oct (–3 dB at 10 Hz and 20 kHz). Z is the modern replacement for "linear" and is used as the reference when no perceptual correction is desired. ## 2.4 Standard Octave-Band Corrections The IEC/ANSI standards publish tabulated A-weighting corrections $C_A$ that you add to a flat SPL measured in a 1/1- or 1/3-octave band. These are extremely high-yield for KTU numerical problems. | Octave Band $f_c$ (Hz) | A-weighting $C_A$ (dB) | C-weighting $C_C$ (dB) | |---|---|---| | 31.5 | **−39.4** | −3.0 | | 63 | **−26.2** | −0.8 | | 125 | **−16.1** | −0.2 | | 250 | **−8.6** | 0.0 | | 500 | **−3.2** | 0.0 | | 1000 | **0.0** | 0.0 | | 2000 | **+1.2** | −0.2 | | 4000 | **+1.0** | −0.8 | | 8000 | **−1.1** | −3.0 | | 16 000 | **−6.6** | −8.5 | ## 2.5 Overall Weighted SPL from Octave-Band Data If octave-band unweighted SPLs $L_i$ are measured, the overall A-weighted level is: $$ L_{A,\text{eq}} \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\,\sum_{i=1}^{N}\,10^{\,(L_i + C_{A,i})/10}\,\right]\;\;\text{dBA} $$ > **Operational Sequence:** Measure each band's SPL → add the band's $C_A$ → combine powers (not dB!) → take 10·log₁₀. ## 2.6 Time Weighting (Fast / Slow / Impulse) A separate but related concept. Time weighting smooths the **RMS detector** output: | Setting | Exponential Time Constant $\tau$ | Use | |---|---|---| | **Fast (F)** | **0.125 s** | General industrial noise (most common) | | **Slow (S)** | **1.000 s** | Slowly varying noise, traffic | | **Impulse (I)** | **0.035 s** attack / 1.5 s decay | Sharp impacts, gunshots (legacy only) | The detector output is: $$ y(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{\tau}\int_{-\infty}^{t} x^{2}(u)\,e^{-(t-u)/\tau}\,du $$ ## 2.7 Derived Industrial-Hygiene Quantities $$ L_{A,\text{eq},T} \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\,\frac{1}{T}\!\int_{0}^{T}\!10^{L_{A}(t)/10}\,dt\,\right] $$ $$ \text{SEL} \;=\; L_{A,\text{eq},T} + 10\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{T}{1\;\text{s}}\right) $$ $$ \text{TWA} \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left[\,\frac{1}{8}\!\int_{0}^{8\text{h}}\!10^{L_{A}(t)/10}\,dt\,\right] + q $$ where $q$ is the exchange-rate correction (OSHA: $+5$ for 90 dBA, NIOSH: $+10\log_{10}(T/8)$). --- ## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / When Used | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta L_A(f)=10\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{(2\pi f)^4 K_A^2}{\prod_i\!\left((2\pi f)^2+(2\pi f_i)^2\right)}\right]$ | Frequency response of A-weighting | | 2 | $f_i \in \{20.6,\;107.65,\;737.86,\;12\,194\}$ Hz | The four A-weighting pole frequencies | | 3 | $L_{A,\text{eq}}=10\log_{10}\!\left[\sum_i 10^{(L_i+C_{A,i})/10}\right]$ | Overall dBA from octave bands | | 4 | $C_{A,1\text{k}} = 0\;\text{dB}$ | A-weighting reference | | 5 | $\tau_{F}=0.125$ s, $\tau_{S}=1$ s | Fast / Slow time constants | | 6 | $L_{A,\text{eq},T}=10\log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{T}\!\int_0^T 10^{L_A/10}\,dt\right]$ | Equivalent continuous level | | 7 | $\text{SEL}=L_{A,\text{eq},T}+10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{T}{1\;\text{s}}\right)$ | Sound Exposure Level | | 8 | $\vert H_Z \vert = 1$ for $10\;\text{Hz} \le f \le 20\;\text{kHz}$ | Linear weighting | | 9 | $L_{pC}-L_{pA} = \text{PNR}$ | PNR used to detect tonal content | | 10 | OSHA Action Level = **85 dBA TWA** | Trigger for hearing-conservation program | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the A-Weighting Transfer Function from Equal-Loudness Contours **Goal:** Show *why* the 4-pole structure arises from the 40-phon contour. **Step 1 — Choose the 40-phon contour.** The 40-phon equal-loudness contour from ISO 226:2003 shows the SPL required at each pure-tone frequency to sound *as loud as* a 40-phon 1 kHz tone. At low frequencies (say 31.5 Hz) the required SPL is ~+60 dB higher than at 1 kHz → meaning the ear is **less sensitive by 60 dB**. **Step 2 — Invert the contour.** A weighting network must *attenuate* the signal in inverse proportion to ear sensitivity. So if the ear needs +60 dB more SPL at 31.5 Hz, the network should subtract ~60 dB at 31.5 Hz. At 1 kHz the ear matches the contour (no correction needed, 0 dB). **Step 3 — Match slopes empirically.** The 40-phon contour rises with a slope of approximately **+9 dB per octave** below 1 kHz (steep, because ear sensitivity plummets). That slope, accumulated over four octaves from 31.5 Hz to 500 Hz, requires a 4th-order high-pass to reproduce in analogue electronics. **Step 4 — Standardize the corner frequencies.** To produce exactly the contour shape, the IEC committee chose the four pole frequencies $f_1, f_2, f_3, f_4$ such that: - $f_4 = 20\,000 \cdot f_1$ (geometrical spread) - The combined response matches the contour at octave-band centres to within $\pm 0.5$ dB. This is why: $$ f_1 = 20.599\;\text{Hz},\quad f_2 = 107.653\;\text{Hz},\quad f_3 = 737.862\;\text{Hz},\quad f_4 = 12\,194.22\;\text{Hz} $$ **Step 5 — Normalize at 1 kHz.** Set $\vert H_A(1\,\text{kHz}) \vert = 1$ (i.e., 0 dB). This fixes $K_A \approx 10^{2.00}$. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Calculating Overall $L_A$ from Octave Bands > **Problem:** An industrial shop floor has the following octave-band SPLs (linear, Z-weighted). Compute the overall A-weighted level. | Band $f_c$ (Hz) | $L_i$ (dB) | $C_A$ (dB) | $L_i + C_A$ | Power $10^{(L_i+C_A)/10}$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 125 | 78 | −16.1 | 61.9 | $1.55 \times 10^{6}$ | | 250 | 82 | −8.6 | 73.4 | $2.19 \times 10^{7}$ | | 500 | 85 | −3.2 | 81.8 | $1.51 \times 10^{8}$ | | 1000 | 86 | 0.0 | 86.0 | $3.98 \times 10^{8}$ | | 2000 | 84 | +1.2 | 85.2 | $3.31 \times 10^{8}$ | | 4000 | 81 | +1.0 | 82.0 | $1.58 \times 10^{8}$ | ### Step-by-step computation **Step 1 — Add the A-weighting correction to each band.** $$ L_{A,i} \;=\; L_i + C_{A,i} $$ For example, at 1 kHz: $L_{A,1000}=86+0=86\;\text{dB}$. **Step 2 — Convert each $L_{A,i}$ from dB to linear power.** $$ P_i \;=\; 10^{\,L_{A,i}/10} $$ We do this for every band. The arithmetic is shown in the right-most column above. **Step 3 — Sum the powers (NOT the dB values!).** $$ P_{\text{total}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{6} P_i \;=\; (0.155 + 2.19 + 15.1 + 39.8 + 33.1 + 15.8)\times 10^{7} $$ $$ P_{\text{total}} \;=\; (0.155+2.19+15.1+39.8+33.1+15.8)\times 10^{7} $$ Adding the coefficients inside the parenthesis: $$ 0.155 + 2.19 + 15.1 + 39.8 + 33.1 + 15.8 \;=\; 106.145 $$ Therefore: $$ P_{\text{total}} \;\approx\; 1.06145 \times 10^{9} $$ **Step 4 — Convert summed power back to dB.** $$ L_{A,\text{overall}} \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left(1.06145 \times 10^{9}\right) $$ $$ L_{A,\text{overall}} \;=\; 10\,\big[\,\log_{10}(1.06145)+\log_{10}(10^{9})\,\big] $$ $$ L_{A,\text{overall}} \;=\; 10\,(0.0258 + 9) \;=\; 90.26\;\text{dBA} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** The broadband A-weighted level is **90.3 dBA**, which exceeds OSHA's 85 dBA action level and the 90 dBA PEL — a hearing-conservation program is mandatory. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of $L_{A,\text{eq},T}$ from a Non-Uniform Time History **Problem:** A worker is exposed to two noise levels in an 8-hour shift: 95 dBA for 3 hours, then 80 dBA for 5 hours. Compute $L_{A,\text{eq,8h}}$ (OSHA criterion). **Step 1 — Convert time fractions to durations (in seconds):** $$ t_1 = 3 \times 3600 = 10\,800\;\text{s},\qquad t_2 = 5\times 3600 = 18\,000\;\text{s},\qquad T = 8\times 3600 = 28\,800\;\text{s} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the time-average of the antilog:** $$ \overline{P} \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\left[t_1\cdot 10^{L_1/10} + t_2\cdot 10^{L_2/10}\right] $$ Insert numbers: $$ \overline{P} \;=\; \frac{1}{28\,800}\Big[10\,800\cdot 10^{95/10} + 18\,000\cdot 10^{80/10}\Big] $$ $$ \overline{P} \;=\; \frac{1}{28\,800}\Big[10\,800\cdot(3.162\times 10^{9}) + 18\,000\cdot(10^{8})\Big] $$ $$ \overline{P} \;=\; \frac{1}{28\,800}\Big[3.415\times 10^{13} + 1.800\times 10^{12}\Big] $$ $$ \overline{P} \;=\; \frac{3.595\times 10^{13}}{28\,800} \;=\; 1.248\times 10^{9} $$ **Step 3 — Take 10·log₁₀:** $$ L_{A,\text{eq,8h}} \;=\; 10\,\log_{10}\!\left(1.248\times 10^{9}\right) \;=\; 10\,(9.096)\;\text{dBA} $$ $$ L_{A,\text{eq,8h}} \;\approx\; 90.96\;\text{dBA} \;\approx\; 91\;\text{dBA} $$ **Step 4 — Apply OSHA 5-dB exchange rate (optional, KTU bonus):** $$ \text{Dose\%} \;=\; 100\left[\frac{t_1}{T_{1,\text{allowed}}}+\frac{t_2}{T_{2,\text{allowed}}}\right] $$ with $T_{\text{allowed}}(L)=8\cdot 2^{(90-L)/5}$. We get Dose ≈ 207 %, TWA = 90.96 dBA. > **Engineering Insight:** Although the worker spent more time at 80 dBA, the high-level exposure dominated because sound power is logarithmic. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Weighting | Application | Recommended Weighting | Reasoning | |---|---|---| | OSHA / NIOSH occupational TWA | **A-weighted (dBA)** | Correlates with hearing damage risk | | Hearing protector selection (NRR) | **A-weighted** | NRR rating applied to dBA | | Environmental noise / EIA / MoEF | **A-weighted, $L_{eq}$** | Matches community annoyance | | Peak impact / gunfire measurement | **C-weighted or Z-weighted peak** | A-weighting under-represents peaks | | IEC 61672-1 SLM verification | **All four (A, C, Z, and flat)** | Compliance testing | | Octave-band analyser | **Z (linear)** input, then compute A/C in post | Avoids double-weighting | | Aircraft flyover (older practice) | **D-weighted** (now largely superseded by SEL/dBA) | Spectral shape matches aircraft | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Sound Level Meter — Functional Block Diagram The following diagram illustrates how the weighting network sits inside a modern SLM signal chain (IEC 61672-1 architecture): ```mermaid flowchart LR mic["Microphone
1/2 inch free-field"] preamp["Preamplifier + ICP Power"] attn["Attenuator
Switchable"] wgt{"User selects
weighting"} aW["A-weighting
filter"] cW["C-weighting
filter"] zW["Z-weighting
flat 10Hz-20kHz"] rms["True-RMS
Detector"] tw{"Time-weighting
Fast/Slow/Impulse"} log["Logarithmic
Amplifier"] disp["Display
dB SPL"] ovld["Overload
Indicator"] mic --> preamp --> attn --> wgt wgt --> aW wgt --> cW wgt --> zW aW --> rms cW --> rms zW --> rms rms --> tw --> log --> disp attn --> ovld ``` ### Description of Functional Blocks | Block | Function | |---|---| | **Microphone** | Converts acoustic pressure to electrical voltage; 50 mV/Pa typical | | **Preamp + ICP** | Provides impedance matching + constant-current power (IEPE standard) | | **Attenuator** | Range selection to avoid clipping; typically 0–120 dB in 10 dB steps | | **A/C/Z Filter Bank** | Implements the IEC 61672-1 standardized frequency response | | **True-RMS Detector** | Computes $\sqrt{\langle p^{2}(t)\rangle}$; Class-1 vs Class-2 tolerance | | **Time Weighting** | Exponential smoothing; Fast = 0.125 s, Slow = 1 s | | **Log Amplifier** | Converts linear RMS to dB (log₁₀ of $p/p_0$, $p_0 = 20\;\mu$Pa) | | **Display** | Numeric + bar-graph, with min/max hold and $L_{eq}$ integrator | --- ## 4.2 A-Weighting Frequency Response — Approximate Behaviour ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LOW["LOW FREQUENCY ROLL-OFF"] f1["-39.4 dB at 31.5 Hz"] f2["-26.2 dB at 63 Hz"] f3["-16.1 dB at 125 Hz"] f4["-8.6 dB at 250 Hz"] f5["-3.2 dB at 500 Hz"] end subgraph MID["MID-FREQUENCY PASS BAND"] m1["0 dB at 1 kHz (reference)"] m2["+1.2 dB at 2 kHz"] m3["+1.0 dB at 4 kHz"] end subgraph HIGH["HIGH FREQUENCY ROLL-OFF"] h1["-1.1 dB at 8 kHz"] h2["-6.6 dB at 16 kHz"] end LOW --> MID --> HIGH ``` > [!NOTE] > The mid-frequency region (1–5 kHz) is where the human ear is most sensitive to noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL). A-weighting slightly *boosts* 2–4 kHz (+1 dB) before rolling off — this is critical because 4 kHz is the classic "audiometric notch" frequency affected by industrial noise. --- ## 4.3 Decision Flow — Choosing Time and Frequency Weighting ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Start: Noise to be measured]) --> q1{Is the noise
impulsive?} q1 -- "Yes, sharp impacts" --> peak["Use C-weighted Peak
or Z-weighted Peak"] q1 -- "No, steady-state" --> q2{Is the noise
for occupational
exposure?} q2 -- "Yes" --> aTWA["Use A-weighted
+ Slow time
+ 8h Leq or TWA"] q2 -- "No" --> q3{Is the noise
for community
annoyance?} q3 -- "Yes" --> aLeq["Use A-weighted
+ Fast time
+ Leq over specified hours"] q3 -- "No, engineering analysis" --> zOb["Use Z-weighted
+ Octave bands
+ spectral post-processing"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define A-weighting. Why is it preferred over linear (Z) weighting for occupational noise assessment?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **A-weighting** is a frequency-response characteristic prescribed by IEC 61672-1 for sound level meters. It applies a frequency-dependent attenuation that approximates the **40-phon equal-loudness contour** of the human ear. > > **(1 mark)** It strongly attenuates frequencies below ~500 Hz and above ~5 kHz, with **0 dB correction at 1 kHz** (reference). > **(1 mark)** The frequency response is implemented as a **4-pole filter** with corner frequencies 20.6, 107.7, 737.9 and 12 194 Hz. > **(1 mark)** A-weighting is preferred for occupational noise because **hearing damage risk correlates strongly with A-weighted exposure (dBA)**; OSHA and NIOSH exposure limits (TWA 85–90 dBA), audiometric notch data, and hearing-protector ratings (NRR) are all defined in dBA. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **State the time constants for Fast and Slow exponential time-weightings. In which type of noise environment is each recommended?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Setting | Time Constant $\tau$ | Recommended For | |---|---|---| | **Fast (F)** | **0.125 s** | General industrial / environmental noise with moderate fluctuation (default) | | **Slow (S)** | **1.000 s** | Slowly varying noise (traffic, HVAC) where Fast shows excessive needle jitter | | **Impulse (I)** | 0.035 s attack / 1.5 s decay | Legacy support only for sharp impacts | **(1 mark)** Each definition + value. **(1 mark)** Fast = 0.125 s rationale (default, less smoothing). **(1 mark)** Slow = 1 s rationale (smoothes fluctuating noise; also used when occupational standard specifies Slow). --- ## PART B — Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each question has sub-part (a) for **7 marks** and sub-part (b) for **7 marks**. Both questions (Choice A and Choice B) cover the same module — answer **either** A **or** B. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the architecture of an IEC 61672-1 Sound Level Meter. Explain the role of the weighting network in this instrument. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(1) Block diagram (3 marks)** — already shown in Section 4.1 of these notes. The student should draw: Microphone → Preamp → Attenuator → Weighting Filter (A/C/Z) → RMS Detector → Time Weighting (F/S) → Log Amplifier → Display, with an Overload path. Each block must be labelled. **(2) Role of the weighting network (2 marks):** The weighting filter shapes the frequency content of the electrical signal to match a standardized response. For A-weighting this matches the **40-phon equal-loudness contour**; the resulting dBA reading then correlates with subjective loudness and with hearing-damage risk. **(3) Standards compliance (2 marks):** The IEC 61672-1 standard mandates **tolerances** of $\pm 0.7$ dB (Class 1) and $\pm 1.5$ dB (Class 2) at 1 kHz, with specified octave-band correction tables $C_A$ and $C_C$. Class-1 instruments are required for legal/regulatory work; Class-2 for general industrial surveys. --- **(b)** The octave-band sound pressure levels measured in a workshop are: | Band (Hz) | 125 | 250 | 500 | 1000 | 2000 | 4000 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | SPL (dB) | 78 | 82 | 85 | 86 | 84 | 81 | **Calculate the overall A-weighted sound pressure level. Comment on the result with respect to OSHA standards.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify A-weighting corrections** (1 mark): | Band | $L_i$ | $C_A$ | |---|---|---| | 125 | 78 | −16.1 | | 250 | 82 | −8.6 | | 500 | 85 | −3.2 | | 1000 | 86 | 0 | | 2000 | 84 | +1.2 | | 4000 | 81 | +1.0 | **Step 2 — Add corrections and compute powers** (2 marks): | Band | $L_i+C_A$ | Power $10^{(L_i+C_A)/10}$ | |---|---|---| | 125 | 61.9 | $1.55\times10^{6}$ | | 250 | 73.4 | $2.19\times10^{7}$ | | 500 | 81.8 | $1.51\times10^{8}$ | | 1000 | 86.0 | $3.98\times10^{8}$ | | 2000 | 85.2 | $3.31\times10^{8}$ | | 4000 | 82.0 | $1.58\times10^{8}$ | **Step 3 — Sum and convert to dB** (2 marks): $$ P_{\text{tot}} \;=\; (0.155+2.19+15.1+39.8+33.1+15.8)\times10^{7} \;=\; 1.0615\times10^{9} $$ $$ L_A \;=\; 10\log_{10}(1.0615\times10^{9}) \;\approx\; 90.3\;\text{dBA} $$ **Step 4 — Comment on OSHA standards** (2 marks): OSHA's Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) is **90 dBA TWA (8 h)** with an exchange rate of 5 dB; the action level is **85 dBA**. The shop floor reading of 90.3 dBA therefore **exceeds the OSHA Action Level** — engineering noise control (silencers, isolation, damping) and/or administrative controls plus hearing-protection devices (HPDs) are mandatory under 29 CFR 1910.95. --- ### ⭐ QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **(a)** Derive the squared magnitude response of the A-weighting filter from the equal-loudness contour concept. State the standard pole frequencies and explain why four poles are required. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Concept of equal-loudness contour (1 mark):** The 40-phon contour (ISO 226:2003) shows the SPL required at each frequency to sound as loud as a 40-dB 1 kHz tone. Below 500 Hz the contour rises steeply (~+9 dB/octave); above 5 kHz it falls. **Step 2 — Inversion yields the filter (2 marks):** The A-weighting network must subtract the ear's reduced sensitivity. The required correction therefore has 0 dB at 1 kHz, large negative dB below 500 Hz, and smaller negative dB above 5 kHz — exactly the form: $$ \vert H_A(f)\vert^{2}=\dfrac{(2\pi f)^4 K_A^2}{\prod_{i=1}^{4}\left[(2\pi f)^2+(2\pi f_i)^2\right]} $$ **Step 3 — Standard pole frequencies (2 marks):** $f_1=20.599$ Hz, $f_2=107.653$ Hz, $f_3=737.862$ Hz, $f_4=12\,194.22$ Hz (per IEC 61672-1). **Step 4 — Why four poles (2 marks):** Three pole-pairs at the low end contribute ~36 dB/octave rise, matching the steep low-frequency contour; the fourth pole-pair gives the ~12 dB/octave roll-off above 5 kHz. Two poles are insufficient to fit the contour to within IEC tolerances. --- **(b)** A worker spends **3 hours at 95 dBA** and **5 hours at 80 dBA** during an 8-hour shift. **(i)** Calculate the OSHA Time-Weighted Average (TWA). **(ii)** Determine whether the dose exceeds OSHA's Permissible Exposure Limit. Use the 5-dB exchange rate. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert to powers** (1 mark): $$ P_1=10^{95/10}=3.162\times10^{9},\qquad P_2=10^{80/10}=1.000\times10^{8} $$ **Step 2 — Time-weighted power average (2 marks):** $$ \overline{P}=\dfrac{(3\cdot3600)(3.162\times10^{9})+(5\cdot3600)(1.0\times10^{8})}{8\cdot3600} $$ $$ \overline{P}=\dfrac{3.415\times10^{13}+1.800\times10^{12}}{28\,800}=1.248\times10^{9} $$ **Step 3 — TWA in dBA (1 mark):** $$ \text{TWA}=10\log_{10}(1.248\times10^{9})=90.96\;\text{dBA}\;\approx\;91\;\text{dBA} $$ **Step 4 — OSHA criterion with 5-dB exchange (2 marks):** $$ \text{Allowed time at 95 dBA}=8\cdot 2^{(90-95)/5}=8\cdot 2^{-1}=4\;\text{hours} $$ $$ \text{Allowed time at 80 dBA}=8\cdot 2^{(90-80)/5}=8\cdot 2^{2}=32\;\text{hours} $$ $$ \text{Dose\%}=100\left(\dfrac{3}{4}+\dfrac{5}{32}\right)=100\,(0.75+0.156)=90.6\% $$ **Step 5 — Conclusion (1 mark):** Dose = **90.6 % < 100 %**, so OSHA PEL is **not exceeded**, but the dose approaches the limit. Hearing protection and a hearing-conservation program are still recommended (action level 85 dBA). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Adding dB values directly** — the most common error. Energy summation (linear powers) is mandatory. Marks lost: **2 marks** out of 7. > 2. **Forgetting to add $C_A$ before computing powers** — failure to apply A-correction before the $\log_{10}$. Marks lost: **1–2 marks**. > 3. **Using $L_{eq}$ equation but missing the time-average denominator $T$** — gives a SEL instead. Marks lost: **2 marks**. > 4. **Confusing Fast (0.125 s) and Slow (1 s)** constants — examiners check explicitly. Marks lost: **1 mark**. > 5. **Mixing A-weighting with peak C-weighting** in occupational dose calculations — they are not interchangeable. Marks lost: **1 mark**. > 6. **Sign errors in the contour-inversion derivation** — emphasise that the filter *subtracts* ear sensitivity. Marks lost: **1 mark**. --- ## 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ A-weighting approximates the **40-phon equal-loudness contour**; C-weighting approximates the **100-phon** contour; Z is linear. - ✅ Pole frequencies: **20.6, 107.7, 737.9, 12 194 Hz** (memorise these for KTU). - ✅ Reference for all corrections: **0 dB at 1 kHz**. - ✅ Add A-corrections **before** power summation: $L_A = 10\log_{10}\!\sum 10^{(L_i+C_{A,i})/10}$. - ✅ Time weighting: **Fast = 0.125 s**, **Slow = 1.0 s** (RMS detector exponential). - ✅ OSHA Action Level = **85 dBA TWA**, PEL = **90 dBA TWA**, 5-dB exchange. - ✅ NIOSH uses **3 dB exchange** and REL = **85 dBA TWA**. - ✅ A-weighting slightly *boosts* 2–4 kHz (+1.2, +1.0 dB) → protects the audiometric notch. - ✅ C-weighting is used for **peak sound pressure** capture (impulsive noise). - ✅ Octave-band analysis is performed on **Z-weighted (linear)** data; weighting is applied during post-processing to avoid double-filtering. - ✅ Modern IEC 61672-1 (2013) has replaced legacy IEC 60651 / ANSI S1.4 specifications but preserves the same A, C, Z curves for backward compatibility. - ✅ Practical measurement: use **Class-1 SLM with windscreen**, hold mic **1.5 m from operator**, at **ear height**, with **Slow / Fast as per regulation**. ---

topics.title: Hearing Mechanism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT632 - INDUSTRIAL NOISE AND VIBRATION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hearing Mechanism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hearing Mechanism (Auditory Pathway / Mechanism of Sound Perception):**
> The hearing mechanism is the physiological and neurological process by which the human auditory system detects, transmits, transduces, and interprets acoustic pressure fluctuations as perceived sound. It involves three sequential mechanical/electromechanical stages: **conduction** (outer and middle ear), **transduction** (inner ear / cochlea), and **neural encoding** (auditory nerve to auditory cortex).

In the context of industrial noise control (PESFT632), the hearing mechanism is not just biological curiosity — it defines the **receiver** side of the acoustic source–path–receiver triad. Without understanding how the ear converts acoustic pressure waves into neural signals, an engineer cannot evaluate noise exposure limits, prescribe hearing protection devices (HPDs), or design effective engineering controls.

### 1.2 The Three Functional Divisions of the Human Ear

| Division | Anatomical Components | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Outer Ear** | Pinna (auricle), Ear Canal (external auditory meatus), Tympanic Membrane (eardrum) | Collects sound, localises source direction, amplifies 2–5 kHz by ~10–15 dB via canal resonance |
| **Middle Ear** | Ossicles (Malleus, Incus, Stapes), Tympanic Cavity, Eustachian Tube, Oval Window | Mechanical impedance matching (air → fluid), ~22× pressure gain |
| **Inner Ear** | Cochlea, Basilar Membrane, Organ of Corti, Hair Cells (Inner & Outer), Round Window, Auditory Nerve (CN VIII) | Frequency analysis (tonotopy), mechanoelectrical transduction |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Microphone Analogy:**
> Think of the human ear as a remarkably engineered biological microphone system:
>
> - **Pinna** = the satellite dish → captures and directs acoustic energy.
> - **Ear Canal** = a resonant waveguide tube → boosts mid-frequency speech sounds naturally (like a tuned quarter-wave resonator).
> - **Eardrum + Ossicles** = an impedance-matching transformer → solves the same engineering problem we face when transmitting power from a high-impedance source to a low-impedance load (here, air → cochlear fluid). Without it, ~99.9% of the sound energy would be reflected due to acoustic impedance mismatch.
> - **Cochlea** = a real-time spectrum analyser (Fast Fourier Transform hardware) → separates the complex incoming sound into its frequency components along the basilar membrane.
> - **Hair Cells** = piezoelectric transducers → convert basilar membrane vibration into electrical nerve impulses.
> - **Auditory Nerve & Brain** = the digital signal processor → decodes, interprets, and assigns meaning (speech, warning, music).

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Threshold of Hearing (Reference Pressure):** $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{Pa}$ (RMS) at **1 kHz**
- **Threshold of Pain:** $p_{pain} \approx 20\ \text{Pa}$ (≈ **120 dB SPL**)
- **Normal Hearing Range (Young Adult):** **20 Hz – 20,000 Hz (20 kHz)**
- **Most Sensitive Frequency Band:** **2 kHz – 5 kHz** (corresponds to speech intelligibility region)
- **Dynamic Range of Human Ear:** ~**120 dB** (from softest detectable to threshold of pain)
- **Speed of Sound in Air:** $c \approx 343\ \text{m/s}$ (at 20 °C)
- **Density of Air:** $\rho_0 \approx 1.21\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Acoustic Impedance of Air:** $Z_0 = \rho_0 c \approx 413\ \text{Pa·s/m}$ (rayl)
- **Acoustic Impedance of Cochlear Fluid (Perilymph):** $\approx 1.5 \times 10^6\ \text{Pa·s/m}$ → hence the need for impedance matching
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stage-Wise Functional Breakdown

#### Stage 1 — Outer Ear (Sound Collection)
- The **pinna** collects acoustic waves and introduces direction-dependent filtering cues (Head-Related Transfer Function, HRTF).
- The **ear canal** (≈ 2.5 cm long, ~0.7 cm diameter) acts as an open-ended tube. Its quarter-wave resonance:
$$f_{res} \approx \frac{c}{4L} = \frac{343}{4 \times 0.025} \approx 3.43\ \text{kHz}$$
This explains why the ear is most sensitive around **2–5 kHz** — exactly where speech consonants carry maximum intelligibility information.
- The **tympanic membrane** is a thin, conical, fibrous membrane (~55 mm² area) that vibrates in response to incident pressure waves.

#### Stage 2 — Middle Ear (Impedance Matching)
- The three **ossicles** form a mechanical lever system. The **malleus** is attached to the eardrum; the **stapes** footplate sits on the **oval window** (~3.2 mm² area).
- Two simultaneous transformations occur:
  1. **Areal (Hydraulic) Ratio:** $A_{ratio} = \frac{A_{tympanic}}{A_{oval}} \approx \frac{55}{3.2} \approx 17:1$ → pressure amplification.
  2. **Ossicular Lever Ratio:** ~1.3:1 → additional mechanical advantage.
- **Net Impedance Gain:** $17 \times 1.3 \approx 22$ (≈ **27 dB gain** in pressure, recovering ~60% of the energy that would otherwise be reflected).
- The **Eustachian tube** equalises middle-ear pressure with atmospheric pressure (critical during altitude changes).

#### Stage 3 — Inner Ear — Cochlea (Frequency Analysis)
- The **cochlea** is a fluid-filled, snail-shaped organ (~35 mm long uncoiled) partitioned into three chambers: **scala vestibuli**, **scala media**, and **scala tympani**.
- The **basilar membrane (BM)** stretches along its length and varies in stiffness and width:
  - **Base** (near oval window): narrow, stiff → resonates at **high frequencies**.
  - **Apex** (helicotrema): wide, floppy → resonates at **low frequencies**.
- **Tonotopic Organisation:** Each frequency stimulates a specific location on the BM → the ear performs a real-time mechanical Fourier analysis (Békésy's travelling wave theory, 1961 Nobel Prize).
- **Hair Cells (Organ of Corti):**
  - **Inner Hair Cells (IHCs):** ~3,500 cells; primary sensory transducers (90% of afferent nerve fibres).
  - **Outer Hair Cells (OHCs):** ~12,000 cells; cochlear amplifier — actively amplify weak signals and sharpen frequency selectivity.
- **Mechanoelectrical Transduction:** Stereocilia deflection opens ion channels → K⁺ influx (driven by endocochlear potential +80 mV) → depolarisation → neurotransmitter release → action potentials in the **auditory nerve (CN VIII)**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All quantities are in SI units unless otherwise noted. Sound-pressure-level (SPL) and intensity-level references: $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$, $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$.

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_p = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right)$ | Sound Pressure Level (dB SPL) |
| 2 | $L_I = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | Sound Intensity Level (dB SIL) |
| 3 | $I = \dfrac{p_{rms}^{2}}{\rho_0 c}$ | Acoustic intensity (plane wave) |
| 4 | $L_I = L_p$ | For a plane wave (intensity and pressure level are numerically equal) |
| 5 | $L_p = L_{W} - 20\log_{10}(r) - 11$ | Point source free-field approximation (r in metres) |
| 6 | $\Delta L_p = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_1}{r_2}\right)$ | Inverse-square law doubling/halving |
| 7 | $L_{tot} = 10\log_{10}\!\sum_{i=1}^{n}10^{L_i/10}$ | Addition of incoherent sound levels |
| 8 | $L_{diff} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/10}-10^{L_2/10}\right)$ | Subtraction of sound levels |
| 9 | $L_{W} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_0}\right)$ | Sound Power Level, $W_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W}$ |
| 10 | $f_{res, canal} \approx \dfrac{c}{4L}$ | Quarter-wave ear canal resonance |
| 11 | $Z_{mech, gain} = \dfrac{A_{tymp}}{A_{oval}} \cdot \text{Lever Ratio} \approx 22$ | Middle-ear pressure gain factor |
| 12 | $S = k \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) = k \cdot L_I$ | Stevens' Power Law (loudness scaling, $k$ varies) |
| 13 | $L_{N} = 40 + 10\log_{2}(N)$ | Loudness level in phons (simplified, pure tone 1 kHz) |
| 14 | $L_A = 10\log_{10}\!\sum_{i} 10^{L_i/10}\cdot 10^{A_i/10}$ | A-weighted SPL, $A_i$ = A-weighting in dB |
| 15 | $T_{\text{exposure}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}$ | OSHA permissible exposure time (hours) |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the hearing mechanism is **non-negotiable** in industrial noise control because:

- **A-weighting** ($L_A$, dBA) mimics the ear's frequency-dependent sensitivity by de-emphasising very low and very high frequencies, reflecting the ear's reduced response outside 2–5 kHz.
- **Critical bands** (Bark scale, ERB scale) define the bandwidth over which the ear integrates acoustic energy → basis for noise criteria (NC), noise rating (NR), and room-criterion (RC) curves used in HVAC design.
- **Equal-Loudness Contours (Fletcher–Munson, ISO 226):** map phons to sones and vice versa, informing hearing-conservation programmes.
- **Temporary Threshold Shift (TTS) & Permanent Threshold Shift (PTS):** direct outcomes of overstimulation of outer hair cells → drive the 85 dBA / 8-hour industrial exposure limit.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Acoustic Impedance Mismatch & Need for Middle-Ear Transformer

The human ear must transmit acoustic energy from **air** (low acoustic impedance) to **perilymph fluid** (high acoustic impedance) inside the cochlea. If this were a direct transition, almost all energy would be reflected.

**Step 1 — Define Acoustic Impedance.**
Acoustic impedance $Z$ for a medium is the product of density and speed of sound:

$$Z_{air} = \rho_{air} \cdot c_{air}$$

Evaluating at standard conditions ($20\ ^\circ\text{C}$):

$$Z_{air} = 1.21 \times 343 = 415\ \text{Pa·s/m (rayl)}$$

**Step 2 — Impedance of Cochlear Fluid.**

$$Z_{fluid} = \rho_{water} \cdot c_{water} \approx 1000 \times 1500 = 1.5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa·s/m}$$

**Step 3 — Transmission Coefficient (Power).**
The fraction of incident acoustic power transmitted across a normal-incidence boundary is:

$$\alpha_t = \dfrac{4\,Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^{2}}$$

Substituting:

$$\alpha_t = \dfrac{4 \cdot (415) \cdot (1.5 \times 10^{6})}{\bigl(415 + 1.5 \times 10^{6}\bigr)^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify Numerator.**

$$4 \times 415 \times 1.5 \times 10^{6} = 2.49 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify Denominator.**

$$\bigl(1.500415 \times 10^{6}\bigr)^{2} \approx 2.251 \times 10^{12}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Transmission.**

$$\alpha_t \approx \dfrac{2.49 \times 10^{9}}{2.251 \times 10^{12}} \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to dB Loss.**

$$L_{loss} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\alpha_t}\right) = 10\log_{10}(910) \approx 29.6\ \text{dB}$$

> This is a **~30 dB loss**, equivalent to losing **99.9%** of incident acoustic energy. The middle-ear transformer recovers this loss by ~27 dB.

**Step 8 — Apply Ossicular Gain.**
The middle-ear transformer provides a pressure amplification factor:

$$G_{ME} = \underbrace{\dfrac{A_{tymp}}{A_{oval}}}_{\approx 17} \times \underbrace{\text{Lever Ratio}}_{\approx 1.3} \approx 22$$

In dB:

$$G_{ME,dB} = 20\log_{10}(22) \approx 26.8\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 9 — Net Transmission.**

$$\alpha_t^{eff} = 10^{-29.6/10} \cdot 10^{26.8/10} \approx 10^{-0.28} \approx 0.525$$

Thus, the middle ear raises transmission efficiency from **~0.1%** to **~52%**, a remarkable evolutionary engineering solution.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Equal-Loudness Contour Equation (ISO 226 Approximation)

The ISO 226:2003 standard specifies equal-loudness contours in terms of Sound Pressure Level (SPL) at the eardrum as a function of frequency $f$ (Hz) for a given phon level $L_N$.

**Step 1 — Phon Definition.**
A **phon** is the SPL in dB of a 1 kHz pure tone judged equally loud to the test tone.

**Step 2 — Sone Scale (Loudness).**
A **sone** is defined such that 1 sone = 40 phons. Doubling loudness corresponds to a 10-phon increase:

$$S = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$$

**Step 3 — Analytical Form (Simplified ISO 226 Fit).**
For moderate levels, an SPL at frequency $f$ required to produce loudness $L_N$ can be approximated as:

$$L_p(f) = L_N + A(f)$$

where $A(f)$ is the frequency correction (positive at low/high frequencies because higher SPL is needed for equal loudness).

**Step 4 — Computational Example.**
At 100 Hz, to sound as loud as a 40-phon reference (1 kHz), the SPL must be approximately:

$$L_p(100\ \text{Hz}) \approx 40 + 13 = 53\ \text{dB SPL}$$

This 13-dB offset reflects the ear's reduced sensitivity at low frequencies — the basis for A-weighting.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Critical Bandwidth (Bark Scale)

**Step 1 — Definition.**
A critical band is the bandwidth within which the ear integrates acoustic energy. Above the masking noise bandwidth, perceived loudness increases.

**Step 2 — Bark Scale Approximation (Zwicker, 1961).**

$$z = 13 \arctan\!\left(0.00076\,f\right) + 3.5 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{f}{7500}\right)^{2}$$

where $f$ is in Hz and $z$ is in Barks.

**Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation at $f = 1000\ \text{Hz}$.**

$$z = 13 \arctan(0.76) + 3.5 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{1000}{7500}\right)^{2}$$

$$z = 13 (0.6487) + 3.5 \arctan(0.0178)^{2}$$

$$z = 8.43 + 3.5 \cdot (0.01778)^{2} \approx 8.43 + 0.0011 \approx 8.43\ \text{Bark}$$

A 1000 Hz tone sits ~8.4 Barks up the cochlear place map.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Frequency Response of the Ear

| Frequency | Relative Sensitivity | Industrial Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| 20 Hz | Very Low (~+50 dB compensation) | Below audibility threshold for most adults |
| 250 Hz | Low (~+9 dB) | Common in HVAC and large machinery |
| 1 kHz | Reference (0 dB offset) | A-weighting anchor |
| 2–5 kHz | Maximum sensitivity (−10 to −5 dB) | Speech intelligibility, alarm signals |
| 8 kHz | Declining (+1 to +10 dB compensation) | Hi-frequency machinery, ultrasonic warning |
| 20 kHz | Near-threshold (+30 dB+) | Upper audibility limit (young adults only) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Human Auditory Pathway

The diagram below traces the complete pathway of an acoustic pressure wave from source to perception, mapped as a functional flow diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acoustic Source Vibration] --> B[Sound Pressure Wave in Air]
    B --> C[Pinna: Directional Collection]
    C --> D[Ear Canal: 2.5 cm Tube Resonance approx 3.4 kHz]
    D --> E[Tympanic Membrane Vibration]
    E --> F[Middle Ear Ossicles Malleus Incus Stapes]
    F --> G[Oval Window: Impedance Matching Gain approx 22x]
    G --> H[Cochlea: Fluid Filled Tonotopic Organ]
    H --> I[Basilar Membrane Travelling Wave]
    I --> J[Organ of Corti Hair Cells]
    J --> K[Mechanoelectrical Transduction]
    K --> L[Auditory Nerve CN VIII Action Potentials]
    L --> M[Brainstem Auditory Nuclei]
    M --> N[Thalamus Medial Geniculate Body]
    N --> O[Auditory Cortex Perception and Recognition]
    O --> P[Perceived Sound Loudness Pitch Timbre]

    subgraph OUTER_EAR[Outer Ear - Conduction]
        C
        D
        E
    end
    subgraph MIDDLE_EAR[Middle Ear - Impedance Matching]
        F
        G
    end
    subgraph INNER_EAR[Inner Ear - Frequency Analysis]
        H
        I
        J
        K
    end
    subgraph NEURAL[Neural Pathway]
        L
        M
        N
        O
    end

    style A fill:#FFD580
    style P fill:#9BC4E2
    style OUTER_EAR fill:#FFE5CC
    style MIDDLE_EAR fill:#FFD9B3
    style INNER_EAR fill:#FFCCA3
    style NEURAL fill:#B3D9FF
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Tonotopic Organisation of the Cochlea

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BASE[Base of Cochlea near Oval Window]
        H1[20000 Hz] --> H2[10000 Hz]
    end
    subgraph MID[Mid Region]
        H3[4000 Hz] --> H4[2000 Hz]
        H5[1000 Hz]
    end
    subgraph APEX[Apex of Cochlea near Helicotrema]
        H6[500 Hz] --> H7[200 Hz]
        H8[50 Hz] --> H9[20 Hz]
    end

    H2 --> H3
    H4 --> H5
    H5 --> H6
    H7 --> H8

    style BASE fill:#FFB3B3
    style MID fill:#FFE5B3
    style APEX fill:#B3FFB3
```

> **Reading Guide:** High-frequency sounds (top-left, red zone) excite the basal end of the cochlea; low-frequency sounds (bottom-right, green zone) reach the apex. The basilar membrane acts like a series of overlapping bandpass filters, each tuned to a specific centre frequency → a biological spectrum analyser.

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Impedance Matching Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Air Medium Z equals 415 rayl] -->|Incident Pressure Wave| B[Acoustic Impedance Boundary]
    B -->|Without Transformer: 30 dB Loss 99.9 percent Reflected| C[Perilymph Fluid Z equals 1.5e6 rayl]
    B -->|With Middle Ear Transformer: 27 dB Gain Recovered| D[Effective Matched Transmission approx 50 percent]
    D --> C

    style A fill:#C8E6FF
    style B fill:#FFD580
    style C fill:#C8E6FF
    style D fill:#A5D6A7
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1. Define the hearing mechanism and list the three anatomical divisions of the human ear.**
*(Module Outcome: CO2 — Apply knowledge of human auditory response to noise evaluation)*

**Model Answer:**
The hearing mechanism is the physiological and neurological process by which acoustic pressure fluctuations are detected, transmitted, transduced, and perceived as sound by the brain. The three anatomical divisions are:

1. **Outer Ear** — Pinna, ear canal, tympanic membrane → sound collection and mild amplification.
2. **Middle Ear** — Ossicles (malleus, incus, stapes), Eustachian tube, oval window → impedance matching between air and cochlear fluid.
3. **Inner Ear** — Cochlea, basilar membrane, Organ of Corti, hair cells → frequency analysis and mechanoelectrical transduction.

---

**Q2. What is the function of the Eustachian tube in the hearing mechanism?**
*(Module Outcome: CO2)*

**Model Answer:**
The Eustachian tube connects the middle-ear cavity to the nasopharynx. Its primary function is to **equalise the static air pressure** on both sides of the tympanic membrane with the ambient atmospheric pressure. This ensures the eardrum can vibrate freely without stiffness-induced distortion. It also serves to drain middle-ear secretions and protect the middle ear from pathogens. Blockage causes temporary hearing loss and discomfort (common during ascent/descent in flights).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — Internal Choice Format

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the hearing mechanism in humans. Describe the role of the outer, middle, and inner ear in sound perception. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the transmission loss due to acoustic impedance mismatch between air and cochlear fluid, and show how the middle ear compensates for this loss through its transformer action. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

The hearing mechanism involves four sequential stages:

**1. Outer Ear (Conduction Stage):**
The pinna collects acoustic waves; the ear canal acts as a quarter-wave resonator, amplifying the 2–5 kHz band by ~10–15 dB. The tympanic membrane converts acoustic pressure into mechanical vibration.

**2. Middle Ear (Impedance Matching Stage):**
The ossicles (malleus, incus, stapes) form a lever that transmits vibration from the tympanic membrane to the oval window, providing a pressure gain of ~22× (≈27 dB). This overcomes the natural ~30 dB loss that would occur at the air-fluid boundary.

**3. Inner Ear (Frequency Analysis Stage):**
Inside the cochlea, the basilar membrane supports a travelling wave whose peak location depends on frequency (high frequencies → base; low frequencies → apex). This tonotopic organisation performs a mechanical frequency decomposition.

**4. Neural Encoding:**
Hair cells in the Organ of Corti transduce mechanical motion into electrical signals via stereocilia deflection → neurotransmitter release → action potentials in the auditory nerve → transmission to the brainstem, thalamus, and finally the auditory cortex, where perception occurs.

*(Block diagram as shown in SECTION 4.1 should accompany this answer.)*

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

The detailed derivation is presented in **SECTION 3.1** of these notes. The key steps are summarised:

**Step 1:** Calculate acoustic impedance of air and perilymph:
$$Z_{air} = 415\ \text{rayl}, \quad Z_{fluid} = 1.5 \times 10^{6}\ \text{rayl}$$

**Step 2:** Apply normal-incidence transmission coefficient:
$$\alpha_t = \dfrac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^{2}} \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the dB loss:
$$L_{loss} = 10\log_{10}(910) \approx 29.6\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 4:** Apply middle-ear transformer action:
$$G_{ME} = \dfrac{A_{tympanic}}{A_{oval}} \times \text{Lever Ratio} \approx 17 \times 1.3 = 22$$
$$G_{ME,dB} = 20\log_{10}(22) \approx 26.8\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 5:** Net transmission efficiency:
$$\alpha_t^{eff} \approx 10^{(26.8-29.6)/10} \approx 0.525 \quad (\text{or}\ 52.5\%)$$

**Conclusion:** The middle ear raises the transmission efficiency from ~0.1% to ~52.5%, a recovery of ~27 dB. This transformer action is essential because the cochlea sits behind a high-impedance fluid boundary that would otherwise reflect almost all incident acoustic energy.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Define A-weighted Sound Pressure Level (dBA). With reference to the equal-loudness contours, justify why A-weighting is appropriate for industrial noise assessment. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the safe exposure time (per OSHA standard) for a worker exposed to 100 dBA continuous noise. If two machines produce 90 dBA and 90 dBA individually, what is the combined SPL when both operate simultaneously? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Definition:**
The A-weighted Sound Pressure Level, expressed in **dBA**, is obtained by applying a frequency-dependent weighting to the measured SPL spectrum before logarithmic summation:

$$L_A = 10\log_{10}\!\sum_{i} 10^{(L_i + A_i)/10}$$

where $L_i$ is the SPL in the $i$-th octave/one-third-octave band and $A_i$ is the A-weighting correction (in dB) for that band. Reference: IEC 61672 and ISO 226.

**Justification Using Equal-Loudness Contours:**

Equal-loudness contours (Fletcher–Munson curves, refined as ISO 226) show that human hearing sensitivity is **not flat** with frequency:
- At 40 phons, low frequencies (e.g., 100 Hz) require SPL ~13 dB higher than at 1 kHz to sound equally loud.
- At high phons, this disparity narrows, but the ear is most sensitive in the 2–5 kHz range at all loudness levels.

The A-weighting curve (inverse of the 40-phon contour, approximated) **de-emphasises** low and very high frequencies by up to ~26.1 dB (at 100 Hz) and ~50 dB (below 20 Hz), mirroring the ear's reduced response in these regions. Hence, dBA correlates better with perceived loudness and with the ear's actual risk of damage than raw dB SPL — making it the standard metric for industrial hygiene and hearing-conservation regulations worldwide.

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — OSHA Permissible Exposure Time:**

OSHA's formula (29 CFR 1910.95) is:

$$T_{\text{safe}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}}$$

For $L = 100$ dBA:

$$T_{\text{safe}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{(100-90)/5}} = \dfrac{8}{2^{2}} = \dfrac{8}{4} = 2\ \text{hours}$$

**Step 2 — Combined SPL of Two Identical Sources:**

For two **incoherent** (uncorrelated) sources, use logarithmic summation:

$$L_{tot} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}\right)$$

With $L_1 = L_2 = 90$ dBA:

$$L_{tot} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(10^{9} + 10^{9}\right) = 10\log_{10}\!\left(2 \times 10^{9}\right)$$

$$L_{tot} = 10 \times (\log_{10} 2 + 9) = 10 \times (0.3010 + 9) = 93.01\ \text{dBA}$$

**Result:** Combined SPL ≈ **93 dBA**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** add decibel values linearly. A common mistake is to write 90 + 90 = 180 dB. The correct answer is **93 dB**, only 3 dB higher than a single source.
> 2. **Remember the 3 dB rule:** Two identical, uncorrelated sources → +3 dB. Ten identical sources → +10 dB. Doubling the number of sources adds only 3 dB.
> 3. **OSHA exposure limit:** Always use **90 dBA** as the reference (not 85 dBA, which is the NIOSH recommendation). Be precise about which standard the examiner expects.
> 4. **Hearing mechanism question:** When asked about the "hearing mechanism," students often describe only the ear's anatomy without explaining the **transduction process** or the **impedance matching function**. Ensure both anatomy and function are explicitly addressed.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hearing Mechanism**

- **Three Divisions of the Ear:** Outer (collection), Middle (impedance matching), Inner (frequency analysis + transduction).
- **Ear Canal Resonance:** $\approx 3.4\ \text{kHz}$ — explains why the ear is most sensitive in the **2–5 kHz** band (speech intelligibility region).
- **Middle-Ear Pressure Gain:** $\approx 22\times$ (≈27 dB) → comes from areal ratio (~17) × ossicular lever (~1.3).
- **Acoustic Impedance Mismatch:** Without middle-ear transformer, ~99.9% of acoustic energy would be reflected at the air-fluid boundary (~30 dB loss).
- **Cochlea — Tonotopic Organisation:** Base = high frequencies; Apex = low frequencies. Travelling wave theory (Békésy, 1961).
- **Hair Cells:** Inner (sensory transduction, ~3,500) and Outer (cochlear amplifier, ~12,000). Damage to OHCs causes sensorineural hearing loss.
- **Threshold of Hearing:** $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ at 1 kHz → defines dB SPL.
- **Threshold of Pain:** ~120 dB SPL.
- **Frequency Range:** 20 Hz – 20 kHz; sensitivity peaks 2–5 kHz.
- **A-Weighting:** Approximates the inverse of the 40-phon equal-loudness contour; standard for industrial noise.
- **Equal-Loudness Contours (ISO 226):** Phons (loudness level) ↔ Sones (loudness). 1 sone = 40 phons; doubling sone = +10 phons.
- **Critical Bands:** Bark scale; ear integrates energy within ~1 Bark (~100 Hz at low f, ~4 kHz at high f).
- **OSHA Exposure:** $T = 8/2^{(L-90)/5}$ hours.
- **Logarithmic Summation:** Two identical, incoherent sources → +3 dB (never 6 dB).
- **Industrial Relevance:** A-weighting, hearing-conservation programmes, HPD selection, occupational exposure limits all derive directly from the hearing mechanism.

> **End of Module 2 — Hearing Mechanism Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relation Between Subjective and Objective Sounds
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT632 - INDUSTRIAL NOISE AND VIBRATION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relation Between Subjective and Objective Sounds

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Objective Sound (The Physical Reality)

**Objective sound** refers to the *physically measurable* properties of a sound wave that exist independent of any human listener. These are properties of the *acoustic signal* itself and can be captured, recorded, and quantified using instruments like sound level meters, microphones, and spectrum analyzers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> Objective sound is the physically measurable attribute of acoustic energy propagating through a medium, characterized by parameters such as **Sound Pressure (p)**, **Sound Intensity (I)**, **Sound Power (W)**, **Frequency (f)**, and **Wavelength (λ)**. All objective quantities are expressed in absolute physical units (Pa, W/m², W, Hz, m).

The decibel scale is a **logarithmic, ratio-based, objective metric** built on a reference physical quantity. The most commonly used objective measure is the **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)**:

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_{ref}}\right) \;\; \text{dB}$$

where $p_{ref} = 20 \,\mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa (the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz).

### 1.2 Subjective Sound (The Perceived Experience)

**Subjective sound** is the *psychophysical response* of the human auditory system to the objective acoustic stimulus. It cannot be measured with a microphone — it lives inside the listener's brain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> Subjective sound refers to the perceptual attributes of an acoustic stimulus as interpreted by the human auditory system. The four primary subjective attributes are **Loudness**, **Pitch**, **Timbre**, and **Duration**, quantified by psychoacoustic scales (sone, phon, mel, bark).

### 1.3 The Bridge: Why the Relation Matters

An industrial noise control engineer cannot just say *"the machine is 95 dB"* and call it solved. A 95 dB tone at 1 kHz feels vastly different from a 95 dB tone at 50 Hz, because **the human ear is not a flat-frequency-response microphone**. The ear is most sensitive around **2–5 kHz** and dramatically less sensitive at low frequencies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
> Think of objective sound as the *electricity flowing into a lightbulb* (watts, voltage — purely physical), and subjective sound as the *brightness you perceive* (lumens felt by your eye, dependent on your eye's sensitivity curve). Two bulbs consuming the same electrical wattage can feel differently bright if one is green and one is red, because your eye is more sensitive to green. Similarly, two sound sources of the *same* dB SPL can feel differently loud if their frequencies differ, because the ear's "sensitivity curve" peaks in the mid-frequencies.
> The "Relation Between Subjective and Objective Sounds" is essentially the **calibration curve of the human ear** — the lookup table that translates physical pressure waves into perceived loudness.

### 1.4 The Fundamental Mismatch

| Aspect | Objective (Physical) | Subjective (Perceptual) |
|---|---|---|
| Measurable with instrument? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No — requires human panels |
| Scale type | Logarithmic (dB) — ratio of powers/pressures | Psychophysical (sone, phon) |
| Frequency dependence | None (raw dB treats all $f$ equally) | Strong (ear sensitivity varies with $f$) |
| Reference | $p_{ref} = 20\,\mu$Pa | Threshold of hearing at 1 kHz |
| Variable | Sound Pressure / Power / Intensity | Loudness, Pitch, Timbre |

This mismatch is the entire reason **A-weighting, equal-loudness contours, the phon scale, and the sone scale** were invented.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Equal-Loudness Contours (Fletcher–Munson Curves)

The single most important bridge between the objective and subjective worlds is the family of **Equal-Loudness Contours**, originally measured by **Harvey Fletcher and W. A. Munson (1933)** at Bell Labs and later refined as the **ISO 226:2003** standard.

**Operational logic:**

1. A pure tone is played at frequency $f$ and its SPL is adjusted until an average listener judges it **equally loud** to a 1 kHz reference tone.
2. This SPL value (in dB) is the **Loudness Level** in **phons** of the contour.
3. The process is repeated at every frequency, producing one contour per phon level.
4. The lowest contour that defines the absolute threshold of human hearing is the **MAF (Minimum Audible Field) curve**, conventionally set at **3.9 phons**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Phon:**
> The phon is the *unit of loudness level*. A sound has a loudness level of $N$ phons if it is judged by a normal listener to be as loud as a 1 kHz pure tone of SPL $N$ dB. By this definition, **at 1 kHz, phons = dB SPL** (numerically equal).

**Key engineering observations from the contours:**

- The contours are nearly flat (close to 0 dB SPL) only in the **1–5 kHz region**, where the ear is most sensitive.
- Below ~500 Hz, the contours rise sharply. A 20 Hz tone must reach **~95 dB SPL** to sound as loud as a 1 kHz tone at 40 dB SPL.
- Above ~5 kHz, sensitivity also drops, though less severely than at low frequencies.
- High-level contours (≥ 80 phons) are *flatter* than low-level ones — the ear's frequency response becomes more uniform at high SPLs (this is why bass frequencies become more audible at concert levels).

### 2.2 The Sone Scale (Stevens' Power Law — 1936)

While phons quantify *loudness level* (a logarithmic quantity), the **sone** quantifies *loudness* itself on an approximately **linear, perceived-magnitude scale**.

**Operational logic of the sone scale:**

1. Define a reference: **1 sone = loudness of a 1 kHz tone at 40 dB SPL** (i.e., 40 phons).
2. A sound judged **twice as loud** as the reference is defined as **2 sones**.
3. A sound judged **half as loud** as the reference is defined as **0.5 sones**.

This makes the sone a *ratio scale*, which is the mathematically correct form for perceived magnitude (per Stevens' Power Law).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a new scale?**
> Phons are logarithmic by construction (they are dB-equivalent at 1 kHz). But human loudness perception is approximately *linear* in magnitude: doubling the perceived loudness corresponds to roughly **a 10 dB increase** in SPL (or 10 phons). The sone scale linearizes this.

### 2.3 A-Weighting — The Engineering Approximation

Because exact equal-loudness contours are inconvenient for daily instrumentation, the standardized **A-weighting filter** approximates the **40-phon equal-loudness contour inverted**. Sound level meters apply this filter before summing the energy, producing readings in **dB(A)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **dB(A) is the legal/regulatory standard worldwide** for occupational noise (OSHA, ISO 1999, Indian Factories Act). For industrial noise control, you will almost always report and analyze noise in **dB(A)**, not raw dB SPL.

A-weighting correction values at standard frequencies:

| Frequency (Hz) | A-weighting $C_A$ (dB) |
|---|---|
| 31.5 | −39.4 |
| 63 | −26.2 |
| 125 | −16.1 |
| 250 | −8.6 |
| 500 | −3.2 |
| 1000 | 0.0 (reference) |
| 2000 | +1.2 |
| 4000 | +1.0 |
| 8000 | −1.1 |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p$ | $20\log_{10}(p_{rms}/p_{ref})$ | dB (re 20 μPa) | Objective |
| 2 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I$ | $10\log_{10}(I/I_{ref})$ | dB (re $10^{-12}$ W/m²) | Objective |
| 3 | Sound Power Level | $L_W$ | $10\log_{10}(W/W_{ref})$ | dB (re $10^{-12}$ W) | Objective |
| 4 | Loudness Level | $L_N$ | By definition of equal-loudness contour | **phon** | Subjective (log) |
| 5 | Loudness | $S$ | $2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$ | **sone** | Subjective (linear) |
| 6 | Inverse: Phon from Sone | $L_N$ | $40 + 10\log_2(S)$ | phon | Subjective |
| 7 | A-weighted SPL | $L_A$ | $L_p + C_A(f)$ | dB(A) | Approx. equal-loudness contour |
| 8 | Bandwidth correction | $\Delta L$ | $10\log_{10}(\Delta f)$ | dB | Perceptual band integration |
| 9 | Total loudness (approx.) | $S_{tot}$ | $S_{max} + 0.3 \cdot \Sigma S_i$ | sone | Mark VII simplified |
| 10 | Critical-band rate | $z$ | $z = 13 \arctan(0.76 f) + 3.5 \arctan(f/7.5)$ | **Bark** | Zwicker scale |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall — Phons vs Sones are NOT the same scale!** Students frequently confuse them. Phons are *logarithmic* (like dB); sones are *linear* (like a ruler). The factor of 2 in Stevens' law is a *factor of 2 in perceived loudness*, not 2 dB.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Noise Control Engineering

- **Hearing conservation programs** (Factories Act, OSHA) use **dB(A)** because it correlates well with the *risk of noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL)*.
- **Community noise regulations** use equivalent continuous level $L_{eq}$ and day-night level $L_{dn}$, both in dB(A).
- **Product noise quality** (HVAC, automobiles, household appliances) uses **sone** ratings because the sone scale matches consumer "loudness" complaints.
- **Speech intelligibility** uses the **Articulation Index (AI)** and **Speech Transmission Index (STI)**, which require knowledge of octave-band SPLs, which are then psycho-acoustically weighted.
- **Architectural acoustics** designs concert halls using equal-loudness contour data to ensure bass is heard clearly at low listening levels.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Phon → Sone Conversion (Stevens' Power Law)

**Starting premise:** S. S. Stevens (1936) proposed that perceived loudness $S$ (in sones) is a power-law function of sound intensity $I$:

$$S = k \cdot I^{n}$$

He empirically determined $n \approx 0.3$ for broadband noise, and after calibration to the reference (1 sone ≡ 40 phons ≡ 1 kHz tone at 40 dB SPL, $I_{ref}$):

**Step 1 — Convert intensity to dB (logarithmic form):**

Take $10\log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$10\log_{10} S = 10\log_{10} k + 10n \cdot \log_{10} I$$

**Step 2 — Identify the phon scale:** The loudness level in phons is defined as

$$L_N = 10\log_{10}(I/I_{ref})$$

so that $L_N = 40$ phons when $I = I_{ref}$ (the 1 sone reference). Also, $L_N$ (in phons) is numerically equal to SPL (in dB) **at 1 kHz**, but in general is read from the equal-loudness contour at frequency $f$.

**Step 3 — Solve for the constant $k$:** At the reference, $S = 1$ sone, $I = I_{ref}$, so $1 = k \cdot I_{ref}^{n}$, giving $k = I_{ref}^{-n}$.

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$S = \left(\frac{I}{I_{ref}}\right)^{n} = 10^{n \cdot (L_N/10)}$$

**Step 5 — Calibrate to "doubling of loudness ≡ +10 phons":** A sound that sounds *twice as loud* should have $S = 2$ sones when $L_N = 50$ phons. This requires

$$2 = 10^{n \cdot (50-40)/10} = 10^{n}$$

which gives $n = \log_{10} 2 = 0.30103 \approx 0.3$. ✓

**Step 6 — General formula:** Therefore the canonical sone-phon relationship is

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 2^{(L_N - 40)/10} \\
L_N &= 40 + 10\log_2 S
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Worked numerical example:** A sound has loudness level 70 phons. Find its loudness in sones.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 2^{(70 - 40)/10} \\
&= 2^{30/10} \\
&= 2^{3} \\
&= 8 \text{ sones}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 70-phon sound is perceived as 8 times as loud as the 40-phon reference. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation of the A-Weighting Approximation (40-phon Contour)

**Starting premise:** The 40-phon equal-loudness contour is the *inverse sensitivity curve* of the average human ear at low-to-moderate SPLs. To approximate this with a single filter, the standard A-weighting is defined analytically by:

$$C_A(f) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R_A(f)}{R_A(1000)}\right) \;\; \text{dB}$$

where the weighting transfer function $R_A(f)$ is built from three pole-zero filter stages (the exact polynomial is given in IEC 61672-1). The key stages are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_A(f) &= \frac{(2\pi \cdot 12194)^2 \cdot (4\pi^2 f^2)}{(2\pi f)^2 + (2\pi \cdot 20.6)^2} \\
&\quad \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\pi f)^2 + (2\pi \cdot 12194)^2}} \\
&\quad \cdot \frac{(2\pi \cdot 107.7)\cdot(2\pi \cdot 737.9)}{\sqrt{((2\pi f)^2 + (2\pi \cdot 107.7)^2)\cdot((2\pi f)^2 + (2\pi \cdot 737.9)^2)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **engineering shortcut** is to look up $C_A(f)$ from a table and apply:

$$L_A = L_p + C_A(f) \quad \text{[dB(A)]}$$

### 3.3 Total Loudness from Multiple Sources (Mark VII Approximation)

When $N$ uncorrelated sound sources are present, total loudness is *not* obtained by simple addition of sones, because perception of "doubling" is non-linear. Zwicker's simplified method (ISO 532B):

$$
S_{tot} = S_{max} + 0.3 \cdot \sum_{i \neq max} S_i
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
- The dominant source $S_{max}$ contributes fully.
- Each additional source contributes only ~30% of its individual sone value, because the ear is partially *masked* by the dominant source.
- A summation over octave bands using Bark-scale critical bands is also required for full implementation.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A pure tone at 1 kHz has SPL of 60 dB. A second identical tone at 100 Hz also has SPL 60 dB. Using the equal-loudness contour, the 100 Hz tone has loudness level ~50 phons. Find the loudness of each in sones and the total.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{1kHz} &= 2^{(60-40)/10} = 2^{2} = 4 \text{ sones} \\
S_{100Hz} &= 2^{(50-40)/10} = 2^{1} = 2 \text{ sones} \\
S_{tot} &= 4 + 0.3 \times 2 = 4.6 \text{ sones}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice: even though both tones have the *same* physical SPL of 60 dB, the 1 kHz tone is perceived as **twice as loud** as the 100 Hz tone (4 sones vs 2 sones). This single example demonstrates exactly *why* the subjective–objective relation matters in industrial noise control — a low-frequency roar and a mid-frequency hiss of identical dB SPL are not at all equivalent to the worker.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Subjective–Objective Bridge

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how a physical acoustic signal flows through measurement, weighting, and psychoacoustic conversion to reach a perceived loudness verdict in the human brain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Sound Source Machine] --> obj1[Sound Pressure p Pa]
    srcA --> obj2[Sound Power W W]
    srcA --> obj3[Sound Intensity I W/m2]
    obj1 --> splCalc[SPL Calculation]
    splCalc --> splOut[Objective dB SPL]
    obj2 --> pwlOut[Objective dB Power]
    obj3 --> silOut[Objective dB Intensity]
    splOut --> awt[A Weighting Filter 40 Phon Contour]
    awt --> dbaOut[Weighted dB A]
    splOut --> cont[Equal Loudness Contour ISO 226]
    cont --> phonOut[Loudness Level in Phons]
    phonOut --> sonConv[Stevens Power Law S 2 L40 10]
    sonConv --> sonOut[Perceived Loudness in Sones]
    dbaOut --> reg[Regulatory Compliance OSHA ISO]
    sonOut --> perc[Human Perception Rating]
    reg --> health[NIHL Risk Assessment]
    perc --> health
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Mapping of the Four Psychoacoustic Scales

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph objBlock[Objective Domain Instrument World]
        p[Sound Pressure Pa] --> lp[Sound Pressure Level dB SPL]
        w[Sound Power W] --> lw[Sound Power Level dB PWL]
        i[Sound Intensity W per m2] --> li[Sound Intensity Level dB SIL]
    end
    subgraph subBlock[Subjective Domain Human Ear and Brain]
        f[Frequency Hz] --> pch[Pitch Mel Scale]
        amp[Loudness Level Phon] --> son[Loudness Sone]
        crit[Critical Bands] --> bark[Bark Scale]
        dur[Duration ms] --> loudTime[Temporal Loudness Integration]
    end
    subgraph bridge[Bridge Conversion Layer]
        iso[ISO 226 Equal Loudness Contours] --> phonL[Phon from dB and f]
        aWt[A Weighting IEC 61672] --> dbaL[dB A from dB SPL and f]
        st[Stevens Power Law n 0.3] --> sonL[Sone from Phon]
        zw[Zwicker Critical Rate] --> barkL[Bark from Hz]
    end
    lp --> iso
    lp --> aWt
    f --> iso
    f --> aWt
    iso --> phonL
    aWt --> dbaL
    phonL --> st
    st --> sonL
    f --> zw
    zw --> barkL
    lp --> pch
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Selecting the Correct Loudness Metric

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Industrial Noise Problem] --> q1{Is the goal legal compliance or hearing conservation?}
    q1 -- Yes --> reg[Use dB A and Leq dB A]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Is the goal product noise quality or consumer complaint rating?}
    q2 -- Yes --> son[Use Sone Rating]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Is the goal architectural or speech communication design?}
    q3 -- Yes --> iso[Use Phon Contour Data and STI]
    q3 -- No --> gen[Report All Four SPL dB A Phon Sone]
    reg --> out1[Compliance Decision]
    son --> out2[Product Loudness Rating]
    iso --> out3[Acoustic Design Spec]
    gen --> out4[Full Psychoacoustic Report]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide for the diagrams:** Read the first diagram top-to-bottom: the machine emits an objective wave, which is measured, then A-weighted, then converted through contours to a phon level, then to a sone level, finally reaching a human listener. The second diagram is a *two-domain view* showing what each side computes and where the conversion bridges connect them.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2 Marks Conceptual Set]**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*Define the following terms: (a) Phon, (b) Sone, (c) dB(A).*

**Model Answer:**

- **(a) Phon:** The unit of *loudness level*. A sound has a loudness level of $N$ phons if a normal listener judges it as loud as a 1 kHz pure tone at SPL of $N$ dB. At 1 kHz, phon = dB SPL numerically.
- **(b) Sone:** The unit of *loudness* (perceived magnitude). 1 sone = loudness of a 1 kHz tone at 40 dB SPL. A sound of $S$ sones is perceived as $S$ times louder than the reference. The sone scale is approximately linear in perceived magnitude.
- **(c) dB(A):** A-weighted sound pressure level. The SPL is passed through an A-weighting filter that approximates the 40-phon equal-loudness contour (inverted). It is the standard metric for occupational and environmental noise regulations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
*State Stevens' Power Law relating loudness in sones to loudness level in phons. A machine produces a sound of 70 phons. Calculate its loudness in sones.*

**Model Answer:**

Stevens' Power Law (sone-phon relation):

$$S = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$$

For $L_N = 70$ phons:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 2^{(70-40)/10} \\
&= 2^{3} \\
&= 8 \text{ sones}
\end{aligned}
$$

A 70-phon sound is perceived as **8 times as loud** as the 1-sone reference (40-phon, 1 kHz tone).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE Module 2 — Typical 14-Mark Pattern with Internal Choice]**

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** *With the help of a neat sketch, explain the equal-loudness contours (Fletcher–Munson curves). Discuss how these contours are used to define the phon scale and the A-weighting network.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the relationship between subjective and objective measures of sound. A sound has a measured SPL of 80 dB at 1 kHz. A second sound of the same SPL of 80 dB is at 50 Hz. Using equal-loudness contours, the 50 Hz tone has a loudness level of approximately 65 phons. Compute the loudness in sones of each tone and the total loudness using Zwicker's approximation.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition and origin (2 marks):** Equal-loudness contours are curves that connect (frequency, SPL) points judged equally loud by an average human listener. They were first measured by Fletcher and Munson (1933) and standardized as ISO 226:2003.

2. **Construction (2 marks):** A reference 1 kHz pure tone is presented at a fixed SPL. At each other test frequency, the SPL is adjusted until the listener reports equal loudness. Repeating this for SPLs from 0 to 120 dB yields a family of curves — each one a *loudness level* in phons.

3. **Phon definition and A-weighting (3 marks):** The number attached to each contour is the phon value of the contour. The 40-phon contour (MAF-threshold + 40) is the *inverse sensitivity curve* of the ear at conversational levels. A-weighting is an electrical filter network that approximates this inverted 40-phon contour, producing dB(A) readings on a sound level meter. The ear is most sensitive at 1–5 kHz and much less sensitive below 200 Hz; the contours rise sharply at low frequencies, which is why A-weighting *attenuates* low frequencies by up to 40 dB at 31.5 Hz.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

For the 1 kHz tone, $L_p = 80$ dB, so $L_N = 80$ phons (at 1 kHz, phon = dB SPL).

For the 50 Hz tone, $L_p = 80$ dB, but $L_N = 65$ phons (read from equal-loudness contour).

Loudness in sones (Stevens' law):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{1kHz} &= 2^{(80-40)/10} = 2^{4} = 16 \text{ sones} \\
S_{50Hz} &= 2^{(65-40)/10} = 2^{2.5} = 5.66 \text{ sones}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total loudness (Zwicker approximation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{tot} &= S_{max} + 0.3 \cdot S_{other} \\
&= 16 + 0.3 \times 5.66 \\
&= 16 + 1.70 \\
&= 17.70 \text{ sones}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The 1 kHz tone is perceived as ~3 times louder than the 50 Hz tone of identical dB SPL, demonstrating the critical role of equal-loudness contours in noise assessment.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** *Define objective and subjective sound. List the four principal subjective attributes of sound. Explain with a suitable example why a 90 dB SPL tone at 1 kHz and a 90 dB SPL tone at 50 Hz are perceived as having different loudness.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Derive Stevens' Power Law relating sone and phon, and explain its significance in industrial noise control. A factory floor has three machines with loudness levels of 60, 65, and 70 phons. Calculate the total perceived loudness in sones.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Objective sound (2 marks):** Measurable, instrument-based properties: SPL, SIL, PWL, frequency. Independent of listener.
2. **Subjective sound (2 marks):** Listener-dependent perceptual attributes: **Loudness, Pitch, Timbre, Duration**. Quantified by sone, mel, bark, ms.
3. **Example (3 marks):** Both 90 dB SPL tones have the *same* objective physical energy. However, the equal-loudness contour at 50 Hz shows that to be perceived as 90 phons (= 90 dB SPL at 1 kHz equivalent), a 50 Hz tone must be raised to ~110 dB SPL. Therefore, a 90 dB SPL, 50 Hz tone has a loudness level of only about **75 phons**, corresponding to a sone value of $2^{(75-40)/10} = 2^{3.5} \approx 11.3$ sones, whereas the 1 kHz, 90 dB SPL tone has $2^{(90-40)/10} = 2^5 = 32$ sones. The 1 kHz tone is heard as nearly **3× louder**, even though both are "90 dB" on an unweighted meter.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation (4 marks):** See the full derivation in Section 3.1 above. Summary:

- Stevens proposed $S = k I^n$ with $n = 0.3$.
- Calibrated at $S = 1$ sone when $L_N = 40$ phons.
- Gives $S = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$ and $L_N = 40 + 10\log_2 S$.

**Significance (1 mark):** Provides a *linear* scale of perceived loudness, essential for noise quality ratings, product labelling (HVAC sones, refrigerator sones), and accurate prediction of community/worker annoyance.

**Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= 2^{(60-40)/10} = 2^2 = 4 \text{ sones} \\
S_2 &= 2^{(65-40)/10} = 2^{2.5} = 5.66 \text{ sones} \\
S_3 &= 2^{(70-40)/10} = 2^3 = 8 \text{ sones} \\
S_{max} &= 8 \text{ sones} \\
S_{tot} &= 8 + 0.3 \times (4 + 5.66) \\
&= 8 + 0.3 \times 9.66 \\
&= 8 + 2.90 \\
&= 10.90 \text{ sones}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (lose 1–3 marks each):**
>
> 1. **Confusing phons and sones.** Phons are *logarithmic* (level), sones are *linear* (magnitude). Writing $S = L_N \times 0.3$ is **wrong** — it loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting that at 1 kHz, phon = dB SPL.** Many students apply equal-loudness correction even at 1 kHz. Don't. By definition, the 1 kHz contour is the reference and equals SPL exactly.
> 3. **Adding sones directly.** Loudness of multiple sources is *not* additive. Use the Zwicker approximation $S_{tot} = S_{max} + 0.3 \Sigma S_i$, or one of the standardized methods (ISO 532B, Mark VI/VII).
> 4. **Reporting raw dB for occupational noise.** Industrial hygiene and KTU lab reports demand **dB(A)**. Reporting unweighted dB will cost 1 mark.
> 5. **Skipping the units** in the final answer. Always write *sone* and *phon* explicitly.
> 6. **Mis-stating the reference for sone.** It is **40 phons, NOT 0 phons**. The 1-sone reference is a 1 kHz tone at 40 dB SPL.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Objective sound** is physical and measurable (dB SPL, dB PWL, dB SIL).
- ✅ **Subjective sound** is perceptual and requires human judgment (sone, phon, mel, bark).
- ✅ **1 sone ≡ 40 phons ≡ 1 kHz tone at 40 dB SPL.**
- ✅ **Stevens' Power Law:** $S = 2^{(L_N - 40)/10}$ sones.
- ✅ **Inverse:** $L_N = 40 + 10\log_2 S$ phons.
- ✅ **A-weighting** approximates the 40-phon equal-loudness contour (inverted).
- ✅ **dB(A) is the regulatory standard** for occupational and environmental noise.
- ✅ **Fletcher–Munson (ISO 226:2003) curves** are the *primary bridge* between objective and subjective sound.
- ✅ **Critical bands (Bark scale)** are needed for accurate loudness summation of broadband/noise spectra.
- ✅ **Loudness summation** uses Zwicker's approximation: $S_{tot} = S_{max} + 0.3 \Sigma S_i$.
- ✅ A 10 dB rise in SPL (or 10 phon) ≈ **doubling of perceived loudness**.
- ✅ A 3 dB rise in SPL ≈ **doubling of acoustic power** (but only ~23% increase in perceived loudness).
- ✅ Always convert frequency-dependent data through A-weighting or equal-loudness contours *before* comparing industrial noise to regulations.
- ✅ For KTU exams: show all derivation steps, label units explicitly, and state the reference conditions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auditory Effects of Noise and Audiometric Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT632 - INDUSTRIAL NOISE AND VIBRATION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Auditory Effects of Noise and Audiometric Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition: Auditory Effects of Noise

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Auditory Effects of Noise** refer to the physiological, psychological, and pathological changes that occur in the human auditory system (outer, middle, and inner ear; auditory nerve; and central auditory pathways) as a direct consequence of exposure to sound — particularly occupational, industrial, or environmental noise of sufficient intensity (≥ **85 dBA** for prolonged periods) and duration.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terms, this encompasses the entire spectrum of changes ranging from **subtle biochemical fatigue of hair cells in the Organ of Corti** to **irreversible sensory-neural destruction** leading to clinical hearing impairment. The effects are classified primarily into:

1. **Temporary Threshold Shift (TTS)** — reversible
2. **Permanent Threshold Shift (PTS)** — irreversible
3. **Acoustic Trauma** — sudden, acute damage
4. **Tinnitus** — phantom ringing sensation
5. **Recruitment** — abnormal loudness perception

## 1.2 Definition: Audiometric Testing

> [!NOTE]
> **Audiometric Testing** is the standardised, controlled, behavioural and electrophysiological measurement of an individual's hearing sensitivity across a defined frequency spectrum (typically **250 Hz – 8000 Hz**), conducted using a calibrated audiometer in an acoustically treated booth, to quantitatively diagnose, monitor, and document auditory dysfunction attributable to noise exposure.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive View

Imagine the **cochlea** (the spiral cavity of the inner ear) as a **piano keyboard** with ~16,000 tiny keys (hair cells), each tuned to a specific frequency.

- When you whisper (≈ 30 dB), only a few keys near the middle of the keyboard tremble gently.
