# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Transmission Loss (TL) and Absorption Coefficient (α). State the mass law for a single partition.**

**Model Answer:**

**Transmission Loss:** It is the ratio (in dB) of incident to transmitted sound power through a partition:

$$TL = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{W_i}{W_t}\right) \text{ dB}$$

**Absorption Coefficient (α):** Ratio of sound energy absorbed by a surface to the sound energy incident on it; range $0 \le \alpha \le 1$.

**Mass Law:** For a single, limp, infinite partition at frequencies above coincidence,

$$TL = 20 \log_{10}(mf) - 47 \text{ dB}$$

Doubling mass or frequency increases TL by 6 dB. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Reverberation Time. Write Sabine's formula and explain each term.**

**Model Answer:**

**Reverberation Time (T₆₀):** Time (seconds) for the sound pressure level in a room to decay by 60 dB after the source is switched off.

**Sabine's Formula:**

$$T_{60} = \frac{0.161 V}{A} \text{ seconds}$$

Where:
- $V$ = room volume (m³)
- $A$ = total absorption = $S\bar\alpha$ (sabin)
- $S$ = total interior surface area (m²)
- $\bar\alpha$ = average absorption coefficient

The constant 0.161 comes from $24 \ln 10 / c$ with $c = 343$ m/s. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the construction and working of **close-fit and loosely covered enclosures** used for industrial noise control. Compare their typical insertion losses and applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **25 mm plywood** partition has surface density $m = 15$ kg/m². Calculate its Transmission Loss at (i) 500 Hz and (ii) 1000 Hz using mass law. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction & Working:**

**Close-Fit Enclosure:**
- Tightly wraps the noisy equipment with minimum clearance (< 0.5 m).
- Built from **2 mm MS sheet** + inner lining of **50 mm mineral wool** ($\alpha \approx 0.85$).
- Sealed using neoprene gaskets at all openings (cable entry, viewing window).
- Works on **mass + absorption + sealing** principle.

**Loosely Covered Enclosure:**
- Partial hood over machine leaving one or more openings for ventilation/access.
- Built from **12 mm plywood** with **25 mm acoustic foam** lining.
- Openings treated with **splitter silencers** or **acoustic louvres**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Close-Fit | Loosely Covered |
|---|---|---|
| Insertion loss | 30–50 dB(A) | 10–25 dB(A) |
| Cost | High | Moderate |
| Maintenance access | Difficult | Easy |
| Ventilation | Requires silencers | Natural |

**(b) Numerical:**

Using $TL = 20\log_{10}(mf) - 47$ with $m = 15$ kg/m².

**(i) At f = 500 Hz:**

$$mf = 15 \times 500 = 7500$$

$$TL_{500} = 20 \log_{10}(7500) - 47 = 20 \times 3.875 - 47 = 77.5 - 47 = 30.5 \text{ dB}$$

**(ii) At f = 1000 Hz:**

$$mf = 15 \times 1000 = 15000$$

$$TL_{1000} = 20 \log_{10}(15000) - 47 = 20 \times 4.176 - 47 = 83.5 - 47 = 36.5 \text{ dB}$$

**Comment:** TL increased by **6 dB** when frequency doubled from 500 Hz to 1000 Hz, validating the mass law doubling rule. **(7 marks)**

---

### Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]
**(a)** Define **Reverberation Time (T₆₀)** and **Room Constant (R)**. Derive **Sabine's formula** for T₆₀ with all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory hall has volume $V = 2000$ m³ and total surface $S = 900$ m² with average absorption $\bar\alpha = 0.10$. A new machine with sound power level $L_W = 95$ dB re 1 pW is installed at the centre ($Q = 2$). Calculate the steady-state SPL at (i) 5 m from source, (ii) 25 m from source. Take reference $p_0 = 20 \,\mu Pa$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Definitions & Sabine's Derivation:**

**Definitions:**

- **T₆₀:** Time for SPL to decay 60 dB after source stops.
- **R:** Acoustic parameter describing room liveness: $R = S\bar\alpha/(1-\bar\alpha)$.

**Derivation:**
Energy decay ODE: $\frac{dE}{dt} = -\beta E \implies E = E_0 e^{-\beta t}$.

Decay constant $\beta = cA/(4V)$ from mean free path $\ell_m = 4V/S$ and number of reflections/sec = $cS/(4V)$.

Setting $E(T_{60})/E_0 = 10^{-6}$:

$$T_{60} = \frac{6 \ln 10}{\beta} = \frac{24 V \ln 10}{c A}$$

Substituting $c = 343$ m/s:

$$T_{60} = \frac{24 \times 2.3026}{343} \cdot \frac{V}{A} = 0.161 \frac{V}{A}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Diffuse sound field (uniform energy density).
2. Energy density uniform throughout.
3. Sound pressure decays exponentially.
4. Mean free path = $4V/S$.

**(b) Numerical:**

**Given:** $V = 2000$ m³, $S = 900$ m², $\bar\alpha = 0.10$, $L_W = 95$ dB, $Q = 2$.

**Step 1: Compute room constant:**

$$R = \frac{S\bar\alpha}{1-\bar\alpha} = \frac{900 \times 0.10}{0.90} = 100 \text{ m}^2 \text{ sabin}$$

**Step 2: Use diffuse field equation:**

$$L_p = L_W + 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{Q}{4\pi r^2} + \frac{4}{R}\right)$$

**(i) At r = 5 m:**

$$\frac{Q}{4\pi r^2} = \frac{2}{4\pi (25)} = \frac{2}{314.159} = 0.00637$$

$$\frac{4}{R} = \frac{4}{100} = 0.04$$

$$\text{Sum} = 0.00637 + 0.04 = 0.04637$$

$$10\log_{10}(0.04637) = -13.34 \text{ dB}$$

$$L_p(5\text{m}) = 95 - 13.34 = 81.66 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**(ii) At r = 25 m:**

$$\frac{Q}{4\pi r^2} = \frac{2}{4\pi (625)} = \frac{2}{7853.98} = 0.000255$$

$$\text{Sum} = 0.000255 + 0.04 = 0.040255$$

$$10\log_{10}(0.040255) = -13.95 \text{ dB}$$

$$L_p(25\text{m}) = 95 - 13.95 = 81.05 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**Interpretation:** Beyond $r = $ critical distance $r_c = \sqrt{RQ/(16\pi)} \approx \sqrt{200/50.27} \approx 1.99$ m, SPL is dominated by reverberant field and becomes nearly constant with distance — confirming **reverberant field behaviour**. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Module 4 (KTU 2024):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing TL with Insertion Loss (IL).** TL is a *material property*; IL is the *measured drop* in SPL after installation, including flanking paths.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting units in Sabine's formula.** Use **V in m³**, **A in sabin**; otherwise 0.161 constant is wrong (0.16 in cm-based, 0.161 in SI).
> 
> 3. **Applying mass law below coincidence frequency.** Mass law is valid *only* above the **critical frequency** $f_c = c^2/(2\pi) \cdot \sqrt{m/D}$ where $D$ is bending stiffness.
> 
> 4. **Treating $R$ as just total absorption $A$.** Room constant is $A/(1-\bar\alpha)$, not just $A$. The difference matters for $\bar\alpha > 0.2$.
> 
> 5. **Ear protection overprotection:** Using muffs + plugs in a 90 dB(A) area causes *overprotection* (loss of warning signals) — examiners deduct for this.
> 
> 6. **Active Noise Control misconceptions:** ANC works only at **low frequencies** (< 500–1000 Hz). Citing ANC for broadband industrial noise is a common mistake.
> 
> 7. **Sketch missing in 7-mark question on enclosures.** Always include a labelled cross-section of the enclosure showing wall, lining, machine, and ventilation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🎯 Quick-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Hierarchy**: Source → Path → Receiver (priority order).
- ✅ **Close-fit enclosure** → 30–50 dB(A); requires sealing + lining.
- ✅ **Loosely covered** → 10–25 dB(A); allows ventilation.
- ✅ **TL formula** = $10 \log_{10}(1/\tau)$.
- ✅ **Mass Law** = $20\log_{10}(mf) - 47$; doubles mass = +6 dB TL.
- ✅ **Sabine T₆₀** = $0.161 V / A$; valid for $\bar\alpha < 0.2$.
- ✅ **Eyring T₆₀** = $0.161 V / [-S \ln(1-\bar\alpha)]$; for highly absorptive rooms.
- ✅ **Room Constant** $R = S\bar\alpha/(1-\bar\alpha)$.
- ✅ **Steady-state SPL** uses $L_p = L_W + 10\log_{10}(Q/4\pi r^2 + 4/R)$.
- ✅ **NRR field correction**: subtract NRR, **add 7 dB** (OSHA method).
- ✅ **Ear plugs/muffs**: required above 85 dB(A); dual protection > 100 dB(A).
- ✅ **ANC** limited to < 1500 Hz (geometric constraints); uses FxLMS algorithm.
- ✅ **Auditorium target T₆₀**: 1.0–1.5 s (speech), 1.5–2.0 s (music).
- ✅ **Industrial hall target T₆₀**: < 1.5 s for speech intelligibility, often < 1.0 s for safety announcements.
- ✅ **Material types**: Mineral wool ($\alpha = 0.85$–$0.95$), acoustic foam ($\alpha = 0.60$–$0.80$), perforated panels ($\alpha = 0.40$–$0.70$), glass fibre ($\alpha = 0.75$–$0.90$).
- ✅ **Double-wall advantage**: air gap + mass-spring-mass resonance gives **extra 10–20 dB** over single wall.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Tip for KTU 2024:** When answering 14-mark questions, always quote **formula → substitution → numerical answer → engineering interpretation**. Examiners at KTU follow this exact 4-step marking scheme for numerical problems.
